content.text:
## 1. Jurisdictional limits — MAXIMUM AMOUNT YOU CAN SUE FOR

**Monetary Cap:** Wyoming “Small Claims Court” (a proceeding in Circuit Court) handles claims for money damages or debts up to **$6,000** (excluding court costs) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,learn%20more%20about%20the%20Circuit)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-201/#:~:text=In%20the%20trial%20of%20civil,subject%20to%20specified%20dollar%20limitations)). This $6,000 limit applies uniformly to all plaintiffs, whether individuals, small businesses, corporations, LLCs, partnerships, or government entities. ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,learn%20more%20about%20the%20Circuit)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)) Unlike some states, Wyoming does **not** set a lower cap for business plaintiffs – any party can sue for up to $6,000 in a small claim. There is also no statutory limit on how many small claims a person or business may file per year (Wyoming law does not impose a yearly filing cap based on claim quantity or amount).

**Split/Excess Claims:** You **cannot split one larger claim into multiple smaller suits** to evade the $6,000 cap. If your claim exceeds $6,000, you must either **reduce** the claim (waive the amount above $6,000) or file in a higher court ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,to%20sue%20in%20another%20Court)). For example, if you are owed $7,500 on a contract, you could choose to sue in small claims for **$6,000** and give up the remaining $1,500 – but you **cannot** file two suits for $6,000 and $1,500; the second would likely be dismissed as an improper splitting of a single claim. Wyoming’s Circuit Courts have general civil jurisdiction up to $50,000, so a claim over $6,000 can be heard there in a regular civil docket if needed ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-128/#:~:text=,00%29%2C%20exclusive%20of%20court%20costs)). In small claims, **$6,000 is an absolute limit** on recovery in one case – the court will not award more than that amount (even if actual damages are higher), and any balance beyond $6,000 is forfeited by choosing the small claims route ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,to%20sue%20in%20another%20Court)).

**Case Example:** If a roofing contractor claims a homeowner owes $8,000, the contractor must either accept $6,000 as the maximum in small claims (foregoing $2,000) or file as a formal civil case. They cannot file two small claims for $4,000 each. Likewise, a victim of a fender-bender who has $7,500 in car repair bills could sue for $6,000 in small claims (e.g. suing for only part of the damages) or sue in regular court for the full $7,500, but cannot recover the full $7,500 in small claims. ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,to%20sue%20in%20another%20Court))

**No Different Caps by Plaintiff Type:** Wyoming does not differentiate the claim limit by the type of plaintiff. Whether you are an individual, a sole proprietor d/b/a a business, or a corporation/LLC, the maximum claim is $6,000 all the same ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,learn%20more%20about%20the%20Circuit)). Government entities (like a city or the state) may also use small claims up to $6,000; in fact, the law explicitly allows “the state [or] any governmental entity” to appear in small claims on its own behalf ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). (For instance, a state agency could sue to recover a fee or a county could sue for property damage, so long as the amount is ≤ $6,000). There is no lower cap for suing a government – but note that **sovereign immunity** might bar certain claims against the state or require special procedures (e.g. tort claims against a government usually must be filed under the Governmental Claims Act in district court). Always verify that the government entity has waived immunity for your type of claim before suing in any court.

**No Annual Filing Limits:** Wyoming law does not impose a limit on the number of small claims cases one can file per year based on the amount (some states limit how many large-dollar small claims a party can file annually, but Wyoming has no such rule). However, filing an excessive volume of cases might draw scrutiny or be limited by general abuse-of-process principles. Businesses that regularly file collection actions can use small claims repeatedly as long as each claim is within $6,000.

**Attorneys’ Fees and Costs Not Counted Toward Cap:** Court costs (filing fees, service fees) are not counted toward the $6,000 cap ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-201/#:~:text=In%20the%20trial%20of%20civil,subject%20to%20specified%20dollar%20limitations)). If you win, the court may award you those costs on top of the $6,000 principal. However, **interest or attorneys’ fees** that you seek are effectively part of your demand – you can’t get $6,000 plus interest beyond the cap unless the interest accrued after judgment. (Pre-judgment interest would be included in the claim; post-judgment interest runs separately by law at 10% per year ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)), see Section 12).

## 2. What types of cases qualify — EVERYTHING YOU CAN SUE FOR

**General Rule:** Wyoming small claims court can hear **any civil dispute for money or the recovery of personal property** within the $6,000 limit, **so long as it is not specifically excluded by law**. The key is that small claims is meant for relatively simple, **money-only cases**. You cannot ask for *non-monetary* relief (like injunctions or orders forcing someone to do something) – only for a **judgment for money** (or return of personal property worth up to $6,000). Below is an exhaustive list of claim types commonly brought in small claims, and whether they are allowed in Wyoming:

### Contract & Money-Owed Disputes

- **Breach of Written Contract:** Yes – if someone breaches a written agreement (e.g. a signed service contract, a loan agreement, a car repair contract), you can sue for the monetary loss up to $6,000. *Examples:* A contractor was paid to remodel a kitchen but never finished the work; a client refuses to pay a freelancer under a signed contract. Written contracts have a long statute of limitations (10 years to file in Wyoming) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)), so even older breaches can be pursued (though collecting on an old judgment is another matter). If the contract’s amount exceeds $6k, you must waive the rest or sue in higher court.
- **Breach of Oral/Handshake Agreement:** Yes – verbal agreements are enforceable, and you can sue for up to $6k on an unwritten contract ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). *Examples:* A friend agreed orally to sell you a used appliance and took your payment but never delivered; a neighbor promised to split the cost of a fence but didn’t pay. Wyoming gives 8 years to sue on oral contracts ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). Tip: Bring any proof of the agreement (texts, emails, witnesses) since it’s not in writing.
- **Unpaid Personal Loans:** Yes – money you lent someone can be recovered. *Example:* You loaned an acquaintance $2,000 via a personal check and they never paid you back. A promissory note (written IOU) is ideal evidence (considered a written contract – 10-year limit) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)), but even without a formal note, you can sue on the implied promise to repay (oral contract – 8-year limit) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). Include any interest that was agreed upon (or statutory interest if applicable).
- **Unpaid Promissory Notes:** Yes – a signed promissory note is a written promise to pay; suing on it is straightforward as long as within 10 years of default ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)). *Example:* A former friend signed a note to repay $5,000 by a certain date and defaulted – you can sue for up to $5,000 plus allowable interest.
- **Unpaid Invoices / Accounts Receivable:** Yes – businesses can use small claims to collect on unpaid bills for goods or services. *Examples:* A small landscaping business has $4,500 in past-due invoices for yard work; a supplier is owed $3,000 on a shipped order that the buyer never paid. Ensure you have documentation (invoices, delivery receipts, etc.). If there was a written contract or purchase order, it’s within 10 years; if it was all informal but implied by conduct, aim for 8 years or less for safety ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)).
- **Unpaid Rent:** Yes – a landlord can sue a tenant (or former tenant) for unpaid rent or property damage, **but not for possession of the rental unit** (evictions are handled separately – see “What you CANNOT sue for” below). Both residential and commercial rent owed can be claimed. If there’s a written lease, it’s a written contract (10-year limit) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)); a month-to-month tenancy without a written lease would be an implied contract (8-year limit) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). *Examples:* A tenant moved out owing two months’ rent ($2,000) – landlord can sue for that plus any proven damages (cleaning, repairs) as a money claim. A commercial tenant defaulted on a short-term lease – the landlord could sue for the remaining rent due under the lease (if within $6k). **Note:** Wyoming law requires landlords to mitigate damages (try to re-rent) in many cases, so landlords should be prepared to show they attempted to re-let the unit or minimize the loss.
- **“Bounced” or Dishonored Checks:** Yes – Wyoming provides a strong remedy for NSF (non-sufficient funds) checks. If someone paid you with a check that bounced, you must first make a **written demand** and give the check writer 30 days to pay the amount plus up to $30 fee ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,provided%20for%20in%20this%20section)). If they don’t pay in 30 days, you can sue for **treble damages** – **3 times the amount of the check**, with a minimum of $100, plus a $30 collection fee and court costs ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,00%29%2C%20and%20court%20costs)). For example, a $500 bad check can result in a $1,500 judgment, and a $50 bad check would yield $150 (since minimum $100 applies). This is authorized by Wyoming’s civil bad check statute ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,00%29%2C%20and%20court%20costs)). These claims fit in small claims as long as the base check amount is $2,000 or less (since trebling it would cap at $6k). Even above that, you could waive the excess treble amount to stay in small claims (e.g. a $3,000 check trebles to $9,000, which is over the limit – you might choose to sue for $6,000 or just actual + fee).
- **Open Accounts/Running Tabs:** Yes – if you had an open account arrangement (e.g. a customer carries a charge account with your small business for services or goods), you can sue for the outstanding balance (up to $6k). Treat it like an unwritten contract unless there’s a signed credit agreement. *Example:* A ranch supply store allowed a local rancher to buy feed on account; the balance due is $1,200 – recoverable in small claims.
- **Co-signer/Guarantor Claims:** Yes – if you co-signed a loan and ended up paying the debt, you can sue the primary borrower for what you had to pay (since by law you stepped into the lender’s shoes after paying). *Example:* You co-signed a friend’s $4,000 car loan; they defaulted and you paid the bank – now you can sue your friend for $4,000. Likewise, if you loaned money to someone and a third party guaranteed it in writing, you could sue that guarantor if the primary doesn’t pay (provided total is ≤ $6k). Always bring documentation of the guaranty or co-sign agreement.

### Consumer & Service Disputes

- **Refund for Defective Product:** Yes – if you bought a product (appliance, electronics, etc.) that turned out to be defective or not as promised, you can sue for a refund or damages up to $6k. *Example:* You paid $1,000 for a used washer advertised as “fully operational” that immediately broke down beyond repair – you could sue the seller for a refund $1,000 plus perhaps any repair costs you incurred. If a warranty was involved, note that under the U.C.C. (Uniform Commercial Code) an action for breach of sale-of-goods contract or warranty must be filed within 4 years of the sale ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/state/wyoming-law/wyoming-civil-statute-of-limitations-laws.html#:~:text=,personal%20property%20damage%2C%20product%20liability)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-34-1/article-2-a/part-5/section-34-1-2-a-506/#:~:text=Wyoming%20Statutes%20%C2%A7%2034.1,An%20action%20for%20default)). Also, if the seller gave an express written warranty, you may also have rights under that (and possibly attorneys’ fees by statute, though usually those cases go to regular court if large).
- **Services Not Performed or Performed Poorly:** Yes – if you paid for a service that was not done at all, or was done in a shoddy or incomplete manner, you can sue for the loss. *Examples:* You hired a painter and paid a $800 deposit, but they never showed up – sue for the $800. Or you paid a contractor $5,000 to repave your driveway and it turned out so substandard that it must be redone – you might sue for the cost to re-do it (up to $6k). Essentially this is breach of contract (written or oral depending on your agreement). Tip: Bring any contract, receipts, and photographs of the poor workmanship if claiming poor quality.
- **Auto Repair Disputes:** Yes – small claims often cover car repair issues. *Examples:* You were overcharged for auto repairs (e.g. the mechanic did unauthorized extra work or charged for parts not actually replaced), or the mechanic’s work was negligent and caused damage. You can sue for the overcharge or the cost to fix the bad repair. If a mechanic gave a written estimate and then exceeded it without permission, that can form the basis of the claim (Wyoming doesn’t have a specific auto repair act with a small-claims remedy, but general contract/misrepresentation law applies).
- **Home Contractor Disputes:** Yes – incomplete or defective home repairs under contract can be resolved in small claims (if within $6k). *Examples:* A handyman was paid $2,500 to install new windows but left the job unfinished or done improperly; you might pay someone else to fix it and then sue the original handyman for those costs. If the contractor abandoned the project after partial payment, you sue for the amount you paid for work not done or the cost to correct work. (Note: If the amount needed to truly fix or complete your home project exceeds $6k, you may need to go to regular court. Also, if a contractor is licensed, you might have other remedies like complaining to a licensing board, but that doesn’t get you money directly).
- **Appliance/Electronics Warranty Issues:** Yes – if a store or manufacturer fails to honor a warranty on a product and you had to pay for a repair or replacement, you can claim those damages. *Example:* You bought a refurbished laptop with a 1-year warranty, it died after 3 months, and the seller refuses to repair or refund – you could sue for the cost of the laptop. These are usually straightforward claims for a refund or repair cost. (Be aware of any **mandatory arbitration clauses** in purchase agreements or warranties – some warranties require arbitration, which could prevent a court lawsuit. If so, mention it to the judge; sometimes small claims judges still proceed if arbitration isn’t invoked by the other side, but legally an arbitration clause could be enforced).
- **No-Show or Canceled Services (Photographers, Caterers, Venues, etc.):** Yes – if you hired someone for an event or service and they bailed or provided nothing, you can sue to recover any deposits or extra costs. *Examples:* A wedding photographer was prepaid $2,000 and never showed up – small claims can recoup that. Or you reserved an event hall with a $1,000 deposit and the venue double-booked and canceled on you – you can claim the deposit and any added expenses you incurred due to the cancellation. These fall under breach of contract. If you had a written contract with a cancellation clause, bring it. If it’s purely verbal, gather texts/emails as evidence of the agreement and cancellation.
- **Dry Cleaning / Property Loss in Custody:** Yes – if a business that was entrusted with your property lost or damaged it (dry cleaners, storage facility, valet parking, moving company, etc.), you can sue for the value of the lost/damaged items up to $6k. *Example:* A dry cleaner ruined a $500 suit; a moving company broke your $1,200 TV. Even without a formal contract, the law imposes a duty of care on bailee businesses. Bring any receipts and evidence of value (like purchase price of the item). Note: Some claim tickets or contracts may purport to limit the company’s liability (e.g. “not responsible for more than $____ for lost item”) – such provisions can be challenged, but they might complicate recovery if they’re legally enforceable.
- **Gym/Club/Subscription Cancellations:** Yes – if you properly canceled a membership or subscription but kept getting charged, or you were owed a refund of pre-paid fees, you can sue for those amounts. *Example:* You paid a year’s gym membership upfront and the gym closed after 3 months without refunding the remaining $600 – that $600 can be claimed. Or you canceled an online service trial and they still billed you $100 – recover it in small claims. Always show your cancellation notice or any proof that you followed the contract’s cancellation terms.
- **Auto Dealer & Used-Car Sales:** Possibly – small claims can handle certain used car disputes, but **beware of “as-is” sales** and the *limited* nature of lemon law in Wyoming. Wyoming’s **lemon law** applies only to **new vehicles** and primarily gives a right to replacement or refund through the manufacturer; it doesn’t explicitly create a small-claims money remedy for consumers (and typically would exceed $6k anyway). However, you can use small claims for standard contract or fraud claims involving vehicles. *Examples:* A used-car dealer failed to disclose a salvage title or rolled-back odometer – you could sue for the diminished value or repair costs (fraud or misrepresentation). Or if you bought a car that was advertised with certain features that it lacks, or a warranty that the dealer refuses to honor, those can be claims. Many used cars are sold “as is,” which means the dealer disclaims warranties; you generally cannot recover for defects in an as-is sale **unless** you prove intentional fraud. If you do allege fraud, note the 4-year limit from discovery ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,and%20not%20herein%20enumerated%3B%20and)). (If it’s a private party sale, similar principles apply – if they lied about something material like “engine was rebuilt last year” and it wasn’t, you might claim fraud.) Wyoming’s consumer protection laws (discussed below) might also come into play for deceptive practices.
- **False Advertising / Deceptive Trade Practices:** Possibly – Wyoming’s Consumer Protection Act (W.S. 40-12-101 *et seq.*) prohibits unfair or deceptive business practices. It allows consumers to sue for **actual damages** suffered from an “unlawful deceptive trade practice,” *but only if the business was given a chance to cure the problem first*. Specifically, a consumer must rely on an **“uncured” deceptive practice** – meaning you likely need to send a demand or complaint to the business and give them an opportunity to make it right, and they did not do so ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-40/chapter-12/article-1/section-40-12-108/#:~:text=,such%20unlawful%20deceptive%20trade%20practice)). If that is done, you can sue for your actual losses; however, Wyoming’s law does *not* provide for treble damages or automatic attorney’s fees for individual consumer suits (attorney fees are only assured in class actions) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-40/chapter-12/article-1/section-40-12-108/#:~:text=herein%20expressly%20provided,restored%20to%20consumers%20within%20one)). In small claims, you could invoke the Act simply as the legal basis for claiming what you lost. *Examples:* A car dealer’s ad promised a vehicle had never been in an accident when it had – your damages might be the difference in value. Or a retailer engaged in a “bait-and-switch” that cost you money. These can be brought as ordinary misrepresentation claims as well. Note: Wyoming’s consumer act **requires** that the deceptive act result in actual damage to you; it’s not enough that it was a lie – you must have lost money because of it. Also, filing under this Act doesn’t get you extra money in small claims beyond your out-of-pocket loss (no punitive damages in small claims). The Act does allow class actions and AG enforcement for broader issues, which is outside small claims scope.

### Property Damage

- **Vehicle Collisions:** Yes – if someone damages your vehicle in a car accident and their insurance won’t pay (or they are uninsured), you can sue the at-fault driver for your repair costs, rental car, etc., up to $6,000. Many fender-bender claims fall under this cap. *Example:* Fender damage costing $2,800 to fix – sue the other driver if they refuse to pay. If you were partly at fault, Wyoming’s comparative negligence law will apply (your award can be reduced by your percentage of fault, and if you were 50% or more at fault, you recover nothing). The small claims judge will consider any evidence of fault – police reports, witness statements, etc. Typically these are straightforward if liability is clear. (Personal injury from accidents is discussed separately below.)
- **Damage to Personal Property:** Yes – any time someone negligently or intentionally damages your property (other than a vehicle, which see above), you can sue for the repair or replacement cost. *Examples:* A neighbor’s kid threw a rock through your $300 window; a friend borrowed your power tools and broke them and won’t pay; a fight or prank resulted in your phone being destroyed. Provide proof of value (receipts or estimates). The statute of limitations for property damage is 4 years ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)).
- **Tenant Damage to Landlord’s Property:** Yes – after a tenant moves out, a landlord can sue in small claims for the cost to repair damage beyond normal wear and tear (if not covered by the security deposit, or if no deposit). *Example:* Tenant punched holes in walls causing $1,000 in drywall repairs – landlord can sue. Be sure to document with move-out inspection notes and photos. (Landlords: Remember you must follow Wyoming’s deposit return statute: you have 30 days to itemize damages and refund the balance, or 60 days if there’s damage requiring estimates ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=prepaid%20rent%20and%20a%20written,may%20be%20made%20or%20mailed)). If you fail to provide the itemization/refund in time without good reason, the tenant can sue you to recover the **full deposit** and court costs ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=,to%20any%20other%20relief%20available)), so always comply before resorting to court.)
- **Landlord Damage to Tenant’s Property:** Yes – if a landlord or their agent damaged or wrongfully withheld your personal property, you can sue. *Example:* During an apartment repair, the landlord’s contractor broke your television ($500) and landlord refuses to compensate – claim it. Or if you were evicted improperly and the landlord kept or threw away your belongings, you can sue for their value (though if an eviction was legal, the landlord’s duties for stored property are governed by law – but any clear-cut property loss could be claimed). This would be a general property damage or conversion claim (4-year limit) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)).
- **Tree/Fence/Boundary Damage:** Yes – disputes with neighbors about property damage can be handled. *Examples:* A neighbor’s tree fell onto your fence and shed – you could sue for the cost of repairs (assuming it fell due to their neglect or an “act of God” that their insurance refused – note, if it was a healthy tree knocked down by storm, the neighbor might not be liable absent negligence, but you can still bring the claim and let the court decide fault). If a neighbor built a fence on your land or damaged your fence, you can sue for the cost of moving/repair. If a boundary line disagreement exists, small claims **cannot** adjudicate title or order an injunction (e.g. cannot force them to move a fence), but you can sue for monetary damage (like diminution of your property’s value or cost to fix damage). In practice, many boundary encroachment issues require an injunction or quiet title action in district court, but incidental damage (like grading your land without permission) can be claimed in small claims.
- **Pet or Animal Damage:** Yes – if someone’s pet or livestock damages your property, you can sue the owner for the cost. *Examples:* Your neighbor’s dog chewed up your $800 patio furniture; your cattleman neighbor’s cow strayed and trampled your garden causing $1,200 in losses. These are property damage claims (4-year limit) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)). Wyoming is an “open range” state in many areas (livestock owners generally not liable for cattle on open range highways, etc.), but if the situation falls outside those rules or involves negligence, you can still attempt recovery. Conversely, if your pet was harmed by someone (e.g. neighbor injured or killed your dog), you can sue for the monetary value of the pet/vet bills (note: pets are considered property in law; emotional distress or punitive damages for pet injury are not awardable in small claims).
- **Damage During Moves or Storage:** Yes – if professional movers or even friends helping you move end up damaging your belongings, you can claim the repair/replacement cost. *Example:* A moving company dropped your appliance, causing $900 damage; or a friend who borrowed your truck crashed it – you could sue for the deductible or repair cost. If using a moving company, check the contract: many limit liability (e.g. 60 cents per pound under interstate moving rules, etc.). You can still sue for the full amount, but the mover might cite the contract to limit what you get. Still, small claims will adjudicate the fair damages unless a law requires honoring that limit.

### Landlord–Tenant (Financial Only)

*(Small claims cannot handle eviction or return of rental property – those are separate forcible entry/detainer cases in circuit court – but it can address *money* disputes between landlords and tenants.)*

- **Unreturned Security Deposits:** Yes – this is one of the most common small claims filings. If you (tenant) moved out and believe the landlord wrongfully kept some or all of your deposit, you can sue for the amount withheld. Wyoming law requires the landlord to mail you either a refund or an itemized list of deductions within **30 days** after you move out (or 15 days after you provide a forwarding address, whichever is later) ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=prepaid%20rent%20and%20a%20written,may%20be%20made%20or%20mailed)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/security-deposits/#:~:text=Yes,15%20more%20days%20to%20respond)). If there’s damage beyond normal wear and tear, the landlord can take up to 60 days total (an extra 30 days) to assess and repair before finalizing the deposit accounting ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=the%20renter%20within%20thirty%20,may%20be%20made%20or%20mailed)). If the landlord **“unreasonably fails”** to follow these rules – for example, they keep your deposit without sending any itemized damages within the required time – you are entitled to **recover the full deposit** plus court costs, and the landlord forfeits the right to claim any damages against you ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=,to%20any%20other%20relief%20available)). (Essentially, the penalty is that they lose the deposit – but not extra multiples, Wyoming does **not** award double or triple deposit as some states do.) In court, if the landlord did send an itemized list, the judge will decide if the deductions were proper. Photographs and move-in/move-out checklists are very helpful evidence for both sides. **Tip:** As a tenant plaintiff, you should explicitly reference W.S. 1-21-1208 in your claim if the landlord violated it, as that statute gives you the right to full deposit and costs ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=,to%20any%20other%20relief%20available)). Landlords, to avoid this, always comply with the notice deadlines and document your damages.
- **Last Month’s Rent / Prepaid Rent Refund:** Yes – if you paid last month’s rent in advance or other prepaid rent and it was not applied properly, you can claim it back. *Example:* You gave first and last month’s rent when you moved in, but you did end up paying rent through the end of the lease – the “last month” you prepaid was never actually used, and the landlord didn’t refund it. That’s essentially a deposit issue and can be pursued. Or if you vacated early and the landlord re-rented the unit for that period you had paid (double collecting rent), you could seek a refund for overlapping period (though mitigation rules apply). These are straightforward contract damages.
- **“Repair-and-Deduct” or Habitability Expenses:** Possibly – Wyoming does not have an expansive statutory “repair and deduct” right like some states, but it does require landlords to maintain fit premises (implied warranty of habitability). If you as a tenant had to pay for certain repairs because the landlord failed to (and the landlord was obligated to under the lease or law), you could sue to recover those costs. *Example:* The landlord was notified of a broken furnace in winter and didn’t fix it for weeks, so you paid $300 for an emergency heater and higher electric bills – you might claim that $300 as damages for breach of the lease/habitability. There’s no guarantee, as Wyoming has limited tenant remedies, but small claims is a venue to make the argument. Ensure you have proof of notifying the landlord and their failure to act, and that your expenditure was reasonable and necessary.
- **Former Tenant Owing Money to Landlord:** Yes – landlords can sue ex-tenants for unpaid rent, late fees (if provided in lease and not excessive), or physical damage beyond normal wear. *Examples:* Tenant left owing two months’ rent = $1,500 and $800 in damages beyond the deposit – you can sue for $2,300 (assuming deposit was applied first or was insufficient). Landlord must demonstrate the damages with bills or estimates, and compliance with deposit rules as noted. Since eviction (possession) is separate, a landlord often first evicts (to regain the unit) and then files a separate small claim for the money owed. Note: If an **existing court eviction** already awarded rent or damages (in an eviction judgment), the landlord cannot recover those again in small claims – they’d enforce the eviction judgment instead. But if the eviction proceeding did not cover money damages, or additional damages were discovered later, a small claim can be filed.
- **Tenant Claims for Property Being Withheld:** Yes – if a landlord kept your personal belongings after you moved out (e.g. locked them up or disposed of them improperly), you can sue for the value. Wyoming law (W.S. 1-21-1210) allows landlords in certain cases to consider property “abandoned” after eviction and dispose of it, but only after following specific steps like written notice. If those steps weren’t followed, the landlord could be liable. Small claims would treat it as a **conversion** (wrongful taking) claim for the value of your items. Be prepared to prove the items’ worth (photos, lists, receipts).
- **Disputes Over Fees:** Yes – landlords and tenants can dispute miscellaneous fees: cleaning fees, late fees, pet fees, key replacement, etc. A tenant might claim a fee was wrongly charged or not authorized by the lease (seeking a refund), or a landlord might claim the tenant agreed to pay a fee and didn’t. As long as the amount is within $6k, small claims can decide if the fee was valid. *Example:* Lease said “$200 cleaning fee non-refundable” but landlord also deducted cleaning costs from the deposit – tenant could argue that was improper double-dipping and sue for $200 back. Or a tenant failed to pay a $50 late fee and landlord sues – the court can decide if the late fee was enforceable and reasonable.

### Employment & Wages

- **Unpaid Wages / Final Paycheck:** Yes – if an employer didn’t pay you for work you did, you can absolutely sue for those earned wages (up to $6,000, which covers a lot of small wage claims). *Examples:* Your final paycheck for $800 was never issued after you quit or got fired; or your employer withheld a week’s pay unlawfully. Wyoming law generally requires that discharged or quitting employees be paid by the next regular payday (or as per any employment contract) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2021/title-27/chapter-4/article-1/section-27-4-104/#:~:text=27,and%20suit%20for%20wages%3B%20generally)) – if that didn’t happen, that’s a clear claim. Wyoming **does not** have a civil “waiting time penalty” (like some states do where late wages incur a daily penalty), but it does make it a misdemeanor for an employer to willfully not pay as required ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-27/chapter-4/article-1/section-27-4-105/#:~:text=Every%20person%2C%20firm%20or%20corporation,00%29%20for%20each%20offense)). Criminal enforcement aside, for civil purposes you’re entitled to the wages plus normal interest. You have up to 8 years to sue on an oral employment agreement ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)) (most wage claims would be considered implied contracts if no written contract). However, it’s best to act quickly (and you may also file a complaint with the Wyoming Department of Workforce Services Labor Standards office – they can investigate wage claims without cost, which can be faster in some cases). In small claims court, claim the net amount owed (or gross minus any amounts truly paid). Bring pay stubs, time records, or any documents to substantiate hours worked and pay rate. If the dispute is over *how much* is owed (e.g. employer says you violated a policy so they deducted something), the court will decide if that deduction was lawful.
- **Unpaid Overtime, Commissions, or Bonuses:** Yes – any form of agreed compensation that wasn’t paid can be claimed. *Examples:* You earned commissions of $2,000 on sales per your employment agreement but after you left the company they refused to pay; or you met the criteria for a $500 bonus that wasn’t delivered. These are contractual wage claims. (Note: Some commission disputes hinge on company policy – e.g. “no commission paid if sale isn’t finalized by time of termination.” Wyoming generally allows such policies ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2021/title-27/chapter-4/article-1/section-27-4-104/#:~:text=Discharged%20and%20Suit%20for%20Wages%3B,104%20%282021)), so your success may depend on what was agreed. But if you clearly earned it under the terms given, you can pursue it.)
- **Unreimbursed Business Expenses:** Yes – if your employer or a client was supposed to reimburse you for expenses (travel, equipment, etc.) and did not, you can claim those just like unpaid wages. *Example:* You spent $1,200 on work travel (with approval) and your employer never paid you back. This is treated as a debt the employer owes you.
- **Misclassification (Independent Contractor vs Employee):** Small claims can’t resolve labor classifications per se, but if the practical effect is unpaid wages, you can still sue for the money you’re owed. For instance, if you were told you’d be paid as an independent contractor but never received payment, it’s simply an unpaid contract amount – you can sue for that. If the issue is you were classified as contractor and thus not paid overtime or minimum wage, that implicates labor laws (state and federal) which might be better handled by the Labor Standards office or federal Department of Labor. Small claims would only address the concrete money not paid. *Example:* A delivery driver was labeled contractor and paid per route, ending up below minimum wage; he could sue for the difference to at least minimum wage for hours worked – but because this can involve legal arguments about coverage of labor laws, it may be complex for small claims. Still, the court can adjudicate a straightforward claim for wages due under an implied employment agreement.
- **Better Forum – Labor Agency:** Wyoming’s Department of Workforce Services (Labor Standards) can take complaints for wage disputes (wage/hour, final pay, etc.). Often, filing a wage claim with the state agency is free and may prompt the employer to pay without court. However, the agency’s ability to recover might be limited to wages and not other damages, and it may take time. Small claims gives you a direct judgment if you win, which you can then enforce (see Post-Judgment Collection). If your wages claim is large (above $6k) or involves technical violations (e.g. overtime under FLSA), consult the labor agency or an attorney – those might go to federal court or be handled through administrative processes.

- **Note:** Workers cannot use small claims for **workers’ compensation** benefits or on-the-job injury claims – those must go through the Wyoming Workers’ Compensation Division process. But once you have an award (or if an employer failed to carry required work comp insurance and you need to sue for an injury outside the system), that would be outside small claims scope or too high value typically.

### Personal Services & Professional Services Disputes

- **Hairdressers / Barbers / Nail Salons (Botched Service):** Yes – if a personal care service was grossly done wrong and caused damage, you can sue for the cost of fixing it or the harm done. *Examples:* A hairstylist’s chemical treatment ruined your hair, and you had to pay $500 for corrective treatments; a nail salon’s technician injured you causing medical bills; a barber accidentally cut and scarred your skin – you could claim medical costs. Many of these might be under $6k and fit small claims. They could be viewed as negligence or breach of contract. (For significant physical injuries, a regular court might be more appropriate especially if value is high or expert testimony is needed, but minor injuries or costs are fine for small claims.)
- **Tutors, Lessons, Coaching Not Delivered:** Yes – if you paid for lessons or sessions (music teacher, tutor, personal trainer, etc.) and the person didn’t provide them or provided subpar services that flout the agreement, you can sue for a refund or portion of money back. *Example:* You paid $400 for 8 piano lessons and the teacher only gave 2 lessons then disappeared – sue for the $300 value of the undelivered lessons. Or if a private coach promised a certain program, you paid, and they failed to do it, claim what you lost. Essentially a contract claim.
- **Pet Services (Boarding/Grooming/Training):** Yes – if a pet boarder, groomer, or trainer breached their agreement or harmed your pet (monetary harm), you can sue. *Examples:* A dog groomer charged $150 but clearly did not groom the dog (or injured the dog causing vet bills); a pet boarding kennel lost your pet or the pet became ill due to their negligence, incurring vet costs – you can claim those costs. Remember, in law animals are property, so you recover economic damages (vet bills, etc.), not emotional distress for your pet’s suffering.
- **Medical/Dental Bills Disputes (Non-insurance)**: If you were overbilled or charged for services not provided, you can sue to recover the overpayment. However, **malpractice** claims (for injury due to negligence) usually exceed $6k and require expert testimony and are **not** appropriate for small claims. But a simpler **billing dispute** can be handled. *Example:* You paid a dentist $1,000 in advance for a procedure that was never done, and they won’t refund – that’s a straightforward contract issue for small claims. Or a doctor’s office billed you incorrectly and refuses to correct it – you could pay under protest then sue for reimbursement. Any issue tied up with insurance reimbursement or complex medical necessity likely isn’t suited for small claims due to complexity. Also note, Wyoming law mandates a special procedure for medical malpractice claims (including a review panel) for negligence – so small claims is *not* usable for actual malpractice suits. Stick to clear-cut monetary disputes.
- **Daycare/Preschool Refunds:** Yes – if you pre-paid for child care or schooling and the provider closed or you withdrew under a refund-eligible circumstance, you can claim a refund. *Example:* You paid $800 for a month of daycare upfront and after one week the daycare shut down without refund – sue for the unused $600. Or you gave a deposit to reserve a spot in a preschool and the spot fell through – claim the deposit. Read any contract you signed; some deposits might be non-refundable, but if the provider breached the contract (e.g. didn’t provide the service at all), you have a case.

### Loans, Debts, and Financial Disputes

- **Loans to Friends/Family:** Yes – as noted under contract disputes, an informal loan is enforceable. If you lent money believing you’d be repaid, you can sue for repayment (with any interest that was agreed). These cases often come down to evidence of the loan: a written IOU, emails/texts acknowledging the debt, or testimony. Small claims judges will weigh credibility. Even absent a note, your testimony that “I gave John $2,000 and he promised orally to pay me back in six months” can be enough if the judge finds you credible and the defendant doesn’t refute it convincingly. It helps to show proof of the transfer of money (check, Venmo record, etc.). **Tip:** Before suing a relative or friend, consider that if there was any ambiguity (was it a gift or a loan?), the court will need clarity. If the other person claims it was a gift, you’ll need evidence it was a loan (like you asked for repayment later and they acknowledged it). The court can’t enforce pure moral obligations – there must have been a mutual understanding of repayment.
- **Jointly Paid Expenses (Roommates, Ex-Partners):** Yes – disputes over splitting bills can be brought. *Examples:* You and a roommate agreed to split utility bills, but the roommate left you with $500 of unpaid utilities – you can sue for their share. Or an unmarried couple broke up and one person had paid the entire $1,200 vacation cost that was supposed to be shared – they can claim $600 from the other. The key is to frame it as either an **oral contract** (“we agreed to split this expense”) or perhaps **unjust enrichment** if no explicit agreement (“it’s only fair they pay their part of a benefit they enjoyed”). Small claims courts often take an equitable commonsense approach on such arrangements.
- **Wedding/Engagement Gifts and Rings:** Possibly – these cases can be tricky, but if money or property was given contingent on an event (like marriage) that didn’t occur, a party may seek its return. Wyoming follows general principles: an **engagement ring** is often considered a conditional gift on the marriage taking place. If the engagement is broken off, the ring typically should be returned to the giver (especially if the giver was not at fault for the breakup). Small claims could adjudicate the ring’s return or its value (if under $6k) if the ring isn’t returned. *Example:* A $3,000 engagement ring – if the fiancée refuses to give it back after the engagement ended, the fiancé can sue for its value or return (since small claims only issues money judgments, in practice the court might value the ring and award that amount conditional on return). For **wedding expenses** between ex-fiancés (like deposits one paid that were forfeited), those get complicated legally. For **gifts** between people who split up (like one ex gave the other a $2,000 computer during the relationship as a gift) – a true gift can’t be reclaimed. But if it was essentially a **loan** or given with expectation of something that didn’t materialize, one might argue for return. Judges will need evidence of an agreement to repay or return. In general, small claims can handle the monetary aspect but will not order, say, emotional compensation for a breakup – only concrete transfers of money or property.
- **Jointly Titled Property After Breakup:** Small claims can divide **money**, but it cannot formally partition real estate or adjudicate title to personal property. However, if you and an ex bought something together (say a car or boat) and only one kept it, the other could sue for the value of their share. *Example:* Unmarried partners bought a $5,000 car together (both contributed $2,500, title may be in one name). After separation, the one who kept the car might owe the other $2,500 – small claims could award that. This is essentially enforcing an implied agreement to share the property’s value. For major assets (house, etc.), those must go to district court. But small items, or cash accounts you agreed to split, can be handled. Just be sure to couch it as a debt or unjust enrichment claim (e.g. “I paid half for an asset that only they now have, so they should pay me back my half”).
- **Recovery of Cash Deposits / Earnest Money:** Yes – if you put down a cash deposit in any context and the other party unfairly kept it, you can pursue it. *Examples:* A rental security deposit (covered above), a deposit on the purchase of a car or other item that didn’t go through, earnest money on a land sale where the contract was voided – as long as you’re legally entitled to the return under the agreement or circumstances, you may sue. Be mindful of contract terms: often earnest money becomes non-refundable if you back out without cause. But if the deal fell apart through no fault of yours (e.g. seller couldn’t deliver good title), you should get it back. If it’s under $6k, you can let small claims sort it out.
- **Creditor (Bank/Collection Agency) Suing Consumer:** In Wyoming, creditors *can and do* use small claims court to collect debts, and there is no prohibition against it. Collection agencies or businesses owed money may file in small claims as long as the amount is ≤ $6,000. (Wyoming does not have a rule like California’s that bars assignees or limits how many cases a business can file – at least not in statute or rules.) However, if a collection agency is involved, note they must be licensed and follow the federal FDCPA; any harassment issues are separate and wouldn’t stop the small claim but could give the consumer a counterclaim if there were clear violations. Generally, a straightforward debt (e.g. unpaid credit card of $5,000) can be brought in small claims by the creditor or debt buyer. The business can appear via an employee or representative (no attorney required; if they use an attorney, you can get a continuance – see Section 10) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)).
    - **Tip for Consumers:** If you’re sued by a creditor in small claims, you have the right to ask for proof of the debt (contract, account statements). If the plaintiff is not the original creditor, ensure they show assignment paperwork that they own the debt. You can also raise any valid disputes (e.g. identity theft, account already paid, etc.). Small claims is less formal but you still need to assert your defenses.

### Vehicle-Specific Claims

*(Some of these overlap with contract or tort categories above, but to organize common scenarios:)*

- **Accident Damage (Not Covered by Insurance):** Yes – if you were in a vehicle accident and the other party (uninsured or underinsured) won’t pay, you can sue them for the damages up to $6,000. This includes the **deductible** you paid if their insurance accepted some liability but not all, or any amount not covered by insurance. *Example:* Other driver’s insurer only paid 80% because they disputed fault, leaving you with $1,000 out-of-pocket – you could sue the other driver for that $1,000. Or if you had only liability insurance and your car was damaged by the other driver who has no insurance, you’d sue the at-fault driver for the full repair cost or fair market value of the car (if totaled), up to the cap. Remember to bring estimates or receipts for repair, and evidence of their fault (police report, witness, photos). 
- **Mechanic / Body Shop Disputes:** Yes – if a mechanic’s work was negligent (causing new damage) or incomplete or overbilled, you can sue. *Examples:* After a $2,500 transmission repair, your transmission failed again due to mechanic’s error – you paid another shop $3,000 to fix it properly; you can claim the $3,000 from the first mechanic. Or a body shop charged for OEM parts but used cheaper aftermarket parts – sue for the difference in value/cost. As mentioned, have evidence (expert statement from the second shop, photos, the itemized bill showing the discrepancy, etc.). Wyoming does not have a specific auto repair act requiring pre-suit notice or treble damages (some states do), so it’s just a normal breach of contract or negligence claim.
- **Dealership Sales Disputes:** Possibly – as discussed under consumer disputes, you can pursue monetary losses from dealer misrepresentations or breaches of warranty. *Examples:* A dealer sold you a car advertising it had a new engine, but it did not – you could sue for the cost to rebuild the engine or the diminished value (if under $6k). Or the dealer failed to timely send the title/registration causing you losses (like fines or fees), or didn’t pay off your trade-in loan as agreed leaving you with extra interest – those specific losses could be claimed. New car lemon law issues (e.g. multiple repair attempts) typically exceed the small claims domain and have other procedures (manufacturer arbitration etc.), but you could use small claims for a clear-cut refund of something like an extended warranty you canceled or a rebate that was never provided.
- **Towing and Impound Fees:** Yes – if your vehicle was wrongfully towed or impounded and you had to pay to get it back, you can sue the towing company or whoever ordered the tow for reimbursement. *Example:* An illegally posted tow-away zone led to your car being towed; or your landlord towed your car without cause. If the tow was not legally justified (check local ordinances), you could seek the towing and storage fees you paid. Or if your car was damaged during a tow (e.g. scratched or mechanical damage), you can sue for those repair costs. Ensure you have evidence showing the tow was improper or the damage resulted from it. **State-specific note:** Some states have towing laws that allow additional damages or attorney fees for wrongful tows – Wyoming’s statutes (Title 31) have regulations on towing, but no special small-claims penalty. You’ll just claim actual losses.
- **Parking Lot Damage (Known Driver):** Yes – if another driver hit your parked car and you identified them (camera or witness), but they refuse to pay or their insurance denies it, you can sue that driver in small claims for the repair cost (again, up to $6k). *Example:* You have a $750 deductible and the other driver’s insurer won’t pay because their driver denies fault; you can bring the other driver to court, show your proof (video of them hitting car, etc.) and get a judgment. Or if someone *admits* they caused a scrape and agreed to pay but then ghosted you, it’s a straightforward claim.
- **Hit-and-Run (Unknown Driver):** If you **don’t know who hit you**, you unfortunately can’t sue in small claims (you need a defendant). This would fall to your own insurance if you have coverage like uninsured motorist property damage. Small claims requires identifying and serving the responsible person.
- **Vehicle Repair Shop Storage or Mechanic’s Lien Issues:** If a repair shop is holding your vehicle claiming you owe money you dispute, you might pay under protest to get the car, then sue to recover the disputed amount. Or if they already sold your vehicle via a mechanic’s lien sale improperly, you could sue for the value (if within $6k). These are more complex because lien laws apply, but small claims could handle the wrongful conversion aspect if the sale was not per the statute.

### Personal Injury (Small/Minor)

Most personal injury claims (like serious car accidents, major injuries) **exceed** small claims $6,000 limit and involve complicated evidence, so they’re filed in higher courts. However, **minor injury cases under $6,000** in damages can be handled in small claims. Remember, $6,000 must cover **all** your damages – including things like medical bills, lost wages, and pain and suffering (if any is awarded at all – small claims judges may be conservative on pain & suffering).

- **Minor Injuries:** Yes – for example, a minor dog bite that cost you $1,200 in medical treatment, or a slip-and-fall bruising that led to $500 urgent care bill and a week off work – those could be sought in small claims (assuming liability is clear). *Example:* Your neighbor’s dog bit you causing a puncture wound: you paid $300 for a clinic visit and tetanus shot and lost two days’ wages $200 – you can sue for ~$500. The court can also award something for pain/scarring, but given the limit, you’d likely just stick to the tangible costs and maybe a token amount for pain. Wyoming’s 4-year statute for injury applies ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,and%20not%20herein%20enumerated%3B%20and)). If the injury was caused by an intentional act (assault/battery), note that Wyoming’s limitation is only 1 year for assault/battery claims ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)) – so file fast in those cases.
- **Examples of small PI cases suitable for small claims:** a minor fender-bender where you had a $1,000 chiropractic bill and no further complications; a simple assault where you had $500 in medical expenses and seek perhaps another few hundred in damages. Ensure you have evidence of both the **liability** (police report, witness to the assault, etc.) and your **damages** (medical bills, proof of lost income).
- **No Major Injury Suits:** If your injuries are substantial (e.g. broken bones, high medical bills, long-term effects), small claims is not the right forum due to the $6k cap and need for expert testimony on medical issues. Those belong in district court, often with an attorney.

### Specific Statutory Claims

Wyoming does not have an extensive array of specialized private lawsuits with statutory damages like some states, but a few to note:

- **Bad Check Claims:** *Covered above in Contract/Money-Owed.* Wyoming’s bad check statute allows triple damages (3× check amount, min $100) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,00%29%2C%20and%20court%20costs)) – this is a statutory multiplier that *is* allowed in small claims (explicitly by law), as long as the total after trebling is within $6k.
- **Consumer Protection (UDAP) Claims:** *Covered above under False Advertising.* Wyoming’s Consumer Protection Act (W.S. 40-12-101) gives a private right of action for actual damages for deceptive practices, but **requires giving the business a chance to cure** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-40/chapter-12/article-1/section-40-12-108/#:~:text=,such%20unlawful%20deceptive%20trade%20practice)). There are no treble or statutory minimum damages for individual actions (only class actions trigger attorney fee awards) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-40/chapter-12/article-1/section-40-12-108/#:~:text=herein%20expressly%20provided,restored%20to%20consumers%20within%20one)). So, in small claims you could assert a CPA violation but you’ll only recover your actual loss. If a statute provides for attorney fees to the prevailing party (e.g. some landlord-tenant or security deposit laws allow costs and fees to the prevailing tenant, and certain consumer contract statutes do), you could ask for those – but in **small claims, attorney’s fees are rarely awarded** since most people are self-represented and the amounts are small. The judge can award costs (and possibly attorney fees if a specific law compels it and you had an attorney or pro bono legal aid).
- **Lemon Law:** Wyoming’s lemon law (W.S. 40-17-101 to 40-17-121) for new vehicles typically involves either manufacturer replacing the car or refunding – those claims often exceed $6k (value of the car) and typically are handled through the manufacturer’s arbitration program or a direct negotiation. Small claims *could* be used if, say, your new car qualifies as a lemon and the manufacturer actually agreed to pay you a certain amount but hasn’t – you might sue for that agreed amount if ≤ $6k. But usually lemon law relief is outside small claims due to the stakes. A used car sale that is misrepresented might fall under fraud/UDAP as noted, which you can do in small claims if under $6k loss.
- **Identity Theft / Credit Fraud:** If someone stole your identity or committed fraud that caused you financial loss, you can sue that person for damages (assuming you can identify them – often it’s unknown criminals). Wyoming law (W.S. 40-3-117) allows actual damages for identity theft victims against the thief, but realistically most cases involve an unknown perpetrator or one that is judgment-proof. In small claims, such a suit is possible if, say, an acquaintance opened a credit card in your name and you ended up paying $2,000 – you could sue them for that $2,000. Criminal charges can also be pursued independently.
- **Wage/Hour Statutory Damages:** Wyoming doesn’t have extra civil damages for wage violations (unlike some states with double wage penalties). It does criminalize willful nonpayment ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-27/chapter-4/article-1/section-27-4-105/#:~:text=Every%20person%2C%20firm%20or%20corporation,00%29%20for%20each%20offense)), but that doesn’t give you extra money in civil court beyond your owed wages and interest. One exception: if it was a federal wage issue (like federal minimum wage or overtime under FLSA), you might be entitled to liquidated damages (double the unpaid wages) under federal law. You could theoretically sue for those in state court, but doing a FLSA case in small claims is uncommon and could be removed to federal court or require more complex proof. Most small claims for wages stick to straight owed amounts.
- **Other Statutory Penalties:** If a Wyoming statute specifically says a person can sue for a certain penalty amount, check if it limits which court. For instance, some states let tenants sue for improper withholding of deposits for a fixed penalty – Wyoming’s deposit law as noted gives only full deposit plus costs to tenant ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=,to%20any%20other%20relief%20available)). Another example: Wyoming has a law requiring landlords to maintain smoke detectors, but no explicit private damages; enforcement is through local authorities. Always ensure any statutory claim is actually recognized as a private civil cause of action. In doubt, consult an attorney or the statute’s text (and consider if the claim might be better in a different court or agency).

### *Other State-Specific Claim Types*

*(This section covers miscellaneous real-world disputes that don’t neatly fit above but can be brought in Wyoming small claims, as well as some that **cannot**.)*

- **HOA / Condo Association Disputes:** If you live in a homeowners’ association or condo, you might have disputes over fines or fees. Small claims can address **money** issues here. *Example:* The HOA unfairly assessed you a $500 fine for an alleged rule violation – you could pay it under protest (to avoid further penalty) and then sue the HOA to refund it, arguing it was improper. Conversely, an HOA could use small claims to collect unpaid dues or fines (provided they total ≤ $6k). Wyoming doesn’t have a separate HOA court, so small claims is accessible. Keep in mind the governing bylaws and state law (Wyoming Nonprofit Corporation Act, etc.) – they may impact the outcome, but the small claims judge can apply those. Any complex covenant enforcement beyond money (like forcing removal of a fence) is not doable in small claims.
- **Agricultural/Farm Disputes:** Many rural small claims involve things like buying/selling hay, livestock deals, custom farm work, etc. These are essentially contracts – yes, small claims can resolve those money disputes. *Example:* You paid $3,000 for delivered hay bales that turned out to be moldy and unusable – you can sue for your $3k back or a portion reflecting the bad product. Or you hired someone to harvest a field for $5,000 and they never did – claim what you paid or the cost difference of hiring another. As long as it’s a straightforward deal, it qualifies. If it’s an issue like fences on open range or estray animals, the law might have specific procedures, but any clear financial loss someone caused you is generally fair game for small claims.
- **Hunting/Fishing Outfitter or Guide Disputes:** If you hired a guide or outfitter for a trip and they didn’t deliver as promised (and won’t refund), you can sue for the fee. *Example:* You paid a hunting outfitter $4,500 for a multi-day guided hunt, but it was cut short or not as contractually promised (say, due to the guide’s issues) and they refuse refund – you could claim a refund or partial refund commensurate with what you lost. If an outdoors trip was canceled and the contract says “no refunds,” it might be tougher, but you could argue unjust enrichment if the cancellation was in their control. These are contract cases. (Be aware: outfitters in Wyoming are licensed; you could also complain to the licensing board, but that’s separate from getting your money back).
- **Ranch/Farm Work or Livestock Deals:** If someone was supposed to perform work (e.g. custom calving, harvesting) and didn’t, or if you purchased livestock that turned out not as represented (non-breeding, ill, etc.), those are contract issues suitable for small claims (within the dollar limit). For instance, buying a purebred bull for $5,000 that turned out infertile – you might sue for a refund (such a case could also involve warranties and might exceed small claims if high-end animals, but at the small scale, yes). The evidence might involve vet statements, etc., but still manageable.
- **Tort Claims Against Businesses:** If a business caused you minor property damage or loss (outside of consumer purchase context), you can sue. *Example:* A utility company truck ran over your mailbox ($200 damage) – you can bring the company to small claims. Or a hotel’s housekeeping lost an item of yours – sue the hotel for its value. Wyoming’s small claims rules even allow **the state or governmental entities** to appear *as parties* ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)), which implies you could sue a local government for property damage in small claims *if* the claim is of a type not barred by immunity and under $6k (e.g. city garbage truck dented your fence). However, be cautious: government defendants usually require a **Wyoming Governmental Claims Act** notice of claim to be filed first (within 2 years of incident) and there are immunity exceptions. Small claims isn’t explicitly off-limits for permissible suits against a government (since Circuit Courts have jurisdiction over claims up to $50k ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-128/#:~:text=,00%29%2C%20exclusive%20of%20court%20costs)) including against governments), but you must strictly follow the claims act procedures or your case can be dismissed. If suing a government entity, consult W.S. 1-39-101 et seq. for notice requirements *before* suing.
- **Auto Insurance Subrogation:** Sometimes, if you cause a minor accident and your insurance pays the other driver, the insurance might pursue the at-fault uninsured driver in small claims to recover what they paid (subrogation). This is allowed (the insurer essentially steps into the shoes of their insured). So, if you’re the defendant in such a case, it’s effectively the same as if the other driver sued you – you can raise any defenses about liability or damages. Just note that an insurance company can appear through an employee (typically they might send a claims adjuster) per the small claims rule ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)).
- **Miscellaneous Real-World Claims:** In general, think of small claims as covering **any scenario where Person A owes Person B money** and it’s $6,000 or less, *except* those where a statute or public policy puts it elsewhere. For example:
    - If a customer’s check bounced, we covered that (treble damages allowed).
    - If a debt collector violated the federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act – that’s a federal claim often pursued in federal court (with statutory damages up to $1,000). Technically, state courts have jurisdiction over FDCPA claims too, so one *could* bring an FDCPA statutory damages claim for $1,000 in small claims. However, this is rare, and notably FDCPA provides for attorney fees which wouldn’t be relevant if you’re pro se and small claims might not be ideal for arguing federal law. Most would go to federal court or at least regular state civil court for such claims if an attorney is involved. But nothing strictly prohibits bringing a straightforward federal claim under $6k in small claims – the court can apply federal law on a small claim. It’s just uncommon.
    - If a tow company or landlord violated a specific Wyoming statute that provides a penalty, you could bring it if under $6k, as long as that law doesn’t specify a different forum.
    - If you have a claim for statutory **interest** or late fees, those can be included in the demand (e.g. a construction contract where interest accrues on late payment – you’d include whatever interest up to the date of judgment as part of your $6k claim).
    - If the case involves **exemplary or punitive damages** (punishment for egregious conduct), note that small claims **will not award punitive damages** – they stick to actual compensatory damages. Punitive claims also often exceed the limit and require a higher court.

In summary, **small claims in Wyoming can cover nearly any private dispute for money under $6,000**, except those types of relief that are non-monetary or those claims exclusively handled by a different court or process (explained next).

### Counterclaims in Small Claims

**Can a Defendant Counter-sue?** Yes – if you are sued in small claims and believe the plaintiff actually owes *you* money on the same matter or an unrelated matter, you **may file a counterclaim**. Wyoming law does not require a formal written answer, but the **Rules for Small Claims** allow a defendant to assert a counterclaim (there is even a form for a counterclaim in the small claims forms packet). ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/litigation/going-to-court/wyoming-small-claims-courts.html#:~:text=a%C2%A0counterclaim,want%20to%20file%20a%20counterclaim)) Essentially, you would obtain and file a *Small Claims Counter Affidavit* (similar to the plaintiff’s initial affidavit) stating what the counterclaim is and for how much. It should be filed before the hearing (the earlier the better, to give notice). The court clerk can provide guidance on the procedure, and the same $6,000 limit applies to counterclaims (you cannot counter-sue for more than $6,000 in small claims) – **W.S. 1-21-201’s** limit governs all small claims actions ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-201/#:~:text=In%20the%20trial%20of%20civil,subject%20to%20specified%20dollar%20limitations)). 

- **Counterclaim Example:** Plaintiff sues you for $1,500 in unpaid rent. You believe the landlord actually owes you $1,200 for property he damaged and for a wrongful utility charge. You as defendant can file a counterclaim for $1,200. Both the original claim and counterclaim will be heard together at the small claims hearing. The judge could end up netting them out or giving judgment on both (e.g. maybe you owe some rent but are owed some damages).
- **Counterclaim Exceeding $6,000:** If your claim against the plaintiff is legitimately more than $6,000, you have a few options: (a) **waive** the amount above $6k and file it as a counterclaim for $6,000 (effectively capping your recovery); (b) ask the court to **transfer** the case to the regular civil docket (Circuit Court) where you can file a counterclaim of the full amount – Wyoming’s rules don’t explicitly outline this situation, but a circuit judge has authority to certify (transfer) a small claims case to the regular circuit court civil procedure if justice requires. Often, if a counterclaim exceeds the limit, a judge *may* either dismiss the counterclaim (telling you to file it separately in the appropriate court) or allow the entire case to be removed to the higher court (so both claims are heard together with formal procedure). This is somewhat discretionary. If you have a significantly larger claim, you might choose to **opt out** of small claims by asking to remove – Wyoming’s small claims rules allow either party to move for transfer to regular civil **before** the hearing begins, which the judge can grant ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Amended%20May%209%2C%202000%2C%20effective,July%201%2C%202000%3B%206%2C%202003)) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)) (the logic being complicated cases or larger stakes can go the normal route). So, a counterclaim over $6k will likely prompt that. 
- **Failure to Counterclaim:** Wyoming’s small claims procedure is intended to be informal, and there isn’t a strict rule that not countersuing waives your claim (unlike higher courts where compulsory counterclaims must be raised or lost). In small claims, if you have a claim against the plaintiff and you don’t raise it, you generally could still sue them later in another court (though it’s more efficient to handle all at once). However, if the claims arise from the same transaction, it’s wise to present it to avoid multiple suits.
- **Form & Filing:** The small claims forms list includes a “SC-01” for claims; for counterclaims, typically the defendant would fill out the same Small Claims Affidavit form but title it as defendant’s counterclaim. The Wyoming Judicial Branch website suggests contacting the clerk for how to file a counterclaim ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/litigation/going-to-court/wyoming-small-claims-courts.html#:~:text=a%C2%A0counterclaim,want%20to%20file%20a%20counterclaim)). There may be a small filing fee for a counterclaim affidavit (often equal to the filing fee the plaintiff paid, i.e. $10), but confirm with the clerk.
- **If Counterclaim Exceeds Plaintiff’s Claim:** If you counterclaim and your counterclaim is within $6k while the plaintiff’s claim is smaller, the case stays in small claims. The judge at hearing can award both parties relief as appropriate. For example, plaintiff claims $1,000; defendant counterclaims $2,000; the judge might find for defendant on counterclaim fully and for plaintiff partially, resulting in a net judgment or two judgments. The small claims court can handle that.
- **No Counterclaim?** If the defendant doesn’t file a formal counterclaim, they can still **present defenses** at the hearing. They can also bring up that the plaintiff actually owes them money as a set-off even if not formally filed – the judge might treat it as a counterclaim if it’s straightforward, or might say “I can’t award you anything because you didn’t file a counterclaim, but I’ll consider it in equity if appropriate.” It’s better to file the counterclaim officially to give notice.
- **Third-Party Involvement:** Small claims rules in Wyoming do not provide for adding third parties (no third-party complaints). It’s just plaintiff(s) and defendant(s) as filed. If a defendant feels someone else is responsible for the situation (e.g. a contractor sues you, but you believe a subcontractor actually caused the loss), you can’t formally add that subcontractor in small claims. You’d have to pursue them separately in another claim if needed.

### What **You Cannot Sue For** in Small Claims

Certain matters are **outside the jurisdiction** of Wyoming small claims (and often any small claims court). These include:

- **Evictions / Possession of Real Property:** You cannot use small claims to evict a tenant or otherwise obtain an order for possession of land. Eviction cases (called **Forcible Entry and Detainer**) are handled in a separate process in Circuit Court (or sometimes Justice Court) under different statutes ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-128/#:~:text=,205)). Small claims is only for monetary relief. So, a landlord must file an eviction action (if removing a tenant) and **not** a small claim for that purpose. (A landlord could separately file a small claim for rent owed, or more commonly, include a rent claim in the eviction case itself.)
- **Title to Real Estate:** Small claims cannot determine ownership of real property or resolve boundary title disputes. Those must go to District Court. If your dispute is that someone is claiming your land or you need a property line adjudicated, small claims is not appropriate. (However, as noted, you can sue for **damages** resulting from a boundary issue – e.g. the cost to move a fence that encroaches – but not for a court order moving the fence.)
- **Family Law Matters:** Small claims cannot handle divorce, child support, child custody, alimony, or any issues arising from family law orders. Those are exclusively in District Court. If your ex owes you money under a divorce decree, you enforce it in the divorce case, not by a new small claim. (If it’s a totally unrelated debt between ex spouses, you could, but typically anything related to marriage dissolution belongs in that case file.)
- **Inheritance or Probate Issues:** If you are owed money from an estate, or disputing an estate distribution, that goes through probate court (District Court). Small claims won’t override a probate. You’d file a claim in the probate, not a small claim against an executor.
- **Libel/Slander (Defamation) above $6k:** You technically *can* sue for defamation in small claims (Wyoming allows libel/slander suits, but they must be filed within 1 year of the defamatory act ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for))). However, the **recoverable damages for defamation are often intangible (reputation harm)** and could easily exceed $6,000. Small claims judges are likely uncomfortable awarding significant sums for things like emotional distress or reputational injury without formal process. If you have a small, concrete defamation claim (say you lost a $2,000 contract because someone told a lie about you, and you have proof of that specific loss), you *could* try it in small claims. But for anything substantial or requiring extensive evidence of reputational harm, it’s not suitable. Moreover, defamation cases involve constitutional considerations and are complex – small claims is not recommended for these. Thus, practically speaking, defamation is **not** handled in small claims.
- **Professional Malpractice:** Claims against licensed professionals (doctors, lawyers, engineers, etc.) for negligence typically require expert testimony and involve damages beyond $6k. For example, suing a doctor for malpractice is definitely outside small claims – it must go through the **Medical Review Panel** (a pre-suit requirement in Wyoming for med mal) and then usually District Court, and damages are usually much higher. Even a straightforward case against, say, a accountant or attorney for a loss would likely exceed $6k or need expert proof. So small claims is not used for malpractice.
