- **Federal Claims Requiring Federal Jurisdiction:** Certain claims are exclusively federal (e.g. patent, trademark, and copyright infringement; federal antitrust; federal civil rights actions against government under 42 U.S.C. §1983 seeking injunctions or high damages). Those cannot be filed in state small claims. If you have a small copyright claim (under $6k damages) theoretically you could bring it, but federal law requires such suits to be in federal court. So, not in small claims. Similarly, if your claim is under federal statutes like ERISA (e.g. suing over a denied employee benefit) or federal tax matters – those are for federal courts or administrative agencies, not state small claims.
- **Class Actions or Mass Tort:** Small claims cannot be used for class action lawsuits or any kind of representative action on behalf of others. It’s only for individual claims. (The Wyoming Consumer Act allows class actions in District Court, but not in small claims.) If many people have the same small claim, each must file individually – the court won’t consolidate them as a class.
- **Orders for **Specific Performance** or **Injunctions****: You cannot ask the small claims court to *force* someone to do something (other than paying money or returning property of a certain value). For example, you can’t sue a contractor in small claims and ask “make him finish my house” – you can only seek money to hire someone else or for the unfinished work’s diminished value. Or if a neighbor’s fence is encroaching, you can’t ask small claims to order it moved (that’s injunctive relief) – you could only sue for monetary harm (e.g. rental value of the strip of land, etc. – which is often nominal).
- **Restraining Orders:** Not in small claims. Any request for a restraining order or protection order goes through a different court process (for stalking/harassment protection, through Circuit Court as a civil protective order; for domestic issues, through District Court).
- **Suing the State of Wyoming or Local Governments (Sovereign Immunity):** While Wyoming’s statutes do allow the state or a government entity to appear in small claims ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)) (typically as plaintiff), suing *them* as a defendant is restricted by the Governmental Claims Act. You generally must file a notice of claim with the governmental entity and its insurer within the time frame, and then any lawsuit would be in District Court (for claims above $7,500 the law requires District Court). In fact, W.S. 1-39-117 says actions under the Claims Act are brought in **“the district court of the county”** where the cause arose or where claimant resides if agreed. This suggests you **cannot sue a governmental entity in Circuit (small claims) if the claim is under the Claims Act**. For very small claims against a town or county (like $200 property damage from a city vehicle), you might see if the entity will pay without suit, because technically to sue, you’d go to District Court (which is more expensive to file). Small claims is not the proper venue in those cases due to statutory jurisdiction issues. 
- **Workers’ Compensation and Other Administrative Matters:** If your matter falls under a specialized admin system – e.g., workers’ comp benefits, unemployment benefits, state tax disputes, professional license issues – you cannot bypass those by going to small claims. For instance, you cannot sue your employer in small claims for an on-job injury (workers’ comp is exclusive remedy in most cases). You also can’t sue for punitive damages or pain & suffering from a workplace injury.
- **Bankruptcy-Stayed Debts:** If someone owes you money but they have filed bankruptcy and the debt is dischargeable, you cannot continue or commence a small claim – it would violate the automatic stay. You’d have to go to bankruptcy court to seek relief from stay or determine dischargeability. So, if you learn the defendant is in an active bankruptcy, alert the small claims court – the case will be put on hold or dismissed. Post-discharge, if the debt was discharged, you cannot pursue it at all. If the debt was not discharged (e.g. occurred after filing, or was a type like recent malicious injury not discharged), then you could proceed after the bankruptcy case. Always check PACER or ask for proof of bankruptcy if a debtor mentions it.
- **Criminal Fines or Restitution:** You cannot use small claims to collect fines owed to the state or restitution ordered in a criminal case. Restitution is enforced through the criminal court. Similarly, if someone owes you money under a **criminal restitution order**, you enforce it in that criminal case (often it’s converted to a civil judgment that you can then record and garnish on through district court). A separate small claim isn’t needed (and likely would be dismissed as duplicative).
- **Child Support or Alimony:** Those are family law orders – not small claims. If unpaid, you enforce via contempt or income withholding in the family court case, not by a new lawsuit. (However, note: If someone who isn’t an ex-spouse just owes you money related to child expenses outside of any court order – e.g. an informal loan to your co-parent – that’s a normal debt claim, which could be small claimed. But formal support obligations must go through the proper court.)
- **Cases Exceeding $6,000:** This sounds obvious, but if your damages *even arguably* exceed $6k, you should file in regular court. You cannot skirt the limit by asking for exactly $6,000 when in reality your losses are a lot higher – courts frown on splitting or artificially capping if the remainder can’t be recovered later. If you do waive the excess explicitly (i.e. you willingly give up everything over $6k), that’s allowed, but it’s a permanent waiver. If you try to get a $6k judgment in small claims and then sue for the rest later, the second suit will be dismissed for claim preclusion. So you must choose: take up to $6k now and lose the rest, or sue in higher court for full amount.
- **Punitive/Exemplary Damages:** Small claims judges in Wyoming will **not award punitive damages**. Punitive damages are beyond making you whole – they punish the wrongdoer for egregious behavior. Such awards require a higher standard and are generally outside the scope of a simplified proceeding. You are limited to your actual losses (and any statutory multipliers like the bad check triple, which is more like a liquidated damage allowed by statute). Even if you prove someone acted willfully or wantonly, don’t expect a small claims court to grant punitive money.
- **Emotional Distress or Pain & Suffering (except minor amounts):** While you can claim intangible damages in certain cases (like pain and suffering from a minor injury, or emotional distress if tied to a tort like assault), small claims tends to be conservative about those because they are subjective. They will primarily ensure your economic losses are covered. If your case is all about emotional harm (e.g. intentional infliction of emotional distress as a standalone claim), that’s not a good fit for small claims – such cases belong in regular court and often require expert testimony or significant narratives.
- **Judicial or Administrative Review:** Small claims is not an appellate court. You cannot use it to “appeal” a decision of a government agency or another court. For example, if you disagree with a decision of a professional licensing board or a property tax assessment, you must use the proper channels (administrative appeals to district court, etc.), not file a small claim.

**Bottom Line:** If in doubt, ask the clerk. Wyoming’s small claims clerks can tell you if a particular type of case is commonly handled or if it’s not allowed. The Wyoming Judicial Branch’s self-help resources also list many of the above exclusions. It’s better to identify an improper claim beforehand than have the judge dismiss it at the hearing.

### Damages — What You Can and Cannot Recover in Small Claims

**Allowed Compensatory Damages:** Small claims can award **compensatory damages**, meaning the actual monetary loss you suffered. This includes out-of-pocket costs, the value of property that was damaged or lost, amounts you paid and should be refunded, etc. Essentially, anything that can be given a dollar value and is a direct result of the defendant’s actions can be awarded, up to the $6,000 limit (or less, depending on proof). If you’re suing for property that was not returned, the court can award you its **market value** (but not force return of the item). If you’re suing for lost income or wages (and you prove the amount), that can be awarded. If it’s a service that was paid and not rendered, the amount paid is straightforward. If it’s defective work, the typical measure is the cost to repair or the difference in value.

**Statutory Multipliers/Penalties:** If a specific statute entitles you to a multiple of damages or a set penalty, the small claims court can award that as long as it doesn’t push the total beyond $6,000 (unless the statute is considered a “penalty” in which case see below on penalties). We saw the example of treble damages for bad checks – that is expressly allowed ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,00%29%2C%20and%20court%20costs)). Another example: if a statute said “violation of this act makes the person liable for $500 penalty to the consumer,” that is something a small claims court can grant as a statutory damage (assuming it is indeed a private right of action and not solely enforceable by the state). **However**, if the statute calls it a “penalty or forfeiture,” Wyoming’s limitation statute says such actions have a 1-year limit and the specific statute’s procedure must be followed ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,including%20sexual%20assault)). Many statutory penalties are enforced by agencies or require a certain court. In small claims, you’re safest sticking to actual damages or clearly authorized multiples like the bad-check triple.

**Incidental and Consequential Damages:** You can recover not just the immediate loss but other reasonable costs that resulted. For example, if a contractor’s breach forced you to hire someone else for more money, the difference is consequential damage – recoverable. If a tow company’s improper tow caused you to take a taxi to retrieve your car, you can add that taxi cost. Judges will usually award such incidental costs if directly linked and documented.

**Interest:** Wyoming law provides **pre-judgment interest** on certain liquidated claims and **post-judgment interest** on all money judgments. In small claims, if your claim is for a liquidated sum (a fixed debt like “loan of $5,000 on which interest was 5%”), you can ask for interest from the due date to the judgment date. Even if not specifically contracted, Wyoming allows prejudgment interest on liquidated damages (where the amount was certain and due at a certain time). It’s at the court’s discretion, usually at the statutory rate of 10% ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)) unless a different rate was agreed in a contract (then that contract rate applies) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=per%20year%20from%20the%20date,of%20rendition%20until%20paid)). For example, on an unpaid $2,000 bill due 1 year ago, you could request ~$200 interest for the year at 10%. Many small claims plaintiffs don’t explicitly claim interest in their filings, but you can mention it to the judge. **Post-judgment interest** is automatically on the judgment by law at **10% per annum** in Wyoming ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)) (or if the judgment is on a contract that had a different rate, it bears that rate ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=per%20year%20from%20the%20date,of%20rendition%20until%20paid))). You don’t need to ask for it – it accrues until the judgment is paid. The judgment form should reflect that interest will accrue at the legal rate. (At 10%, roughly $0.0274 per day per $100 owed. If the defendant doesn’t pay promptly, this adds up, and you can potentially recover that interest when you collect.)

**Court Costs:** If you win, you are typically entitled to **court costs** – this includes the **filing fee** ($10) and the **service of process fee** you paid (e.g. what you paid the sheriff or process server to serve the papers). Wyoming statutes and rules generally allow the prevailing party to recover costs. In small claims, the practice is to award costs to the winner by adding them to the judgment amount ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=,to%20any%20other%20relief%20available)). Make sure to ask the judge to include your costs. Keep receipts (like the sheriff’s invoice). These costs do *not* count against the $6,000 cap – they’re awarded on top ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-201/#:~:text=Libraries%20Universal%20Citation%20Guide%20and,not%20necessarily%20the%20official%20citation)). If each party wins partly (say both have some judgment), the court might offset or split costs – but if one side clearly prevails, that side gets costs.

**Attorney’s Fees:** In Wyoming small claims, **attorneys can participate** (unlike some states that ban them) but if one side has an attorney, the other can get a continuance ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). However, **can you get attorney’s fees in the judgment?** Generally, each party pays their own attorney’s fees in lawsuits unless a contract or statute says otherwise. Small claims is no exception. So if you hired a lawyer (which is uncommon for such a small amount, but possible), you won’t automatically get their fees back from the other side if you win. The only times attorney’s fees might be awarded: (1) **Contractual Attorney Fee Clause** – e.g. a lease or promissory note that says “if this debt is collected by an attorney, debtor pays attorney’s fees.” The court can enforce that and award reasonable fees to the prevailing party if an attorney was used. But in small claims, since most don’t use attorneys, it’s rare. (2) **Statutory Attorney Fees** – e.g. if you sued under a law that grants fees (like some consumer protection class actions or civil rights – but those aren’t small claims domain typically). As an individual consumer under Wyoming CPA, you wouldn’t get fees; as a tenant under the deposit statute, the court can award *court costs* but not attorney fees, unless perhaps the withholding was **unreasonable** (the statute only mentions court costs explicitly ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=,to%20any%20other%20relief%20available))). Generally, don’t expect attorney fees in small claims judgment unless you have a clear contract clause and you actually had attorney expenses.

**Punitive Damages:** As noted, punitive damages (meant to punish the wrongdoer) are not something small claims will award. They are outside the mandate of compensatory relief and typically beyond the scope of a $6k case. If you feel punitive damages are warranted (e.g. in a fraud case with malice), that case probably belongs in District Court (and indeed Wyoming law requires a separate hearing and higher standard for punitive damages). So **no punitive damages** in small claims – stick to your actual losses (or statutory double/treble if applicable).

**Non-Monetary Remedies:** Small claims cannot directly order someone to *do* something other than pay. For instance, they won’t order “repair the fence” or “apologize” or anything of that sort. They also won’t rescind or rewrite contracts (other than effectively by awarding a refund). They just give money judgments. If your aim is to get an item back (like a specific family heirloom), small claims isn’t ideal because they can’t force delivery of a specific item. Instead, they’d value the item and give money – which might not be what you truly want. So consider a replevin action in Circuit Court if you need the item itself.

**Mitigation of Damages:** Remember, you have a duty to mitigate (minimize) your damages. For example, if your tenant abandoned the unit, the landlord should try to re-rent rather than letting it sit empty for months and piling up rent – the court might not award all lost rent if they didn’t attempt to mitigate. Or if someone owes you $1000 and offers $500 settlement that you unreasonably refuse and you only recover $500 in court, the court isn’t going to give you any extra for having refused the earlier offer. Small claims judges use common sense fairness – they won’t reward a party for letting damages unnecessarily increase.

**Interest on Judgment:** Already noted above – 10% simple interest per year ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)) on the unpaid balance from the date of judgment until paid. This is automatically accruing. If you enforce the judgment later via a garnishment, you can include the accrued interest in the balance. Wyoming judgments accrue interest at a healthy rate (10%), so there is a real cost to the debtor for not promptly paying a judgment.

**Multiple Party Issues:** If there are multiple defendants and you win, the judgment can be **joint and several** (meaning each defendant is liable for the full amount, so you can collect from either, but you can’t double collect). The court will clarify if, say, two roommates owe you $1,000, whether each owes $500 or both owe $1,000 together. Usually, if they were both responsible for the whole debt, the judgment is joint for the full amount. If it was clearly divisible, the judge might apportion. Also, if both a business and an individual guarantor are defendants and you win, the judgment will likely be against both for the full sum (again, you collect once but from either/or).

In summary, **small claims damages are intended to compensate your proven loss**. Bring proof for every dollar you claim – invoices, contracts, receipts, estimates, etc. The court will not award amounts based on speculation or punishment. If you keep your claim within those bounds, you’ll maximize your chances of full recovery (within the cap).

## 3. Where to file

**Court Name:** In Wyoming, small claims cases are filed in the **Circuit Court** for the appropriate county ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=are%20suing%20is%20the%20Defendant,you%20are%20filing%20your%20suit)). There isn’t a separate “Small Claims Court” building – small claims is a streamlined procedure within the Circuit Courts (which are the limited jurisdiction courts in Wyoming handling civil cases up to $50,000, family violence protection orders, misdemeanor criminal cases, etc.). When you go to file, you’ll go to the Circuit Court Clerk’s office. Some Circuit Courts may colloquially refer to “small claims division,” but it’s the same court staff – just tell them you want to file a small claim.

**Venue (Which County):** Wyoming’s venue rules for small claims recently expanded. **Up until 2021**, you could only sue in the county where the **defendant resides** ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,December%201%2C%202021)). Effective December 1, 2021, the rule now allows filing in the county where **the defendant has an address *or* where the cause of action (or part of it) arose** ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)). This aligns small claims with standard venue provisions. So, you have two main choices for venue:
- **County of Defendant’s Residence or Principal Address.** If you’re suing a person, that typically means the county where they live. If it’s a business, use the county where the business is located or does business (for a company, also consider where its registered agent is, if suing a company). This is usually the preferred venue for small claims.
- **County Where the Dispute Occurred.** If the claim arose from something that happened in a different county (and that’s not also where defendant lives), you can opt to file there. For example, if you contracted with someone in County A and they did work in County A, but then they moved to County B, you could still file in County A because that’s where the contract was made/performed – your “cause of action” arose there. Or if a car accident happened in County X but the other driver lives in County Y, you may file in County X (accident location) or County Y (their home). Venue is proper in either under the new rule.
  
**Practical Tip:** If multiple defendants are in different counties, venue is okay in any county where **any one** of the defendants resides or where the claim arose ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)) – typically you’d choose where at least one defendant is, or where it arose. 

If you file in the county where the defendant lives, service is easier (the court or sheriff is local to them). If you file where the event occurred but the person lives far away, you can still do it, but service might need to go to that other county (which is doable by mail or by getting service done in that county – often the clerk can mail by certified mail statewide ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete))). Consider convenience: you and your witnesses will need to appear in the filing county for the hearing. If you and the evidence are mostly where the incident happened, that county might be best. If both parties actually live elsewhere, maybe file where the defendant lives as that’s required for older rules and still a safe bet under new rules.

**Improper Venue:** What if you file in the “wrong” county? The defendant can object and the case could be moved or dismissed. Under Wyoming rules, the small claims affidavit form asks for the defendant’s address and that helps ensure proper county. If you accidentally file in the wrong Circuit Court, the defendant could motion to dismiss for improper venue (or more informally, the judge may simply raise it). The likely result is the case gets dismissed without prejudice so you can refile in the correct county. It’s important to get it right to avoid delay. Wyoming’s small claims Rule 4 (venue) is straightforward now: sue where defendant resides or where the cause arose ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)). Stick to that. If both those places are inconvenient for you, unfortunately that’s how it is – you can’t haul someone to a county with no connection. 

There is **no provision to transfer** a small claim from one county’s Circuit Court to another for convenience; it’s essentially dismiss and refile in proper county if venue was wrong. So ensure venue is proper from the start.

**Multiple County Scenario:** If the dispute spans multiple counties (e.g. contract negotiated in County A, signed in County B, performed in County C), venue is proper in any where a “substantial part” of the events occurred. Generally, where it was performed or breached would count. If in doubt, choose the defendant’s home county.

**Residence for Businesses:** If suing a business, a **corporation or LLC** is generally considered to “reside” in the county of its principal office or registered agent. A partnership or sole proprietorship resides where it does business or the owner lives. If a business has a branch in your county where the issue happened, that likely qualifies as cause of action locale. Wyoming’s rule references “defendant’s address” ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)), which for a business could be the address where it operates. For example, if a contractor from Casper did work for you in Cheyenne, you could sue in Laramie County (cause arose there in Cheyenne) even though their base is Natrona County. Or you could go to Natrona (their home base).

**Filing Against Out-of-State Defendants:** If the person or business you want to sue is not in Wyoming at all, Wyoming courts might lack jurisdiction unless the dispute has a Wyoming connection. Circuit Courts have statewide jurisdiction, but if the defendant is non-resident, you must serve them (often personally) out of state and also ensure Wyoming’s long-arm statute covers your case (e.g. they did business in Wyoming or caused injury in Wyoming). If, say, you had an online transaction with someone in Colorado and they never delivered the item, you *could* try suing in Wyoming if part of transaction was here (like you sent payment from here), but enforcement even if you get judgment is tricky – you’d have to domesticate that judgment in Colorado to collect. Often it’s better to sue an out-of-state defendant in their state’s courts. Wyoming small claims likely won’t be practical if the person is truly out-of-state with no Wyoming presence (the court might dismiss for lack of personal jurisdiction if the person has no contacts here related to the claim). If the claim is eligible for Wyoming (e.g. an out-of-state contractor did work in Wyoming), then venue would be where it arose and you’d serve them via certified mail or personal service out of state. Keep in mind collecting across state lines adds steps.

**Filing Location:** Each county has at least one Circuit Court location (some larger counties have multiple Circuit judges but usually one clerk’s office). You should file at the **Circuit Court Clerk’s office** for that county. The Wyoming Judicial Branch website has a **Circuit Court Directory** with addresses and phone numbers ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=However%2C%20the%20Sheriff%E2%80%99s%20Office%20or,for%20the%20Circuit%20Court%20Directory)). For example, if your venue is Laramie County (Cheyenne), go to the Circuit Court for Laramie County at the courthouse. If it’s a small rural county, the circuit court might be in the county courthouse or a branch office.

**Electronic / Online Filing:** Currently, Wyoming does **not** have statewide e-filing for small claims. The Wyoming courts have been planning a new case management system, but as of 2026, small claims filings are done on paper (or you may be able to email forms in some courts – but confirm with the clerk). There is mention of “eFile Cover Sheets” in the forms ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2007,732)), which suggests that some circuits might accept filings by email/fax or that an e-filing system is being tested. In general, expect to **file in person or by mail**. If you can’t physically go, you can mail your completed affidavit and summons to the clerk with the fee, or perhaps some courts allow filing by email plus mailing a check. Always call the clerk to ask about remote filing options. The official stance: no broad online portal yet for trial courts. (There is an appellate e-filing for the Supreme Court, but not relevant here ([efiling.courts.state.wy.us](https://efiling.courts.state.wy.us/#:~:text=Case%20Search%20Clerk%27s%20Office%20Supreme,signed%20original%20and%20six%20copies)).) So plan to file at the courthouse or by mail. 

**Venue Mistakes:** If you file in one county and it should have been another, the judge at the small claims hearing can dismiss for improper venue under Rule 4. They might dismiss “without prejudice,” meaning you can refile in the correct county (pay the fee again unfortunately). It’s unlikely they would transfer it for you. Thus, double-check venue. The small claims affidavit form includes the defendant’s address and likely the clerk will flag if it’s out-of-county. But ultimately it’s on you.

**Changing Venue:** If after filing, circumstances change (e.g. defendant moves to a different WY county before service), venue is still assessed at time of filing, and cause-of-action location doesn’t change, so you’re okay. If a defendant feels a different county is more appropriate (but one of the allowed venues is already chosen by you), they can’t force a change just for convenience if venue is proper where filed. Only if it was *improper* do they get a change (really a dismissal). So the plaintiff usually gets to choose among the permitted venues.

In summary, **file in a county that has a logical connection to the defendant or the dispute**. A safe bet is the defendant’s county. Use the Circuit Court in that county – addresses and phone numbers can be found on the Wyoming Judicial Branch website’s directory ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=However%2C%20the%20Sheriff%E2%80%99s%20Office%20or,for%20the%20Circuit%20Court%20Directory)). For example, if suing someone in Gillette, go to Circuit Court of Campbell County; if the incident was in Jackson and defendant is out of state, you might file in Teton County (Jackson) if that’s where it arose. The clerk will issue a small claims **Summons** with a hearing date (see next sections), so pick a county accessible for you because you’ll have to attend the hearing there.

*(Venue reference: Wyo. Stat. § 1-21-203(b) incorporates general venue statutes ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2017/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=return%20receipt%20requested,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2017/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=%28b%29%20Venue%20provisions%20in%20W,actions%20commenced%20under%20this%20article)), and Small Claims Rule 4 now explicitly allows cause-of-action venue ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)).)*

## 4. Filing fees

**Filing Fee:** Wyoming keeps small claims affordable – the filing fee in any small claim case is **$10.00** flat ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=,cost%20to%20file)). This is the fee to file the Small Claims Affidavit (complaint) with the Circuit Court. It’s the same whether your claim is $50 or $6,000. This $10 fee is set by court rule ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%203)). You pay it to the Circuit Court clerk when you file; they’ll usually accept cash, check, or money order (call ahead for credit card acceptance – some courts in Wyoming accept cards with a small extra charge, but policies vary by county).

There are no additional escalating fees based on the claim amount in small claims – $10 is the total filing fee (compare to regular civil cases in Circuit Court which have higher fees on a sliding scale). So, Wyoming is very inexpensive to access small claims.

**Service of Process Fees:** In addition to the $10 filing fee, you will incur costs to serve the Summons and Affidavit on the defendant(s). Wyoming allows service in small claims either by sheriff/per process server or by certified mail in some circumstances:
- Most commonly, plaintiffs use the **Sheriff’s Office** or a private process server to hand-deliver the papers ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=The%20defendant%20must%20be%20served,your%20case%20cannot%20go%20forward)). Sheriff departments charge a fee (often around $30-$50) for service of civil papers. For example, if the defendant is in the same county, the sheriff might charge ~$30 to serve them at their address. If the defendant is in another Wyoming county, you can have that county’s sheriff serve – you’d coordinate through the clerk or directly and pay their fee (it may vary by county but generally similar range).
- **Certified Mail by Clerk:** Wyoming law (W.S. 1-21-203) provides that if the defendant *resides in Wyoming*, the court **may** serve the summons by certified mail, return receipt requested, to the defendant’s in-state address ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)). This means the clerk can mail it for you (some courts do this routinely). There’s typically a small fee for certified mail (roughly $7-$10). Check with the clerk whether they do mail service. The catch: Service is only complete if the defendant (or their agent) signs the receipt and it comes back ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=on%20the%20defendant%20in%20the,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)). If they refuse or don’t claim the mail, you’ll have to do personal service. The official Wyoming Judicial Branch FAQ emphasizes personal service: “The defendant must be served in person…papers must be handed to the defendant” ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=The%20defendant%20must%20be%20served,your%20case%20cannot%20go%20forward)), which suggests in practice they often rely on sheriff or process server. But the statute does allow certified mail *as an option*. Using sheriff or process server is more reliable because they will physically find the person.
- **Private Process Server:** You can hire a local certified process server (fees vary, often $50-$75). In some counties, private servers exist; in others, maybe not. The clerk can advise if any are available locally. The advantage can be speed or flexibility in timing. Either way, ensure proof of service is filed (the server or sheriff will complete a Return of Service form).
  
**Who Pays for Service:** Initially, **you (the plaintiff)** pay the service fee, whether it’s to the sheriff or mailing costs. But if you win the case, you can ask to have that cost added to your judgment ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=,to%20any%20other%20relief%20available)) so the defendant ultimately reimburses it. Wyoming statute and rules contemplate awarding “court costs” to the prevailing party, and service fees are part of costs. So save your receipt or the amount – the court often just includes it if you mention it at hearing or list it on the affidavit. (On the small claims Affidavit form, there may be a line to list the filing and expected service cost – check the form SC-01; even if not, bring it up to the judge.)

If you need to attempt service multiple times or via alias summons, each attempt may incur a separate fee which you’ll have to pay. As the FAQ notes: each time you have to re-serve (alias summons with a new date), you pay for that service attempt ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=If%20the%20Defendant%20is%20not,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)). If you eventually win, you might recover the total of all service fees, but if the defendant was evading and you had to do multiple tries, be prepared to lay out those costs.

**Fee Waiver (In Forma Pauperis):** If you cannot afford the $10 filing fee or service cost, you can apply for a fee waiver. Wyoming courts allow indigent persons to proceed *in forma pauperis* (IFP) upon approval. You would need to file an **Affidavit of Indigency** (some courts have a form for this) disclosing your finances. A judge reviews it and can waive the fees. Given the small amount ($10), this is not commonly needed, but it’s available. If you’re truly without funds, ask the clerk for the local process – typically you file the indigency affidavit along with your complaint. If granted, you wouldn’t pay the $10 or sheriff fee; the court might ask the sheriff to serve without charge (the sheriff’s statutory fees can be waived for indigents in some cases). Note, if you win and collect, the court could theoretically require you to pay back waived fees from the judgment, but usually it’s just waived.

**Other Costs at Filing:** Generally none. There’s no “summons issuance” fee separate from filing like some states have. The $10 covers filing and issuance of summons. If you have multiple defendants, it’s still $10 total (not per defendant). If you want additional copies of documents or certified copies, there might be small per-page fees, but not mandatory. For example, if you want a certified copy of the judgment to file a lien, the statute mentioned a $5 fee for an abstract ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=,00%29%20fee)) and perhaps nominal per page copy fees.

**E-Filing Fees:** Since e-filing isn’t broadly available, no user fees apply. If e-filing is piloted, there might be a registration but currently not relevant.

**Recoverable Fees:** As mentioned, if you win, you get back filing and service fees as part of judgment ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=,to%20any%20other%20relief%20available)). If you lose, you’re out those fees. If it’s a mixed result, judge may decide each bears their own, or may award costs to the party that substantially prevailed.

**Payment Methods:** Most circuit courts accept cash or check. Some accept credit card with a few-dollar surcharge (since $10 is small, they might not have a surcharge). It’s wise to call the clerk: “What forms of payment do you take for small claims filing?” If mailing, you’d send a check or money order to “Circuit Court of X County”.

**Service Options and Fees Quick Reference:**
- Sheriff in same county: pay at clerk or sheriff (the clerk will instruct how – some clerks collect the fee and forward the paperwork to the sheriff; other times they give you the papers to take to the sheriff yourself with payment). The Circuit Court Directory can give contact for sheriff’s civil process division.
- Sheriff in another county: the clerk may send the summons to that county’s sheriff – you may need to provide a check payable to that sheriff’s office. Confirm with your clerk.
- Certified mail by court: pay the certified postage (the clerk will tell you how much or have you provide a pre-paid envelope). Many courts will attempt certified mail first for in-state addresses because it’s cheaper than sheriff. 
- Private server: you pay them directly whatever they charge; you’ll need to give them the filed papers. Usually done if the sheriff was unsuccessful or timing issues.

**Filing by Mail Costs:** If you file by mail, include the $10 (and perhaps a self-addressed stamped envelope for the clerk to mail back your filed copies and summons). You’ll then be responsible for arranging service – sometimes the clerk can still forward to the sheriff if you included service instructions and fees.

**Multiple Defendants:** If you’re suing two or more defendants, the filing fee is still $10 total (one case). *However*, you will incur separate service fees for each defendant served (the sheriff charges per person). So if you sue two people, and a sheriff serves both, you’ll pay two service fees. The court’s FAQ explicitly notes: “If you are suing two people in the same case, you will pay separately for service for each Defendant.” ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Court%20Directory)). So plan on that (but you still just pay one $10 filing fee).

**Additional Fee (if any) for Judgment Enforcement:** Not at initial filing, but be aware down the road: if you win and need to do things like a **garnishment** or **judgment lien filing**, those have their own fees (e.g. obtaining an abstract of judgment costs $5 ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=,00%29%20fee)); filing a garnishment might require a new filing fee or sheriff fee for serving the garnishment). Those are post-judgment and covered in section 12. They aren’t front-loaded, but you might eventually pay them (and in many cases can add those to the amount you collect from defendant).

In summary, **$10 to file** is all it takes to start a Wyoming small claim ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=,cost%20to%20file)). Budget additionally for maybe ~$30-$50 for serving the defendant. If money is very tight, ask about waiving the fees. Always save your receipts to recover costs if you win. Wyoming’s low fees are designed to keep the courthouse open to everyone.

## 5. Statute of limitations

The **statute of limitations** is the legal deadline to file your case. If you file after the applicable time period has passed, the defendant can move to dismiss the case as “time-barred.” Wyoming’s limitation periods are set by statute (primarily Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105 for most civil actions ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)), plus some specific statutes for certain claims). Below is a breakdown by type of claim, with the most relevant time limits for small claims scenarios. (All periods start from the date the claim “accrues” – generally when the injury or breach happened or when it was discovered, as explained.)

**Important:** If you are close to a deadline, file as soon as possible. If you’re unsure which limit applies, err on the shorter side or consult an attorney. Also note that certain events can “toll” (pause) the limitations clock – for example, if the defendant was a minor or out of state for a time, or if the harm was not reasonably discoverable right away (discovery rule). Wyoming recognizes tolling for minority/disability ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/state/wyoming-law/wyoming-civil-statute-of-limitations-laws.html#:~:text=In%20the%20state%20of%20Wyoming%2C,statute%20runs%20from%20that%20date)) and a “discovery rule” mainly in cases like fraud or malpractice where the injury is hidden ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/state/wyoming-law/wyoming-civil-statute-of-limitations-laws.html#:~:text=The%20discovery%20rule%20means%20the,the%20discovery%20of%20the%20injury)). I’ll note where relevant.

Here’s a **table of common claim types and limitation periods in Wyoming**:

| **Type of Claim**                   | **Limitations Period**        | **Wyoming Statute**                 |
|-------------------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Written Contract** (signed agreement, promissory note, written lease) | 10 years from breach or due date ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(i) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)). |
| **Oral Contract** (or implied-in-fact contract with no writing) | 8 years from breach ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(ii)(A) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). |
| **Contract - UCC Sale of Goods** (e.g. sale of a product, under Uniform Commercial Code) | 4 years from breach (delivery of goods or when defect discovered) ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/state/wyoming-law/wyoming-civil-statute-of-limitations-laws.html#:~:text=,personal%20property%20damage%2C%20product%20liability)). *This is a specific UCC provision.* | Wyo. Stat. § 34.1-2-725 (UCC 2-725). |
| **Open Account** (running account with no defined final payment date, e.g. credit card, ongoing credit line) | 8 years if viewed as implied contract ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). *However, each charge may be its own contract – often treated as 8 years from last charge or last payment.* | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(ii)(A) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). |
| **Debt on Account Stated** (a finalized balance acknowledged in writing) | 10 years (if a written acknowledgment of debt) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(i). |
| **Promissory Note (written)**       | 10 years from default ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)). | § 1-3-105(a)(i). |
| **Judgment (enforcing a judgment)** | *Dormancy 5 years if no execution* (then revive within 10 years of dormancy) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2022/title-1/chapter-17/article-3/section-1-17-307/#:~:text=1)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-5/section-1-16-503/#:~:text=,to%20bring%20the%20action%20was)). (Effectively can enforce up to 15 years or more by renewal, see below). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-17-307 (5-year dormancy) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2022/title-1/chapter-17/article-3/section-1-17-307/#:~:text=If%20execution%20on%20a%20judgment,estate%20of%20the%20judgment%20debtor)); § 1-16-503 (revivor within 10 years of dormancy) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-5/section-1-16-503/#:~:text=,to%20bring%20the%20action%20was)). |
| **Personal Injury** (general, from negligence: car accident, slip & fall, etc.) | 4 years from injury or discovery ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,and%20not%20herein%20enumerated%3B%20and)). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(iv)(C) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,and%20not%20herein%20enumerated%3B%20and)). |
| **Medical Malpractice** (injury by healthcare provider) | 2 years from the date of the alleged act *or* 2 years from discovery of the act, but no more than 4 years after the act (statute of repose), except for foreign object cases ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/state/wyoming-law/wyoming-civil-statute-of-limitations-laws.html#:~:text=,personal%20property%20damage%2C%20product%20liability)). Also must file with Medical Review Panel first (a procedure outside SOL). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-107 (and § 9-2-1518 et seq. for panel). |
| **Wrongful Death**                 | 2 years from date of death ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/state/wyoming-law/wyoming-civil-statute-of-limitations-laws.html#:~:text=,personal%20property%20damage%2C%20product%20liability)). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-38-102 (by case law, 2-year limit). |
| **Property Damage** (to personal property or real property) | 4 years from damage ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)). (Trespass to land and injury to chattel are explicitly 4). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(iv)(A), (B) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)). |
| **Fraud** (including false representation, concealment) | 4 years from when fraud was or should have been discovered ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,and%20not%20herein%20enumerated%3B%20and)). (Fraud accrual is upon discovery by rule). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(iv)(D) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,and%20not%20herein%20enumerated%3B%20and)) and discovery rule ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/state/wyoming-law/wyoming-civil-statute-of-limitations-laws.html#:~:text=The%20discovery%20rule%20means%20the,the%20discovery%20of%20the%20injury)). |
| **Libel/Slander (Defamation)**     | 1 year from publication ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(v)(A) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)). |
| **Assault or Battery (civil)**    | 1 year from the act ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(v)(B) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)). |
| **False Imprisonment / Malicious Prosecution** | 1 year from incident end ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(v)(C) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)). |
| **Statutory Penalty or Forfeiture** (where statute imposes a penalty) | 1 year unless the statute says otherwise ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,including%20sexual%20assault)). | Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105(a)(v)(D) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,including%20sexual%20assault)). |
| **Debt Collection (for creditor to sue)** | 8 years oral / 10 years written, depending on contract type as above. E.g. credit card: arguably written if signed app, so 10; if no signed writing, 8. | § 1-3-105 as above. |
| **Judgments from Other States (“foreign judgment”)** | You can domesticate anytime, but once domesticated, treat as a WY judgment. Practically, if older than 5 years, may need to revive first in original state. | (Uniform Enforcement of Foreign Judgments Act – not a SOL but dormancy may apply). |
| **Security Deposit Recovery** (tenant suing landlord) | Arguably an “injury to rights not on contract” – 4 years ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,and%20not%20herein%20enumerated%3B%20and)). Safer to not wait that long; some might argue it’s written contract (lease) 10 years if lease was written. But since deposit return is statutory duty, 4 years is likely. | § 1-3-105(a)(iv)(C). (No specific shorter period in deposit statute). |
| **Unpaid Wages (no written contract)** | 8 years as implied contract ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). (Though one should never wait that long; wage claims often pursued within months by labor standards). |
| **Consumer Protection Act claim** (private UDAP) | 1 to 4 years? Not explicitly stated in CPA. Likely viewed as an action upon a liability created by statute (if not a penalty) – which is 8 years ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,either%20express%20or%20implied%3B%20or)), *or* as an injury to rights not arising on contract – 4 years ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,and%20not%20herein%20enumerated%3B%20and)). Courts might apply 4-year catchall for tort-like claims. Best to assume 4. | Possibly § 1-3-105(a)(iv)(C) (4 years). |
| **Open account between merchants (goods)** | 4 years via UCC if goods; 8 if services. | As above. |

And so on. The above covers most small-claims scenarios.

To summarize key ones:
- Written contracts: **10 years** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)). This is generous – most states are shorter. So in Wyoming you have up to a decade to sue on a written loan agreement, promissory note, written service contract, etc. (Interest might stop accruing earlier by law or contract, but the claim remains alive.) E.g. if someone signed a promissory note in 2015 due in 2016, you technically have until 2026 to file – but don’t cut it that close!
- Oral contracts: **8 years** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action)). That’s a long time for an oral promise – evidence gets stale. Still, legally you have up to 8 years from breach. If someone orally agreed in 2018 to repay by 2020 and didn’t, you likely have until 2028. Wyoming’s 8-year is quite high (many states are 3 or 4 for oral).
- Injury or property damage: **4 years** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)). So, car accidents, damage to your stuff, trespass, etc., all 4 years. If your car was hit in 2019, you have until 2023 to sue for damages. (Wrongful death is 2, but those tend to exceed small claims anyway.)
- Fraud: **4 years from when you discovered it** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,and%20not%20herein%20enumerated%3B%20and)). So if you only realized in 2020 that something sold to you in 2017 was fraudulent, you’d have until 2024. Don’t delay once discovered.
- Defamation: **1 year** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)) – very short. If someone slandered you, you have one year. These rarely go to small claims (also defamation often not a money claim that fits small claims easily).
- Assault/battery (civil): **1 year** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)). If you want to sue your assailant for medical bills, do it within a year. (Often it’s handled via criminal restitution or so, but civil suit option exists.)
- Against Government: If suing under the Governmental Claims Act (like a negligence by a government employee), you must present notice within 2 years of the incident and then file within 1 year of claim denial (effectively, it’s complicated – but just know government claims have separate rules). Also small claims may not handle those as discussed.

**Tolling and Extensions:** Wyoming tolls for minors until age 18 ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/state/wyoming-law/wyoming-civil-statute-of-limitations-laws.html#:~:text=Tolling%20and%20the%20Discovery%20Rule)). So if a minor had a claim, the clock starts at 18. Also tolls for legal incompetency (like mental disability) during the disability. If defendant is out of state, Wyoming historically had a tolling statute (1-3-116) that tolls while defendant not in Wyoming and can’t be served. But with modern long-arm jurisdiction, that tolling may not apply if they can be served out of state. (The law says absence from state tolls except for periods the person is subject to service under Wyoming law. Since nonresidents can be served by Wyoming’s long-arm in many cases, tolling might not apply unless they conceal themselves.)

**Discovery Rule:** Particularly for fraud and injury cases where harm is hidden, Wyoming’s rule is that the statute doesn’t start until you knew or reasonably should have known of the problem ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/state/wyoming-law/wyoming-civil-statute-of-limitations-laws.html#:~:text=The%20discovery%20rule%20means%20the,the%20discovery%20of%20the%20injury)). E.g. in professional malpractice (beyond small claims scope typically), the discovery rule explicitly applies. In a small claim scenario, say a home repair defect that you only discovered 3 years later – you could argue the breach or injury was not apparent until discovery, thus limitations from discovery. It’s case-specific.

**Effect of Limitations in Court:** The statute of limitations is an **affirmative defense** – meaning if you file late, the defendant must raise it to defeat the claim. The judge won’t automatically check dates and toss it; the defendant has to say “this claim is time-barred under X statute.” In small claims, a savvy defendant will do so if applicable. If it’s clearly expired, the judge will dismiss. If there’s doubt (e.g. discovery rule issues), the judge will hear arguments on it. Frankly, if something is way outside the limit (like suing on a 15-year-old debt), expect a dismissal.

**Renewal of Debt:** Note that if a debtor makes a **partial payment** or a **written acknowledgment** of the debt after default, it can restart the clock on a contract in Wyoming (common law). So, e.g., on an oral debt from 9 years ago, if 5 years ago they sent you an email admitting they owe or paid $50, that could reset the 8-year from that date. This can be argued to extend the time.

**Statute of Repose:** For some claims (not typically small claims amounts), like construction defects on improvements to real property, Wyoming has a 10-year statute of repose regardless of discovery (W.S. 1-3-111). Usually too high for small claims though. Just be aware if you're suing over something like a building defect discovered 9 years later, might still be under 10-year repose.

**Summary for common small-claims scenarios:**
- Unpaid rent or security deposit: If written lease, probably 10 years, but practically you’d act within a year or two. If oral (month-to-month), 8 years max, but again you’d rarely wait.
- Unpaid wages: 8 years (implied contract), but typically you’d address within months or go to labor.
- Fraudulent sale of a used car: 4 years from discovery – often the “discovery” is when you find out the defect, usually fairly soon, so either 4 years from purchase or from when a hidden defect came to light.
- Collection of a personal loan (with IOU written): 10 years from breach (if due on a date or from last payment made). If no specified timeframe, arguably demand needed then 10 from demand; if open-ended, there's some nuance, but likely 10 from when you reasonably expected repayment.
- Damage to personal property (e.g. neighbor broke your fence): 4 years.
- Minor car accident property damage: 4 years.
- Injury from car accident: 4 years.
- NSF check claim: The underlying obligation (the check) is written instrument, 10 years, but practically you’d use the statutory triple damages which doesn’t specify a separate SOL, so default to underlying debt – likely 10 or possibly 8 if viewed not as written contract. To be safe, treat it as written contract (the check is written promise) so 10, but one would never wait since easier to collect near incident time.

**If Statute Is Close or Past:** If you realize you waited too long, the claim is likely lost. If it’s borderline, you might still file and see if defendant raises it. For example, if an oral contract breach happened 8 years and 2 months ago, and you file – the defendant might not know to assert the defense and could default, giving you a judgment. However, this is risky and arguably unethical to knowingly pursue a time-barred claim. But small claims being informal, sometimes defendants don’t respond and default even on old debts. Default judgments can be set aside if the defendant later proves it was time-barred and they had a valid defense they didn’t present, but it’s discretionary. It’s better to not knowingly file stale claims.

**Recommendation:** Always file as soon as you can. Don’t use the full length if avoidable. Memories fade, evidence can be lost, and even though Wyoming’s limits are generous in many categories, a prompt filing makes your case stronger.

*(Statutes: Wyo. Stat. § 1-3-105 lists most civil limitations ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)); § 1-3-107 for med mal; others as noted above.)*

## 6. Pre-filing requirements

Before you file your small claim in Wyoming, check if there are any steps you **must** or **should** take. Wyoming’s small claims statutes and rules have a couple of specific requirements, and certain types of claims (especially against government or under particular statutes) have notice requirements. Generally:

- **Demand Letter / Prior Notice:** Wyoming law actually requires the plaintiff to have made a **demand for payment** on the defendant and been refused before filing the small claim. When you fill out the Small Claims Affidavit, you must swear that “demand has been made and payment refused” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)). This is a statutory prerequisite under W.S. 1-21-202(a) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)). So, **yes, you need to ask for your money and be turned down (or ignored)** before you sue. This demand need not be a formal letter (a verbal demand or a billing that was not paid can suffice), but it’s **highly advisable** to make a clear written demand and give a reasonable time (e.g. 10 or 15 days) to pay up. Why? It not only satisfies the requirement, but a polite demand letter might prompt payment without court. Also, if it ends up in court, you can show the judge you tried to resolve it first. 
    - **What to include in a demand:** State the amount owed, what for, a deadline to pay (or respond), and that you will take legal action (small claims) if not resolved. Keep a copy. You can send it via certified mail to have proof of receipt, or at least by regular mail or email so you can show it was sent. There’s no statutory notice period (unlike some states that require, say, 10 days for bad checks or triple damages, etc., though Wyoming’s bad check law does specify a written demand 30 days before suing for treble ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,provided%20for%20in%20this%20section)) – follow that for NSF checks!). In general, giving around 10 days is fair.
    - In the small claims Affidavit form, you will be signing an affidavit that includes a statement like "the amount due and that demand for payment has been made and refused" ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)). The judge may ask at hearing, “did you ask the defendant to pay before coming here?” You want to be able to say yes and even provide a copy of your demand letter or recount the conversation.
    - If you haven’t made a demand yet, you technically should do so **before filing**. If you file without doing it, you’ve filed a potentially false affidavit. The case might be dismissed or just proceed if not contested, but it’s not advisable. This requirement is in the law to encourage out-of-court resolution.
    - **Exceptions:** In certain situations, making a demand might be futile or even dangerous (e.g. if the opposing party has been violent). But even then, a simple written request usually won’t hurt your case; if you truly cannot safely contact them, you might explain that to the court. However, generally for money disputes, a demand is expected.
- **Time to Cure (for specific statutes):** Some claims require giving the other party a chance to fix the problem first:
    - For example, as mentioned, a **Consumer Protection Act** claim (deceptive trade practice) typically requires that the business be given an opportunity to cure the deceptive practice (stop the practice, refund, etc.) before you sue ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-40/chapter-12/article-1/section-40-12-108/#:~:text=,such%20unlawful%20deceptive%20trade%20practice)). This is not explicitly spelt out in the statute text we have, but the phrase “uncured unlawful deceptive trade practice” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-40/chapter-12/article-1/section-40-12-108/#:~:text=,such%20unlawful%20deceptive%20trade%20practice)) implies you should notify the business of the issue and allow them to remedy it. Hence, if you’re suing under the CPA, definitely send a written demand letter describing the violation and allow a reasonable time (the law doesn’t set a days count, but 10-30 days is common), and note that they failed to cure.
    - **Bad Checks:** Wyoming’s bad check statute explicitly requires a **30-day written demand** by **certified mail (certificate of mailing)** to the check writer at the address on the check or last known address ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,provided%20for%20in%20this%20section)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,00%29%2C%20and%20court%20costs)). Only if they don’t pay within 30 days can you sue for treble damages. So for NSF checks, a demand letter (with correct language per statute) is absolutely required. If you didn’t do it, file small claims just for the face amount and fee (not treble) or wait and send the letter then sue after 30 days for the triple. The letter needs to state that they must pay the value plus $30 fee within 30 days or face liability for triple the amount, etc. ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,provided%20for%20in%20this%20section)). If you intend to seek the treble damages, ensure you did this step.
    - **Security Deposits:** Wyoming law doesn’t require tenants to send a demand to the landlord for the deposit; instead the burden is on the landlord to send itemization/refund. However, if the landlord doesn’t comply, a polite written inquiry from the tenant (“It’s been 30 days, where is my deposit accounting?”) could help as evidence of reasonableness. Not required though.
    - **Landlord’s duty to cure defects:** There’s no statutory “notice to landlord then sue” requirement beyond normal deposit and habitability. But practically, if you are a tenant claiming damages for, say, lack of repairs, you should have notified the landlord of the issue and given them a chance to fix (document that). If you didn’t, the landlord will say “I was never even told of this problem,” and you might lose because you didn’t give opportunity to cure, which leases often require. So while not an official condition to sue, notice to the other side is generally good practice.
- **Mandatory Mediation:** Wyoming does not mandate mediation or any alternative dispute resolution for small claims statewide. Some states have small claims mediation requirements on the day of trial or before – Wyoming’s rules don’t specify that. However, a particular judge might encourage parties to settle or even suggest mediation if available. There’s no pre-filing mediation requirement. Equal Justice Wyoming and some community dispute resolution services exist, but they are voluntary. So you do not need to mediate or arbitrate before filing, except if your specific dispute is covered by a contract with an **arbitration clause**.
    - **Arbitration Clauses:** If the contract you’re suing on says disputes must be arbitrated (e.g. many credit card agreements, gym contracts, cell phone contracts have them), the defendant can ask the court to compel arbitration instead of court. In small claims, sometimes companies ignore those clauses and just litigate, but they have a right to enforce them. So be aware: if you signed an agreement to arbitrate, the defendant might move to dismiss due to that. There’s no pre-filing requirement that you attempt arbitration unless the contract or law requires it. But if you know about such a clause, perhaps try to use the contract’s dispute process (like often credit card disputes go through arbitration or an internal claim process) before small claims. If the other side doesn’t raise it, the small claims court could still proceed. But if they do raise it, the judge likely will dismiss or stay the small claim and direct you to arbitration. It might not come up often in small claims for consumer debts, ironically since often those are initiated by the business who might prefer small claims over arbitration cost. If you are the plaintiff consumer wanting to avoid arbitration, sometimes the business will waive arbitration just by participating in small claims. 
- **Special Notice to Government:** If you have a claim against a city, county, or the State of Wyoming, the **Wyoming Governmental Claims Act** requires you to serve a formal written notice of your claim to the government (and its insurance dept) within **2 years** of the event (and for some, 1 year) *before* you can sue ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Enforcing%20a%20Money%20Judgment%20,Sometimes%2C%20the)). Then if the government denies or doesn’t satisfy the claim in 90 days, you have a window to file suit (1 year after denial). *And*, notably, suits under that Act (when allowed) must be in district court if over $7,500. If $6,000 <= claim <= $7,500, there’s a grey area – the Act says district court for claims over $7,500, implying possibly under that could go to circuit court. But given the complexities, I would treat any Government claim as out of small claims. In any case, **serving the notice is mandatory**. If you fail to do so timely, you lose the right to sue. So if your small claim is against a public entity (say a city damaged your fence), you must send the statutory notice to the city clerk or as required (usually written notice with time, place, circumstances, and amount of compensation demanded). Because of the short timeline (often 2 years), check those rules promptly. This notice is a separate thing from a demand letter (though your notice can also demand payment). 
    - Also, for suits against governments, even if you do the notice, small claims jurisdiction might not cover it (the Act’s language about >$7,500 to district implies <= $7,500 could be circuit, but arguably any official governmental tort claim should be in district).
- **Affidavit Specifics:** The small claims process itself requires you to file an **Affidavit of Claim** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)). By signing it, you’re swearing to the claim’s validity, the demand made, etc. There’s no need to notarize separately if you’re signing before the clerk because it’s done under oath as part of the filing.
- **If You’re a Business (Plaintiff):** No special pre-filing, but ensure if you’re an out-of-state company suing, you are properly registered to do business in Wyoming if required. An unregistered foreign company might face a defense that it can’t maintain an action until registered (Wyo. Stat. §17-16-1502). Usually debt collection suits by out-of-state agencies in WY require them to be registered as a foreign business. Registration can often be done, or if your business activity was solely interstate, you might fall under exception. Check with an attorney if that applies. It’s not strictly a small claims rule but general corporate compliance – it can be raised as a bar to suing. The fix is usually to register and then continue the suit (courts sometimes stay the case to allow that).
- **If The Claim Involves a Professional License or Bond:** Some claims, like against contractors, might have a bond or license requirement. Wyoming doesn’t require general contractors to be bonded (except some specific trades), but if you were dealing with, say, a bonded outfitter, you might notify the bonding company. Not required to file suit, but could help you recover without court if bond pays. For small claims court itself, no.
- **Optional: Attempt Settlement First:** Not legally required except as above with demand. But practically, try to resolve amicably. Often a phone call or letter can avoid court. Judges appreciate seeing that you tried. If you reach a partial agreement, put it in writing. If you can’t agree on the whole thing, small claims is there.
- **Evidence Preparation (Pre-filing):** Not a requirement, but before filing ensure you have the documents needed. E.g., for a breach of contract, have the contract copy. For a car accident, get the repair estimates, crash report, etc. It’s wise to gather these early. Also have defendant’s address (required for filing). If you only know a work address or PO Box, consider how you will have them served.
- **Multiple Defendants Consideration:** If multiple people are responsible, you should include them all in one small claim (if within same transaction). E.g. if a married couple both signed a contract, sue both. There’s no rule forcing it, but you cannot recover more than once for same loss. It avoids them pointing fingers at each other. Pre-filing, get all names and addresses.
- **Check For Special Jurisdiction:** Very rarely, a claim might fall under exclusive federal or other jurisdiction (e.g. a claim under federal law where state courts don’t have jurisdiction – like certain federal statutes). If your claim is something unusual (like suing an airline under federal aviation rules for bumping – likely preempted, or suing a bank for something involving federal banking law), check if state court can hear it. Most everyday stuff is fine in state court. But e.g. claims under federal patent/trademark must be in federal court. Pre-filing, ensure your case is of a type state courts handle (almost all normal contracts/torts are).
- **If You Are Suing an Estate:** If the person who owes you died, you can’t do small claims; you must file a creditor claim in probate. So pre-filing, if you learn the debtor is deceased with an open estate, use probate. Small claims won’t circumvent that – they’d get dismissed because the estate must be handled in probate court.
- **If The Defendant is in Bankruptcy:** As mentioned, an automatic bankruptcy stay stops you from filing or continuing small claims. So if you know the person filed Chapter 7 or 13 and the debt was from before that, you cannot proceed (and likely the debt will be discharged). *Pre-filing, always ask:* did the person hint at bankruptcy? Did you receive a notice? If so, consult an attorney before suing. If they filed after you filed but before hearing, the case will be stayed; you’d then need to dismiss if it’s dischargeable. In summary, bankruptcy = do not file small claim (or if already filed, pause).
- **Work Comp:** If you were an employee injured at work and attempt to sue employer for injuries, note you usually cannot (workers’ comp is exclusive). Don’t file that – it’ll be dismissed. Pre-filing, consider if the law bars the claim in regular court (some matters are non-suable as civil claims).
- **Medical Malpractice Pre-Filing Panel:** Wyoming has a required **Medical Review Panel** for med mal claims (W.S. 9-2-1518). You can’t directly file a malpractice suit in any court without going through that panel and obtaining a disposition (or time out period). While most malpractices exceed $6k, even if you had a small med mal claim (say $5k in unnecessary bills from a dentist’s mistake), theoretically you’d need to file with the panel first. So small claims is not a shortcut around that; you must comply with that pre-filing requirement. That being said, practically no one does med mal in small claims due to complexity and need for expert testimony. But it's a legal requirement to be aware of in case.
- **Legal Malpractice or Other Professional Negligence Pre-filing:** Wyoming doesn’t require a panel for legal malpractice, etc., so you could file those in small claims if damages small, but again, proving it might require expert – not suitable for small claims.

In conclusion, the **main pre-filing requirement in Wyoming small claims is to make a demand for payment and have it refused** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)). Additionally, adhere to any special notices for your type of claim (e.g. NSF check 30-day letter ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,provided%20for%20in%20this%20section)), government claim notice, etc.). Doing these steps will ensure your case isn’t thrown out on a technicality and shows the court you acted in good faith. If you’re unsure, it’s wise to call the clerk and ask “Is there anything I need to do before filing a small claim on X?” They might remind you of the general demand requirement or specific ones like the bad check letter. 

*(Authority: W.S. 1-21-202(a) requiring affidavit stating demand made ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)); W.S. 1-1-115 bad check demand ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,provided%20for%20in%20this%20section)); W.S. 1-39-113 et seq. (Gov’t claims notice); Small Claims Rule 5 (no formal answer needed, but implies attempt resolution).)*

## 7. Forms required

To start a small claims case in Wyoming, you will need to fill out and file certain **forms**, primarily:
- the **Small Claims Affidavit** (complaint), 
- and the **Small Claims Summons**. 

After filing, you’ll also need a **Proof of Service** form once the defendant is served. If the defendant is served and doesn’t respond, you might need a default judgment form, and if it goes to hearing, a Judgment form. Each of these has a standardized version provided by the Wyoming courts.

Fortunately, Wyoming’s Judicial Branch provides a suite of standardized small claims forms (with “SC” form numbers) that are accepted in all counties ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=Name%20%20,718)). Here are the key forms and their form numbers (as of current Judicial Branch listings):
