- **SC-01 – Small Claims Affidavit.** This is the **complaint form**. It’s a sworn statement by the plaintiff stating the names and addresses of the parties, the nature of the claim, the amount due, and that demand for payment was made and refused ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)). You must fill this out with detail about what happened and how much money you seek. It has a signature line where you sign under oath (in front of a notary or the clerk) that the facts are true. This effectively initiates the case. *(If multiple plaintiffs, they can all be listed on one form. If multiple defendants, list all their names and addresses.)* **Where to get it:** The Circuit Court clerk can give you a blank or you can download it from the Wyoming Judicial Branch Self-Help website ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2001,Filling%20Guide)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2001,722)). It’s available as a PDF or Word doc (as noted) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=Name%20%20,718)). Use the official format to ensure you include all required elements. 
- **SC-01a – Instructions for Small Claims Affidavit.** This is not filed; it’s an instruction sheet the courts provide to help you fill out SC-01. It’s basically a how-to. Definitely read it if you’re doing it yourself (likely posted online  ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2001,Download)) or given by clerk). 
- **SC-02 – Small Claims Summons.** This is the **summons** form the court will issue after you file the affidavit. The summons is directed to the defendant(s) and commands them to appear at the hearing on a given date/time. It also notes that if they don’t appear, judgment may be entered by default. The clerk typically prepares this, but sometimes the plaintiff might be asked to fill in names/addresses and the clerk signs and dates it. On SC-02, you’ll fill in parties’ names, the address of the court, and leave the appearance date blank for the clerk to set (though some forms have a line for you to request a date). The clerk will then sign (or stamp) it and assign a hearing date. **Where to get it:** Clerk will have it, but it’s also available to download as SC-02 PDF/Word ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2001,722)). 
- **SC-03 – Return of Service (Non-Continuing Garnishment)**: Actually, there are two SC-03s listed: one related to garnishment and possibly one for normal service. The Self-Help list shows SC-03 is used in both garnishment context and likely as a generic “Return of Service” for small claims documents ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=Download%20%20SC%2002,Download)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=03%20Order%20for%20Service%20Continuing,20)). Usually, the sheriff or process server will complete a Proof/Return of Service indicating when and how the defendant was served the summons and affidavit. The form SC-03 likely has blanks for case number, name, person served, method, date, server’s signature. The server will file this with the court to prove the defendant got notice. **Where to get:** The clerk often provides a Return of Service form to whoever is serving, or the sheriff has their own. SC-03 is downloadable (as part of garnishment forms) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=03%20Order%20for%20Service%20Continuing,20)) or ask clerk for a generic proof of service.
- **SC-04 – Small Claims Affidavit of Service.** There appears to be SC-04 designated for “Affidavit of Service” ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=match%20at%20L903%20SC%2004,Download)), which might be a more plain form for the person who served to fill out under oath if not an official officer. For example, if a private adult (not a sheriff) serves the papers (Wyoming allows anyone 18+ not a party to serve), they should sign an affidavit of service detailing service. SC-04 likely accommodates that. It’s basically another version of proof of service.
- **SC-05 – Instructions for Summons and Service.** This likely explains how to get the defendant served, for the plaintiff’s knowledge. Not filed, just given to you. 
- **SC-06 – Judgment and Order.** This is the **judgment form** the judge will sign if you win (or whatever the outcome). It will list who appeared, findings, and the amount awarded, etc. Sometimes the judge or clerk prepares it at hearing, but it’s wise for you to have a blank copy ready. In some courts, after a default or hearing, the prevailing party might be asked to fill out the proposed judgment for the judge to sign. SC-06 covers that – it’s a standard template for the final judgment (which doubles as an order since it’s signed by a judge). It includes any payment plan terms or post-judgment interest/costs. **Where to get:** Provided online ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2003,722)). 
- **SC-07 – Understanding a Judgment and Order.** This is likely an info sheet given to the losing party explaining what a judgment means and possibly how to pay or appeal. Not a form to file.
- **Counterclaim form:** Wyoming doesn’t list a separate SC form number for a counterclaim, because the defendant can essentially file their own Small Claims Affidavit labeling it a counterclaim. The Judicial Branch’s FAQ snippet we saw suggests contacting clerk for counterclaim process ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/litigation/going-to-court/wyoming-small-claims-courts.html#:~:text=a%C2%A0counterclaim,want%20to%20file%20a%20counterclaim)). In practice, the defendant would go to the clerk, say “I want to counterclaim,” and likely they’ll fill out an SC-01 with roles reversed. The court might have a stamp or notation “Counterclaim of Defendant” on it.
- **Continuance or Motion forms:** Typically small claims is informal enough that if you need a continuance, you could request by letter or at the clerk. There isn’t a standard form listed for motions. If needed, you could write a simple request or use a generic motion form if clerk has one. But none listed in SC forms means they expect you to just request in writing or in person.
- **Appeal Notice:** If a party wants to appeal to District Court, they will file a Notice of Appeal. The SC rules might not have a numbered form for that, often the clerk has a generic form or you write one including case info and statement of appealing the judgment. The appeals are governed by Wyoming Rules of Appellate Procedure (modified for lower court to district appeals). Not in SC list, possibly because it’s not part of initial case, but the clerk can supply a Notice of Appeal form if needed.
- **Garnishment and Enforcement Forms:** If you win and want to garnish wages or bank accounts, there are specific forms (the list in [94] shows continuing (wage) garnishment forms 1-8, and non-continuing (bank levy) garnishment forms 1-8). These are beyond initial case but they exist:
    - “Request for Writ of Garnishment” (for one-time or continuing),
    - the actual “Writ of Garnishment” (which the clerk/judge issues),
    - “Order for Service” (instructing how to serve garnishee),
    - “Return of Service – Garnishment”,
    - “Notice of Right to Hearing and Claim of Exemption” (must be given to the debtor),
    - “Answer to Writ of Garnishment” (for garnishee to fill out how much they hold),
    - “Order on Hearing” (if debtor objects and hearing held),
    - “Privacy Notice” (since garnishments involve SSNs or personal data).
    - These are numbered GA-xx or similar in some states, but Wyoming appended them to SC form list as well ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=)). Actually, in [94], we see the list under “Non-continuing Garnishment Forms” with numbering 01 through 08 for that. So likely:
        - SC (or GN) 01: Request for Writ (Non-Cont),
        - SC 02: Writ of Non-Cont,
        - SC 03: Order for Service Non-Cont,
        - SC 04: Return of Service Non-Cont,
        - SC 05: Notice of Right to Hearing,
        - SC 06: Garnishee’s Answer Non-Cont,
        - SC 07: Order on Hearing,
        - SC 08: Privacy letter.
      And similarly a set for continuing (wage) garnishment (though [94] doesn’t list continuing forms, [94] L53-L61 shows numbers 03 and 04 for continuing garn but that might be mis-indexing).
    - These garnishment forms would come into play after you have a judgment (see Section 12). The Judicial Branch has them on the self-help page under “Garnishment Forms” accessible via that guide link ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Go%20to%20the%20Self,%28Click%20on%20Garnishment%20Forms)) or by filtering on the site.
- **Fee Waiver form:** If you need to file In Forma Pauperis, the site doesn’t explicitly list an SC form number for motion to proceed IFP. Usually, there is a generic form used in all civil cases titled “Affidavit of Indigency” or “Motion to Proceed Without Fees”. The clerk can provide it. If not, you can draft a simple affidavit citing Wyo. Stat. § 5-3-111 (which allows waiver of fees for indigents) and listing your income, assets, and that you can’t afford the costs. You’d sign it and perhaps present to a judge for approval. Not an SC-coded form but available on the site possibly under general forms.
- **Service by Publication form:** Rarely needed in small claims (because small claims requires personal service not pub, generally). But if you cannot find the defendant, you technically could ask the court for permission to serve by publication (like in a newspaper). There’s no specialized small claims form for that; you’d use a motion and a supporting affidavit showing you did due diligence to locate them. That’s more complex and not common in small claims (most people find an address or drop the case if can’t find the person because even if you get a default via publication, collecting is near impossible since you don’t know where they are).
- **County-specific forms or differences:** Wyoming uses statewide forms. There should be no significant difference by county. The formatting is uniform. One county might provide an instructional packet unique to them, but largely they all rely on the standard forms from the Supreme Court’s approved set. For example, Teton County’s site might have a local Q&A, but the actual forms are the same SC-01, SC-02 etc. So you can confidently use the forms from the state website in any Circuit Court.
- **Where to find forms online:** The Wyoming Judicial Branch website (courts.state.wy.us or specifically wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms) has a section for “Small Claims” forms ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2001,Filling%20Guide)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=The%20links%20in%20this%20section,them%20out%20using%20a%20pen)). They have them in PDF and Word. Also, they offer a “Form-Filling Guide” which is an online interview (through a platform called *Wyoming Interactive Court Forms*) to help you generate completed forms by answering questions ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=)). For example, click “SC 01 Form-Filling Guide” and it will walk you through inputting your info and produce a filled affidavit and summons that you can print. This is very handy if you’re unsure about how to fill it manually.
- **Filling out the Affidavit (SC-01):** Key details to include:
    - Your full name and address (as Plaintiff).
    - Defendant’s full name and street address (for service). Include as much as known (DOB or work info not required, but address is necessary).
    - Exact amount you are claiming, not exceeding $6,000, and whether you also seek court costs and maybe interest (some forms have a blank for amount “plus court costs and interest as allowed” – you can write that).
    - A brief statement of the claim (e.g., “Defendant failed to repay a loan made on [date] in the amount of $X” or “Defendant breached a contract to repair Plaintiff’s roof on [date] causing Plaintiff $Y in losses”, or “Defendant negligently drove a vehicle on [date] causing $Z damage to Plaintiff’s car”).
    - Date and sign in front of clerk or notary – it’s an affidavit so it must be notarized or administered.
    - There is language saying “demand for payment has been made and refused” – by signing you assert that is true ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)). Ensure you have done that as per Section 6.
    - Usually, the clerk will fill in the case number and hearing info after you file.
- **Summons (SC-02):** Usually, just list parties and addresses. The court clerk will assign the hearing date/time and location and include it on the summons. Many Circuit Courts set small claims hearings about 3-6 weeks out from filing, and importantly must allow time for service not less than 3 days before hearing and not more than 12 days ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Important%21%20The%20court%20date%20must,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)). The clerk knows to schedule accordingly. If there is a day of week small claims are heard, they’ll set it to the next available such day that fits service window. You can likely request scheduling preferences if you have conflicts, but final date is at clerk’s discretion. They often choose a **morning or afternoon docket** where multiple small claims might be called sequentially.

**After Filing:** The clerk will issue the Summons and give it (and copies of your Affidavit) to you for service. If you use the Sheriff, they might send those to the sheriff internally or hand them to you to deliver to sheriff. If you are using certified mail by clerk, they will mail and then docket if the green card comes back. They may also give you a copy for your records. Keep at least one copy of everything you file.

**Forms for Defendant’s use:** If the defendant wants to file a written counterclaim or answer, as noted they can fill out an Affidavit for counterclaim. Usually no formal answer is required (Rule 5 says no responsive pleading required ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%205))). If a defendant wants to show up and defend orally, they can do so without filing anything (just appear at hearing). The Summons might have a tear-off “NOTICE: you may appear and defend at the hearing, or prior to the hearing you may contact the court to request change of date or to file a counterclaim if you have one…”. So the onus is on the defendant to show up; they aren’t required to answer in writing.

**To Summarize the Minimum Forms to start:**
1. **Small Claims Affidavit (SC-01)** – the complaint. 
2. **Small Claims Summons (SC-02)** – issued by clerk. 
3. Provide copies of both for each defendant (original to court file, plus one set per defendant for service, plus one for your records).
4. **Proof of Service (SC-04 or SC-03)** – to be filed after service done, by whoever served.

**Optional helpful forms:**
- Bring a **military service affidavit** if you are going for default judgment and the defendant isn’t present. Under federal law (Servicemembers Civil Relief Act), if the defendant is defaulting, the court may require you to attest that they are not in active military (which would entitle them to a stay). Some states have a specific form or the judge might just ask under oath. Not explicitly listed, but be prepared – perhaps check the Department of Defense SCRA website to verify defendant’s status before default hearing and have that info. Wyoming small claims doesn’t have a separate form number for it, perhaps the Judgment form has a clause about it.

- If either party wants to subpoena a witness to testify or to bring documents (“subpoena duces tecum”), you would ask the Circuit Court for a subpoena form. Usually, they use the same subpoena form as in other civil cases (the clerk issues it, you serve it, includes witness fee $10/day + mileage). Not a small-claims-specific form, but available from clerk. On the Wyoming courts site, subpoena forms are often under general forms. If needed, e.g., you want a mechanic to testify or someone to produce records at trial, do this ahead of the hearing.

- **Consent/Authorization Form:** If you are a business and want an employee to represent you, or you’re representing a relative, technically the rules allow non-attorneys for companies and for individuals (with permission) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). No formal authorization form is required by rule (just show you’re an employee or officer of company). But if you’re, say, helping a small LLC and you’re not an officer, maybe bring a letter on letterhead confirming you’re authorized. For an individual using a friend for help (not common, attorneys are allowed so usually they either have an attorney or self), but since attorneys are allowed, generally a non-attorney friend can’t represent *unless* maybe under the rule any “authorized employee or organization rep” covers individuals? Actually, 1-21-202(b) says even natural persons can appear without attorney *through authorized employees* ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). That’s a bit odd, as a natural person doesn’t have employees, presumably. It likely means an individual can represent themself or have an attorney, but not a random person. The rule’s main effect is for corporations and organizations to appear via employees. So anyway, no form needed if you’re the business owner; just show up with proof of your role if questioned.

**County Differences:** As stated, all counties use these same forms. Possibly some counties might have **additional local forms** like:
- An **Acknowledgment of Satisfaction** form (when judgment paid, to file satisfaction). There’s likely a generic one; not in SC list, but one can be drafted or sometimes provided.
- A local **Stipulation form** if parties settle and want to file an agreed order. Usually, parties can just put an agreement in writing and ask the judge to make it the judgment or dismiss accordingly.

**Filing the Forms:** You will fill out SC-01 and maybe a draft SC-02 (with defendant’s name/address). The clerk finalizes SC-02 by adding hearing details and signing/sealing it. Then you arrange service.

**Costs for Forms:** All these official forms are free to obtain (online or at clerk). The only costs are the filing fee ($10) and copies if needed (like if you want clerk to make copies, they might charge e.g. $0.50/page – but you can bring your own copies). Summons issuance is included in filing fee.

**Completing Forms Legibly:** Use black ink or type. The form-filling interview is great because it prints neatly. The court clerks in Wyoming often prefer typed or very clear handwriting. 

**Double-Check:** Always double check the **spelling of names** and **addresses** on your forms because service and judgment enforcement depend on correct info. If you sue “Jon Smith” but legally it’s “John Q. Smith”, that might complicate collection. If a business is involved, ensure you have the correct legal name (e.g. “Acme Plumbing, LLC” rather than just “Acme Plumbing”). It’s wise to look up any business on Secretary of State’s business search to get exact name and maybe add “d/b/a” if needed (like if Joe Doe does business as Joe’s Garage, list both Joe Doe and “Joe’s Garage” as defendants to cover it). 

**Sample Completed Forms:** Check the self-help resources or ask the clerk if they have a sample filled-out affidavit for reference. Some might have a dummy example posted. The instructions (SC-01a, SC-05) likely walk you through line by line.

In summary, for a Wyoming small claim you’ll need to file a **Small Claims Affidavit (SC-01)** and have the court issue a **Summons (SC-02)**, then ensure a **Proof of Service** (SC-03/04) gets filed. Upon resolution, a **Judgment (SC-06)** is entered. All these forms are available on the Wyoming Judicial Branch’s website and from Circuit Court clerks ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=Name%20%20,718)). Use the official forms to avoid omissions – they contain the necessary legal language (for instance, the affidavit has the perjury statement and demand made statement which you might not know to include if drafting from scratch). 

*(Resources: Wyoming Judicial Branch Self-Help Forms – Small Claims ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2001,Filling%20Guide)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=The%20links%20in%20this%20section,them%20out%20using%20a%20pen)); Rules and Forms Governing Small Claims (Appendix of Forms list) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2001,Download)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=Name%20%20,718)).)*

## 8. Service of process

After filing your small claim, you (or rather, the court) must properly “serve” the defendant with notice of the lawsuit. **Service of process** means delivering the **Summons** and a copy of your **Affidavit/Complaint** to the defendant in a manner prescribed by law, so that the court has jurisdiction and the defendant is informed of the case and hearing date.

**Who can serve:** In Wyoming small claims, typically the **Sheriff’s Office** or a **private process server** is used, or the **court clerk via certified mail** in certain cases ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=The%20defendant%20must%20be%20served,your%20case%20cannot%20go%20forward)). Personal service (hand delivery) is preferred. Wyoming law (W.S. 1-21-203) provides that the clerk shall have the summons served “in the manner provided by law” (meaning like normal civil cases), **or** “if the defendant resides in the state, service may be made by the court by certified mail” to their in-state address with return receipt ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)). 

So you have a few options:
- **Sheriff’s Deputy:** This is a common, reliable method. You give the Summons and Affidavit to the Sheriff’s civil process division in the county where the defendant is located. They will attempt in-person delivery. A Sheriff or deputy can serve anyone in their county and will sign a return of service.
- **Private Process Server:** If available (especially in more populous counties), you can hire a certified process server to deliver the papers. They must be a neutral adult (Wyoming doesn’t license servers generally, but if it’s a professional server, they know the rules). After service, they’ll provide a notarized affidavit of service (which you file).
- **Any Adult (over 18, not a party):** Technically, under general civil procedure (W.R.C.P. 4(c)), any disinterested adult can serve. The small claims statute doesn’t limit service to sheriffs; it says “manner provided by law,” which includes private service. So if you have a friend or relative (not involved in the case) willing to hand-deliver, that is allowed – but then that person must complete an **Affidavit of Service (SC-04)** and have it notarized, and you file that. However, using the Sheriff has the advantage of official capacity and often more likelihood the defendant will accept service.
- **Certified Mail by Clerk:** If the defendant has an address in Wyoming, the Circuit Court clerk *may* attempt service via **certified mail, return receipt requested** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)). Some courts do this routinely for small claims – it’s convenient and low-cost. They will send the Summons + Affidavit to the defendant’s address on file, restricted delivery to addressee (ideally) or with return receipt. If the **green card comes back signed by the defendant or their agent**, that constitutes valid service on the date signed ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=on%20the%20defendant%20in%20the,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)). The clerk will file that return receipt as proof. 
    - Note: If the certified mail is **unclaimed or refused**, then service by mail failed and you must use personal service. The small claims FAQ explicitly says “Most people use the Sheriff’s office or a private process server because they know how to serve the papers according to the law” ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Most%20people%20use%20the%20Sheriff%E2%80%99s,to%20find%20out%20what%20service)), implying that relying on mail can be iffy if the defendant doesn’t sign. Also, some clerks might only do mail if you request it or for rural areas with reliable mail service. 
    - If the defendant **actually signs** the certified receipt, service is done ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=on%20the%20defendant%20in%20the,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)). If someone else at their address (like a spouse or receptionist) signs, that might be considered agent if authorized, but there’s risk the court says need actual defendant or an appointed agent. The statute says “signed by the defendant or his agent” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=defendant%20at%20his%20address%20within,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)). The term “agent” could cover someone in their household or business if appropriate. Usually, restricted delivery is recommended so only defendant can sign. 
    - If you want to attempt mail first (cheaper), ensure you give the clerk a correct address and perhaps mention “certified mail preferred.” Some courts might do it automatically for in-county addresses.
- **Substitute Service:** Under standard civil procedure (W.R.C.P. 4(d)), personal service can be made by leaving the papers at the person’s **usual residence** with someone “suitable” (e.g. an adult resident) if the defendant isn’t home. Also, for businesses, delivering to an officer, managing agent, or the registered agent is acceptable. 
    - Wyoming rules typically allow leaving with a family member over certain age at the house as valid. For small claims, since it references “manner provided by law,” these substitute methods are likely allowed. A sheriff or process server would know to do that if they can’t catch the defendant personally. 
    - Example: Process server goes to defendant’s house, defendant isn’t there but spouse is; leaving with spouse might suffice, or they might try again. Usually, leaving with a competent adult co-resident is valid service under W.R.C.P. 4(d)(2). 
- **Posting or Publication:** Small claims rules lean against these because you need the defendant to know about the hearing. There’s no special small claims rule for service by publication. But in theory, if you cannot find the defendant after due diligence, you could ask the Circuit Court for permission to serve by publication (like you would in a normal civil case by W.R.C.P. 4(e)). That involves publishing notice in a newspaper once a week for 4 weeks. It’s slow and costly and rarely used for small claims because if you can’t find them, even a judgment is moot for collection. But legally, it’s a fallback. If you find yourself unable to locate the person, talk to the clerk or judge about whether publication is allowed for small claims. (Given small claims hearing is set usually within a month, an attempt at publication might necessitate continuing the hearing to allow the 4 week run and the answer period. So it complicates the simple small claims timeline.)
    - The safer route: have at least a last known address to try certified mail; if that fails, consider hiring a skip-trace or process server to locate them or just not proceed until found. 
- **Timing of Service:** Wyoming’s rule for small claims (in court rules or statute) requires that service of the summons must occur within a **specific window** before the hearing: not less than **3 days** and not more than **12 days** prior to the court date ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Important%21%20The%20court%20date%20must,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)). This is unusual – it’s a tight “9-day window” (3 to 12 days before). The official website states: “the Defendant has to be served in a nine-day window (3 to 12 days) before the scheduled court date” ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Important%21%20The%20court%20date%20must,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)). This means:
    - You cannot serve too far in advance; if served earlier than 12 days before hearing, that’s technically out of window (maybe require re-service closer to date).
    - You cannot serve too late (must be at least 3 full days before). 
    - That’s why clerks set hearing about a month out, giving you time to effect service and then ensuring you coordinate so that service happens in that golden window. Typically, they might instruct the server to attempt service within that timeframe. If you get defendant served earlier than 12 days out – say 20 days out – the rule might necessitate re-serving them closer to the date (or possibly the judge can deem it ok if defendant actually got notice – but to be safe, abide by it).
    - If defendant is not served by 3 days before, the hearing cannot proceed and will be reset with an alias summons ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=If%20the%20Defendant%20is%20not,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)). As the FAQ states: “If Defendant is not served in those nine days, the hearing cannot be held on your scheduled date. You can ask the Clerk to issue an Alias Summons with a different court date. However, you will have to pay separately for each attempt to serve the Defendant.” ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=If%20the%20Defendant%20is%20not,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)).
    - So, timing is critical. Work with the clerk and the server so that service is done in that window. Many sheriffs, when they get the paperwork early, will hold onto serving until ~2 weeks before court to ensure compliance. Or the clerk may not even generate the summons until about two weeks out.
    - Ask the clerk: “When will the Summons be issued / how do I coordinate service in the 3-12 day window?” They may give the summons to you immediately, and it’s on you to orchestrate service at the right time. Or some might schedule hearing for say 30 days out, but instruct you not to serve until at least 12 days out – but that’s risky to rely on a volunteer process server to time it precisely. If using sheriff, you could give it to them early and mention the required window – they likely know it and will attempt accordingly.
    - If service happened outside window, you’ll likely need to have the hearing continued and do an alias (new summons with new date) and serve again. Better to avoid that by mindful timing.
- **Serving Businesses or Entities:** 
    - If defendant is a **corporation/LLC**, serve the registered agent or an officer. You can find the registered agent’s name/address from the WY Secretary of State business search. A sheriff can deliver to that agent. You can also serve any officer, director, or perhaps the manager at a business location if the agent is not easily found. Under rules, serving a business by delivering to a manager at its principal office can be valid. But best is the registered agent because then no dispute.
    - If defendant is a **partnership**, serving any general partner should suffice.
    - If defendant is a **public entity** (city, county, etc.), service often is to the official like city clerk or designated agent by statute. But as noted, suing governments is special – if you did, you’d serve the mayor or clerk as appropriate (and consider the claim notice issues).
- **After Service – Proof:** Whichever method, you need proof filed:
    - The **sheriff** will usually send a “Return” to the court indicating date/time of service or inability. Check with the clerk if they received it before the hearing. 
    - A **private server or adult** must complete an **Affidavit of Service** (SC-04). It should list: when (date, time) and where they served, and on whom (if substitute, describe person and location). It should be notarized. File that with the court as soon as possible (at least by hearing).
    - **Certified mail:** The clerk will attach the green card to the summons or note in the file that it was delivered on X date. If the green card (return receipt) does not come back, or comes back “unclaimed,” the clerk might notify you and in any case hearing can’t proceed. You’d then do personal service via alias summons as mentioned.
    - **What if defendant refuses service in person?** If a person refuses to accept the papers (like won’t take them or tears them up), generally the server can “drop them at their feet” or in close proximity and that counts as delivered (presence and identification and informing them suffice). The server will note “defendant refused to accept, so I informed of nature and left documents at their feet” – that’s valid.
    - If the defendant is **evading** (hiding), a server can try alternate times (evenings, weekends). If truly evasive, one might attempt substitute service (leave with family or even post on door followed by mailing – though posting on door isn’t explicitly provided by rule except in evictions, but some judges allow if clearly defendant is dodging).
- **If Service Fails by hearing time:** As noted, you’ll have to **reset** the hearing by getting an **Alias Summons** from the clerk with a new date and repeat service attempts ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=If%20the%20Defendant%20is%20not,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)). There’s usually no extra filing fee for alias summons, but you **do pay new service fees** (thus multiple attempts cost more, though you can add them as costs if you eventually get judgment).
    - Communicate with the clerk if you see trouble serving – you might proactively ask to reschedule so that you don’t waste a court date. Usually, the clerk will wait to see if proof of service comes in by 3 days prior; if not, they might automatically remove the case from that docket. But don’t assume – it’s usually on you to request alias.
    - “Alias Summons” is just a new summons with a later date, labeled alias. The process is same as original service.
- **Out-of-State Defendants:** If the defendant lives out of Wyoming:
    - Wyoming’s small claims statute doesn’t explicitly forbid suing a non-resident if the cause of action happened in Wyoming (long-arm jurisdiction). You can theoretically serve them out-of-state via certified mail or through the sheriff in that state. But practically, if they aren’t local, collection also is tough. If you do proceed:
        - Use a process server or sheriff in the other state – you’ll need to find one and pay them, as Wyoming sheriffs only serve in WY.
        - Or send by certified mail (RRR) to out-of-state address. Wyoming’s statute authorizing mail explicitly says “if the defendant resides in the state, service may be by certified mail” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)) – that implies for out-of-state, perhaps they intended requiring personal service. The general rule is you can use any method allowed by Wyoming Rules or that state’s law. The Wyoming Rule of Civil Procedure 4 allows service out-of-state by any manner as if service within WY (so certified mail out of state probably okay, but ensure restricted to addressee).
        - Check the time frames – mailing to another state might take longer, so ensure they actually receive it 3 to 12 days before hearing. That can be tricky if mail is slow. Possibly the judge might be lenient if out-of-state service happened 15 days before hearing, but strictly speaking, the rule doesn’t excuse it for out-of-state. You might need to schedule hearing a bit further out to accommodate mailing time, but still ensure within 12 days once delivered. Very finicky – you might ask the clerk how to handle an out-of-state defendant’s timing. 
        - If the defendant objects to jurisdiction or venue (because they’re out-of-state and say they have no ties), the case could be dismissed. If the dispute arises from an event in Wyoming, there’s usually jurisdiction, but if not, the case doesn’t belong in WY at all.
    - Many small claims courts might effectively not entertain suits against far-away folks, because they can’t enforce appearance or easily enforce judgment. But legally, it’s possible.
- **Service Charge and Payment:** As mentioned, you have to pay for service upfront. Sheriff often expects payment when you deliver the documents (some bill later, but assume prepay). Process servers definitely require fees. If you do certified mail through clerk, you may need to provide a postal fee or the clerk might send and add a postal fee to costs due at hearing.
- **If Multiple Defendants:** Each must be served separately. If, say, you sue two people living together, a server might deliver both sets to one of them if the other is absent – leaving one copy for each defendant, ideally with each or a suitable person for each. Many times, leaving both sets with one co-resident adult (like a spouse) and explaining it’s for both is done. Legally, the person who receives them can only accept for themselves unless also agent for the other; but if they’re family at same address, judges often find that sufficient if the second defendant obviously got notice. To be safe, if both are home, serve each personally. If one’s home and one’s not, leaving both with the one present typically works (especially if spouse).
    - The server’s affidavit should specify names: e.g., “Delivered copies for John Doe and Jane Doe to John Doe (husband) at their residence, he stated he would give Jane her copy.” Ideally, also mail a copy to Jane as follow-up for caution. Usually that’s enough in small claims. 
    - The best practice: serve each individually when possible (means maybe making a second trip to catch the other).
- **If Defendant is Minor or Incompetent:** If you somehow are suing a minor (e.g. a 17-year-old caused damage), you must also serve their parent/guardian. If suing someone who is under legal guardianship due to incompetence, you serve their guardian. 
- **Proof of Service Filing Deadline:** The proof of service should be filed by the time of the hearing. At the latest, bring it to the hearing to show the judge. It’s better to get it in a couple days before, so the judge sees in the file that service was done properly. If using sheriff, check with clerk a few days before hearing whether they got the return. If not, get a copy from sheriff or have the deputy present at hearing to testify to service if needed (rarely needed if clear).
- **If Defendant was served but doesn’t show:** That’s grounds for default (the judge will verify service was proper and within required timeframe before granting default). If any doubt, the judge may ask you to re-serve and continue case. That’s why doing it right is crucial – you don’t want a dismissed or delayed case due to technical service flaw.

**Key Takeaways:**
- **Personal service is the gold standard** – have an adult physically hand the papers to the defendant.
- **Timing** – serve 3-12 days before court ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Important%21%20The%20court%20date%20must,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)). Too early or too late will void service for that hearing.
- **Document it** – a completed Return/Affidavit of Service must be filed.
- **Costs** – you’ll pay initially, but add those costs to your claim (the judgment can include reimbursing the service fee and even postage).
- **Alternate Service if needed** – if normal attempts fail, consult the court about substitute or publication. The court wants the defendant to have actual notice – publication is last resort if you can demonstrate the defendant cannot be found.

*(References: Wyo. Stat. § 1-21-203(a) on service by law or certified mail ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)); Small Claims FAQ from Wyo courts on service window and alias summons ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Important%21%20The%20court%20date%20must,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=If%20the%20Defendant%20is%20not,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)); W.R.C.P. 4 on general service methods (not quoted above but underlying authority); Wyo Judicial Branch “How do I serve the Affidavit and Summons?” ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=The%20defendant%20must%20be%20served,your%20case%20cannot%20go%20forward)) which emphasizes personal service by sheriff or server.)*

## 9. Defendant's response and default

In Wyoming small claims, the **Defendant is not required to file a written answer** or other pleading prior to the hearing ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%205)). Instead, the summons instructs the defendant to **appear at the scheduled hearing** to present their side. Here’s how the defendant’s “response” works and what happens if they don’t respond:

- **No Formal Answer Required:** Wyoming’s small claims Rule 5 explicitly says, “No answer nor responsive pleading shall be required in small claims actions.” ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%205)). This means the defendant doesn’t have to submit a written answer or denial. The case simply goes to the hearing on the date set, and at that time the defendant can dispute the claim orally and/or with evidence. So as a defendant, you have the right to just show up and defend without having done any paperwork beforehand.

- **What if the Defendant wants to file something?** They *may* file a written explanation or documents or a **counterclaim** (as discussed). But they are not obligated to. If a defendant does file a written answer or letter, the court will accept it into the file, but it doesn’t substitute for appearing. The important thing is showing up to contest. 

- **Timeframe to Appear or Respond:** The summons itself will specify the **date, time, and location of the hearing** which effectively is the “appearance” date ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/litigation/going-to-court/wyoming-small-claims-courts.html#:~:text=A%20party%20may%C2%A0appeal%C2%A0the%20small%20claims,A%20party%20that%20wants%20to)) ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/litigation/going-to-court/wyoming-small-claims-courts.html#:~:text=appeal%20must%20file%20a%20Notice,days%20of%20the%20judgment%E2%80%99s%20issuance)). In Wyoming, that hearing date is typically about 3-4 weeks after service (depending on scheduling). Unlike some states where a defendant might have, say, 20 or 30 days to file an answer, here the defendant’s deadline is just *show up at that hearing.* If they can’t make that date, they should contact the court *beforehand* to ask for a continuance date (most courts will grant a first continuance if requested timely, especially since attorneys are allowed and they might want time to hire one – indeed if plaintiff has an attorney and defendant doesn’t, the defendant can get one continuance as of right to seek counsel ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=persons%2C%20corporations%2C%20partnerships%2C%20associations%20or,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own))).

- **If Defendant Wants a Continuance:** According to W.S. 1-21-202(b), if an attorney appears for one side and the other is unrepresented, the unrepresented side is entitled to a **continuance to obtain an attorney** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=persons%2C%20corporations%2C%20partnerships%2C%20associations%20or,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). Apart from that scenario, any party can request continuance for good cause (scheduling conflict, need more prep time, etc.). The proper way is to call or write the clerk as soon as you know. Some circuit judges might require a written motion or an agreement by the other side. Because small claims is informal, often a call or letter suffices. If the defendant doesn’t show and later claims they had a good reason, they can ask the court to set aside a default (more on that below).

- **What Happens at the Hearing if Defendant Appears:** It becomes essentially a simple trial (no jury). The judge will have both parties tell their story, present evidence, and the judge will decide on the spot or shortly after. The defendant can present defenses (reasons why they don’t owe the money or why the amount is wrong). They can also raise procedural issues (like improper venue or service or statute of limitations, etc.). In small claims, judges may not strictly enforce all technical defenses if not raised, but the defendant can and should assert any that apply.

- **Default if Defendant Fails to Appear:** If the defendant was **properly served** and does not show up for the hearing, the plaintiff can ask the judge to grant a **default judgment**. The judge will usually:
    1. Confirm that service was completed correctly (by reviewing the return of service and ensuring it was within 3-12 day window as required).
    2. Possibly ask the plaintiff to briefly state the case or provide proof of the amount owed (especially if the amount isn’t liquidated, like damages needing proof).
    3. If satisfied, the judge will enter a **Default Judgment** for the plaintiff for the amount proven (plus allowed costs and interest).
    
  This typically happens in the same hearing slot – the case is called, defendant

summary.text:
**Exploring case reopening options**

If the request is granted, the case reopens; if denied, they might attempt to appeal, claiming the denial was improper. That’s complicated since appeals focus on legal issues. It’s best to advise that if someone missed the hearing due to reasons like lack of service or emergencies, they should contact the court immediately to explain and likely file a motion to vacate the default. Judges tend to want to hear the merits if addressed promptly; waiting too long can result in the judgment standing.

**Understanding case motions and outcomes**

I need to verify if Wyoming has a specific timeframe for motions, possibly around six months for certain reasons. These motions are typically discretionary and not often granted without a valid excuse.

If the default judgment stands, the focus shifts to enforcement. If a defendant shows up but admits they owe the debt, the judge may enter a consent judgment, possibly discussing a payment plan. If the plaintiff doesn’t appear, the case might be dismissed. In such cases, if the defendant has a counterclaim, they could potentially proceed even if the plaintiff is absent.

**Emphasizing defendant response strategy**

It’s important to highlight that the best way for a defendant to respond is by being prepared. If they ignore the situation, there's a high chance the plaintiff will receive a default judgment. In cases of default judgment, there are post-judgment options available. The defendant can't appeal based on the facts but may try to set the judgment aside or appeal if they claim they didn’t receive proper service. I’ll finish section 9 and then review the following sections.
