## 9. Defendant's response and default

**No Written Answer Needed:** In Wyoming small claims, a defendant is **not required to file a written answer** or response before the hearing ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%205)). The Summons will specify a hearing date, and the defendant’s “response” is simply to **appear in court on that date** to tell their side. There is no pre-trial motion practice or discovery in small claims – everything typically happens at the hearing. Of course, a defendant **may** file a written statement or **counterclaim** if they wish (especially to formally assert a claim back against the plaintiff), but it isn’t mandatory. The critical thing is that the defendant **shows up** at the scheduled hearing. 

**Hearing Appearance:** The Summons (Form SC-02) will state something like: *“You are hereby summoned to appear at Small Claims Court on [Date] at [Time]…”* ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/litigation/going-to-court/wyoming-small-claims-courts.html#:~:text=A%20party%20may%C2%A0appeal%C2%A0the%20small%20claims,A%20party%20that%20wants%20to)) ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/litigation/going-to-court/wyoming-small-claims-courts.html#:~:text=appeal%20must%20file%20a%20Notice,days%20of%20the%20judgment%E2%80%99s%20issuance)). That is the defendant’s deadline – the court date. If the defendant **appears**, the case will proceed to a hearing. At the hearing, the defendant can present any defenses (e.g. “I did pay this debt” or “The product wasn’t defective” or legal defenses like statute of limitations or improper venue). The defendant can also bring evidence (receipts, texts, witnesses, etc.) to refute the plaintiff’s claim. Because attorneys are allowed in Wyoming small claims, a defendant **may** hire an attorney to represent them (or represent themselves). If one side has an attorney and the other doesn’t, the unrepresented side is entitled to a **continuance (postponement)** to hire counsel if they want ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). In practice, most small claims parties appear without lawyers, but the rule protects against surprise attorney appearances.

**Requesting a Continuance:** If a defendant (or plaintiff) cannot attend on the set date or needs more time (to gather evidence, hire a lawyer, etc.), they should **promptly request a continuance** from the court *before* the hearing. You can contact the Circuit Court clerk’s office as soon as you know of a conflict. Often, the court will grant one continuance for good cause. It’s best to put the request in writing, but some courts accept phone requests. If both sides agree to a continuance, it’s almost always granted (be sure to confirm a new date with the clerk). If you as defendant get served very close in time and cannot prepare by the hearing, ask the court – small claims judges tend to be lenient on one reschedule. Remember, if the **plaintiff’s case involves an attorney** and you as defendant don’t have one, you have a right to a continuance to seek counsel ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). Use that if needed. The goal is that both sides have a fair chance to be heard.

**What if the Defendant does nothing?** If the defendant **fails to appear** at the hearing and the plaintiff has proof they were properly served, the court will likely enter a **default judgment** for the plaintiff. Here’s how that works:

- The judge will call the case at the hearing. If the plaintiff is present and the defendant is absent, the judge will verify that the defendant was indeed served with the Summons and Affidavit in the required manner and time frame (see Section 8). The judge may ask the plaintiff to briefly state the nature of the claim and provide any evidence of the amount owed (especially if the claim isn’t a sum-certain like a promissory note). As long as the claim is credible and service was valid, the judge can issue a **default judgment** in favor of the plaintiff then and there ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/litigation/going-to-court/wyoming-small-claims-courts.html#:~:text=A%20party%20may%C2%A0appeal%C2%A0the%20small%20claims,A%20party%20that%20wants%20to)).

- **Soldiers and Default:** One caveat – under the federal Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA), courts must ensure that a defaulting defendant is not on active military duty. Typically, the plaintiff may be required to submit an affidavit or testify that the defendant is not in military service. If the defendant is in the military and on active duty, the court will usually not enter default or will appoint an attorney to protect their interests ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)). If you’re a plaintiff, it’s wise to check the defendant’s military status (there’s an online DoD database where you can enter their name and birthdate to verify). If the defendant is an active servicemember, the case may be stayed until they can participate. Assuming that’s not an issue, the default can proceed.

- **Default Judgment Entry:** When entering default, the judge will sign a **Judgment** (often using Form SC-06) awarding the plaintiff the amount of the claim proven, plus court costs (filing fee, service fee) and any pre-judgment interest if appropriate. Post-judgment interest at 10% per year will also apply by law ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)). This judgment is as valid as any – the plaintiff can then move to enforce it (see Section 12 on collection). The court typically will mail a notice or copy of the judgment to the defendant’s last known address, but that is a courtesy; legally, the defendant has already had their chance by being served and failing to appear.

- **Set-Aside of Default:** A defendant who missed the hearing can ask the court to **vacate (set aside) the default judgment** if they have a good reason. They must act quickly. Under general civil rules (Wyo. R. Civ. P. 55(c) & 60(b)), a default judgment can be set aside if the defendant demonstrates excusable neglect, lack of proper service, or another valid ground (e.g. serious illness on the hearing date, or they were never served). The defendant should file a **Motion to Set Aside Default Judgment** as soon as possible (ideally within 30 days of learning of the judgment, and the sooner the better). They should explain the reason for not appearing and also show they have a meritorious defense (courts don’t set aside default if it’s a waste of time – the defendant should show there’s some dispute to try). The Circuit Court judge has discretion to grant or deny that motion. If granted, the case is reopened and a new hearing date set, giving the defendant a chance to fight the claim. If denied, the default stands. 
    - **Appealing a Default:** Wyoming’s small claims appeal rules (see Section 11) allow appeals only on issues of law, not a new trial of facts. A pure default (where no evidence was presented by defendant) leaves little to appeal except perhaps a legal mistake (like the court lacked jurisdiction or applied the wrong law in awarding damages). Generally, if a defendant wants relief from a default, the motion to vacate in Circuit Court is the proper route; appeal is limited. 

**Plaintiff’s Non-appearance:** It’s not just defendants who sometimes no-show – if the **plaintiff** fails to appear at the hearing (and hasn’t requested a continuance), the court will typically dismiss the case. This is often a dismissal “without prejudice,” meaning the plaintiff can re-file the claim anew (though they’ll have to pay the fee again). If the defendant is present and has a counterclaim filed, the court might proceed on the counterclaim even if plaintiff isn’t there – or at least reset the counterclaim hearing. But for the plaintiff’s claim, not showing up means they abandon their case for that day. Always attend the hearing or notify the court well in advance if an emergency arises. If you’re the plaintiff and you miss the court date due to something like a calendaring error or emergency, you can promptly file a Motion to Reschedule or to reinstate the case, but it’s up to the judge. It’s easier to just be there on time.

**Consent or Settlement at Hearing:** If both parties appear and one effectively admits the claim or they reach an agreement in the hallway, the court can enter a **consent judgment** or **dismissal** as appropriate. For example, a defendant might say, “I owe the money, I just need a month to pay.” The judge can either pause the case to allow payment, or more commonly, enter judgment for the agreed amount and perhaps stay execution until the agreed payment date. Alternatively, the parties might announce a settlement (e.g. defendant will pay $X by a date). The court can then dismiss the case, usually “without prejudice, to be reopened if payment not made” or enter judgment but not enforce it if paid by that date. The exact handling depends on what parties want. The key is: if you settle before the hearing, let the court know so the time isn’t wasted (some courts have a form or you can simply appear and state the agreement for the record).

**Bottom Line for Defendants:** Do **not** ignore a small claim summons. If you do nothing, the plaintiff will most likely get a default judgment against you, and they can then garnish your wages or bank account, lien your property, etc. If you dispute the claim or even if you can’t pay it fully, it’s far better to show up and speak to the judge. Small claims judges are used to one or both sides being non-lawyers and will guide the process. There is no jury – only the judge (or magistrate) will decide ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=No%2C%20you%20are%20not%20required,for%20help%20finding%20a%20lawyer)), so you can calmly explain your position. By appearing, you preserve your rights: you can defend, negotiate, or request a continuance if caught off guard. By staying absent, you essentially forfeit.

*(Authority: Rule 5 of Rules for Small Claims (no responsive pleading required) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%205)); W.S. 1-21-202(b) (continuance if attorney appears) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)); Wyoming Judicial Branch FAQ (“No answer required… just appear on your court date”) – implied from context; default procedure is per standard civil practice since small claims rules are silent – Wyo. R. Civ. P. 55 default and 60(b) relief apply. SCRA: 50 U.S.C. § 3931 requires affidavit of military status before default judgment; Small claims appeals discussed in Section 11.)*

## 10. Hearing procedure

**Informal Bench Trial:** A Wyoming small claims hearing is a relatively informal **bench trial** (no jury) in front of a Circuit Court **judge** or sometimes a magistrate. The atmosphere is meant to be accessible to laypeople. The judge will call the case, verify who is present, and then allow each side to present their story and evidence. There isn’t a strict set of courtroom procedures like in higher courts – the judge often will ask the plaintiff to speak first, then the defendant, ask questions as needed, and review any evidence either side provides. The goal is to get to the truth efficiently. There is typically **no court reporter** in small claims hearings (they may be audio-recorded in some courts, or not recorded at all except the judgment in the minutes). So be prepared to make your points clearly and succinctly during this one chance.

**Role of Attorneys:** Unlike some states that ban lawyers in small claims, Wyoming **allows parties to be represented by attorneys** in small claims court ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). In fact, W.S. 1-21-202(b) explicitly says any party (including corporations, partnerships, the state, etc.) may appear “with or without an attorney,” though if an attorney does appear, the other side is entitled to a postponement to get their own attorney ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). In practice, many small claims litigants do not hire lawyers because the amounts are modest – but you have the right to. If only one side has a lawyer, the judge will ask the unrepresented side if they want time to seek counsel (and will grant it if requested). At the hearing, if attorneys are present, procedure may be a bit more formal (the judge might let the lawyer for plaintiff present an organized case, then defense, etc.). If neither side has a lawyer, the judge will guide the process in plain English. **Important:** Even if you don’t have a lawyer, you should still follow basic court etiquette – be respectful, don’t interrupt the other side or the judge, and address the judge as “Your Honor.” The judge will ensure each side gets time to speak.

**Length and Format:** Small claims hearings are usually very short – often scheduled with multiple cases on a docket. Expect maybe **10–20 minutes** of actual hearing time for straightforward cases, perhaps a bit more if the case is complicated. The court’s goal is to handle many cases efficiently. For example, if you have lots of documents or multiple witnesses, you likely won’t go through them in minute detail as you might in a regular trial. Instead, highlight the key points. The judge may do a lot of the questioning to speed things up (“Mr. Plaintiff, what proof do you have of the debt?” … “Ms. Defendant, why do you believe you don’t owe it?” etc.). Because it’s a bench trial, the judge can also admit evidence with more leeway (they’re less strict about formal rules of evidence).

**Evidence Rules:** Wyoming’s small claims rule explicitly states that *“Strict rules of evidence shall not apply in trials of small claims actions.”* ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%206)). In other words, the judge can consider almost anything relevant. You won’t typically be objecting “hearsay!” or “lack of foundation!” – the court can hear hearsay and give it whatever weight it deems appropriate. That said, you should still bring **reliable** evidence: firsthand testimony, original documents if available, photographs, etc. The more solid your evidence, the more persuasive. If the other side brings something obviously unreliable, you can certainly point that out (“Your Honor, that’s just a rumor, there’s no document or witness backing it”). The judge will use common-sense judgment on evidence. So:
  - **Documents:** Bring **copies** of any relevant documents (contracts, emails, bills, texts, photos). It’s good to have at least two copies (one for the judge, one for the other side) in addition to your own. Since formal discovery isn’t done, you may be seeing each other’s evidence for the first time at the hearing. The judge will often ask each of you to show the other any documents before giving them to the court.
  - **Witnesses:** You can have witnesses testify on your behalf. Because it’s one hearing, you must have them ready to go that day (there’s no separate witness deposition stage). If a witness is important and not likely to come voluntarily, you can request the court issue a **subpoena** to compel their attendance (and/or bring documents). The clerk has subpoena forms (the standard civil *Subpoena* form) you can fill out and have the court issue. You must serve the subpoena on the witness and pay them the statutory witness fee ($10 per day plus mileage at the state rate) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-14/section-1-14-102/#:~:text=or%20commissioner%2C%20ten%20dollars%20%28%2410,for%20half%20a%20day%3B%20and)). In small claims, witnesses often come voluntarily (friends, family, etc.). If you have an expert witness (e.g. a mechanic to say a repair was faulty), it’s allowed, but you’ll have to pay their time and the court won’t necessarily give formal expert weight; they’ll just consider their testimony like any other knowledgeable person. Keep witness testimony concise and focused. The judge may limit cumulative witnesses or repetitive statements due to time.
  - **Affidavits/Statements:** Since not all witnesses can appear, sometimes parties bring written statements or affidavits from absent witnesses. These are hearsay, but given relaxed evidence rules, the judge might read them. Be aware, though, that a live witness is far more effective (they can be questioned).  A letter from someone carries less weight if the other side disputes it. Use them only if the witness truly can’t attend and their statement is clear and credible.
  - **Physical evidence:** If there’s a small item in dispute or damaged goods, you can bring it (or photos of it). For example, if suing over a defective product, you could bring the product to show the judge the defect, if practical. If not practical (e.g. a car’s damage), photos or even a video are great. Wyoming’s courts are not highly technical, so have prints of photos in case electronic display is not available. The judge may even step outside to view a car in the parking lot if it’s critical, but that’s rare.
  
**Sequence of Trial:** Typically:
  1. **Plaintiff’s presentation:** The judge often asks the plaintiff to speak first since plaintiff bears the burden of proof. The plaintiff should clearly state what the claim is about, the key facts, and the amount sought. The judge may interrupt with questions to clarify. The plaintiff can hand up any documents to the judge (and defendant). The plaintiff can call any witness they brought – e.g. “I’d like my friend who was present to testify on what was said.” The judge will swear in the witness (all testimony is under oath) and allow a brief narrative or Q&A. In an informal setting, witnesses typically just tell their account; if attorneys are involved, they will do direct examination. After a plaintiff’s witness speaks, the judge will allow the defendant or their attorney to ask that witness questions (cross-examination). Judges keep this very informal for unrepresented parties – they might ask, “Ms. Defendant, do you have anything you want to ask or say about what this witness said?” 
  2. **Defendant’s presentation:** After the plaintiff (and their witnesses) have spoken, the judge turns to the defendant. The defendant can then explain their defense and present evidence and witnesses similarly. The plaintiff (or their lawyer) can also ask questions of the defendant’s witnesses if needed. The judge might cut in to ask the parties questions directly at any point.
  3. **Rebuttal:** Because small claims is informal, there isn’t a strict rebuttal phase, but the judge usually asks the plaintiff after the defense, “Do you have anything further?” This is the time to address any new points the defendant raised. Keep it brief and factual. The defendant might get a final word as well if necessary. Essentially, the judge ensures both sides feel they’ve had the chance to say everything important.
  4. **Evidence objectivity:** While strict rules don’t apply, if one side thinks something is truly irrelevant or bogus, they can politely object or comment (“Your Honor, I don’t see how that relates to this case.”). The judge can decide whether to consider it. Generally, judges let people talk but give irrelevant or unsubstantiated claims little weight.
  
**Burden of Proof:** The plaintiff must prove their claim by a **preponderance of the evidence** (more likely than not) since this is a civil case. The judge will decide whom to believe. If it’s basically one person’s word against the other’s with no documentation, the court will weigh credibility. Small claims judges rely on common sense – having organized evidence, clear stories, and a demeanor of honesty can make the difference. 

**Courtroom Setting:** Small claims are typically held in the regular courtroom or a smaller hearing room. Dress neatly (business casual is fine – no need for a suit, but avoid shorts, tank tops, or shirts with offensive images). Arrive early. The court may handle multiple cases that session, so expect to wait until yours is called. When speaking to the judge, stand (unless told otherwise) and address the judge as “Your Honor.” Each time you speak, it helps to begin with “Your Honor, …” especially if you’re explaining or responding rather than blurting statements towards the opponent. Remain calm, even if you strongly disagree with the other side – let them finish and then politely counterpoint. Wyoming’s judges are often personable in small claims, but respect is still key.

**Language and Interpreters:** If you or a witness is not fluent in English or needs an interpreter (including sign language), contact the court **in advance** to request an interpreter. Courts can provide interpreters at no cost in civil cases when necessary (usually by arrangement; Wyoming state courts will arrange one if given notice). Do not rely on a friend to interpret – the court prefers certified interpreters. If none is available in person, they may arrange a telephonic interpretation. So notify the clerk as soon as you know an interpreter is needed. The court wants everyone to understand the proceedings. Similarly, if any party has a cognitive disability or similar issue, inform the court; they may make accommodations.

**Time Management:** Because time is limited, focus on the key points of your case:
  - For plaintiffs: prove the **existence** of the debt/damage, and the **amount**. “I loaned $1,000 on June 1 per this IOU ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)), and he only repaid $300, leaving $700, which I demanded and he refused.” Show the IOU and any payment records. If claiming damages (like for property damage), show repair estimates or bills to substantiate the dollar amount.
  - For defendants: emphasize any **legal defenses** (e.g. “the suit was filed too late under the statute of limitations of 4 years ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)), and 5 years have passed”) and any **factual defenses** (“I did pay – here’s a receipt” or “The work was never done properly, so I don’t owe”). If you owe something but dispute the amount, clarify what portion you agree is owed and why the rest isn’t.
  
**After Both Sides Speak:** The judge may do one of the following:
  - **Deliver a judgment immediately:** Often, the judge will announce their decision right then. E.g. “I find for the plaintiff in the amount of $2,315 plus court costs and interest.” This will be followed by a written Judgment handed to you or mailed shortly after.
  - **Take it under advisement:** If the case is a bit complex or the judge wants to verify something, they might not rule on the spot. They may say, “I’ll mail my decision to you.” Expect a decision by mail in days or a couple of weeks at most (small claims decisions are usually prompt).
  - **Facilitate a settlement:** Sometimes, especially if the dispute is minor or involves ongoing relationships, a judge might encourage the parties to step outside and discuss settlement (for example, a payment plan). In some counties, a volunteer mediator might even be present to help negotiate (Equal Justice Wyoming has promoted mediation in some small claims). Mediation isn’t required, but if both parties are open to it, it might happen on the hearing day. If settlement is reached, it’s either written as a stipulation or put on record for the judge to adopt.
  
**Courtroom Technology:** Don’t assume high-tech presentation tools. Bring printed photos (in color) rather than planning to show things on your phone. Some Circuit Courts have TVs or projectors for evidence, but not all. If you have a video or audio recording critical to your case (say, a voicemail admitting debt or a security camera clip of damage), contact the clerk ahead of time to ask how you can present it. They might ask you to bring a laptop or it might need to be converted to a common format. Always have a transcript or summary in case technology fails, and offer to play only the relevant snippet, not a 10-minute ramble.

**Subpoenas for Witnesses:** If you need a reluctant witness, ask the Circuit Court clerk well before the hearing for a subpoena. The Wyoming Rules of Civil Procedure on subpoenas apply (so serve it reasonably in advance, and include the $10+ mileage fee) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-14/section-1-14-102/#:~:text=or%20commissioner%2C%20ten%20dollars%20%28%2410,for%20half%20a%20day%3B%20and)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-14/section-1-14-102/#:~:text=,the%20discretion%20of%20the%20court)). For example, if a mechanic who fixed your car would provide crucial testimony but won’t come on their own, subpoena them. If they still don’t show, the court can issue a show-cause or treat it as contempt. However, in small claims, it’s uncommon to enforce witness subpoenas with contempt – but the threat may encourage them to appear or send a statement. Use subpoenas judiciously (they involve service costs and are a hassle). 

**Witness Fees:** As noted, Wyoming law sets a modest witness fee of **$30 per day plus $0.58 per mile** (as of recent statute) for regular witnesses (the statute historically said $10/day ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-14/section-1-14-102/#:~:text=or%20commissioner%2C%20ten%20dollars%20%28%2410,for%20half%20a%20day%3B%20and)), but that was amended – actually, the current law shows $30/day for lay witnesses and $25/day for expert plus discretionary) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-14/section-1-14-102/#:~:text=or%20commissioner%2C%20ten%20dollars%20%28%2410,for%20half%20a%20day%3B%20and)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-14/section-1-14-102/#:~:text=expert%20witness%20shall%20be%20allowed,the%20discretion%20of%20the%20court)). In practice, if you subpoena someone, you must tender the fee with the subpoena (or promptly thereafter). If a witness comes voluntarily, you can optionally reimburse them, but it’s not required.

**Translation of Documents:** If you have documents in another language, translate them beforehand. The court could accept an informal translation, but it’s better to have a written translation to present alongside, and if possible, a translator to verify its accuracy.

**Behavior in Court:** Let the other party finish speaking – do not interrupt, even if you disagree strongly. You will get your turn to respond. If you need to object (for example, the other party is talking about something completely irrelevant or won’t stop interrupting you), politely say, “Your Honor, I object, because… [e.g., this isn’t related to the case].” The judge may address it or just note it and let things proceed. Remain calm; losing your temper or being overly argumentative can hurt your credibility. Judges observe demeanor – the more composed and respectful you are, the more credible you seem.

**No Jury:** Small claims cases in Wyoming are heard by the judge – there is no option for a jury trial in the small claims process ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=No%2C%20you%20are%20not%20required,for%20help%20finding%20a%20lawyer)). (If a defendant wanted a jury trial, they would effectively have to seek transfer of the case out of small claims to the regular civil docket, which typically one cannot do unless counterclaim exceeds jurisdiction or some constitutional right is at stake. But practically, small claims means judge trial.)

**After the Hearing:** The judge will enter a judgment or take it under advisement as noted. If you get a judgment in your favor, see Section 12 for how to collect. If you lose, the judge will dismiss the claim (or enter judgment for the defendant on any counterclaim). The judge should explain your appeal rights (see Section 11). Either way, thank the judge and exit promptly as there may be other cases waiting.

*(Authority: W.S. 1-21-202(b) (attorney appearance and continuance) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)); Small Claims Rule 6 (evidence rules not strict) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%206)); Rule 7 (appeals only on law) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)); common practice gleaned from Wyoming Judicial Branch FAQs and standard civil procedure W.R.C.P. 43 (evidence presentation), 45 (subpoenas) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-14/section-1-14-102/#:~:text=or%20commissioner%2C%20ten%20dollars%20%28%2410,for%20half%20a%20day%3B%20and)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-14/section-1-14-102/#:~:text=,the%20discretion%20of%20the%20court)). The absence of jury and presence of judge is clear from statute (small claims handled by circuit judges) and FAQ ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=No%2C%20you%20are%20not%20required,for%20help%20finding%20a%20lawyer)).)*

## 11. Appeals

**Who Can Appeal:** In Wyoming, **either party** (plaintiff or defendant) has the right to appeal a small claims judgment to the District Court (the next higher court) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)). There is no limitation that only the defendant can appeal (some states restrict plaintiffs who chose small claims from appealing – Wyoming does not have such a rule explicitly). However, there is one asymmetry: if a default judgment was entered (defendant didn’t appear), the **defaulted defendant** cannot directly appeal on the facts – they would first have to ask the circuit court to set aside the default (as discussed in Section 9). Appeals are for judgments on the merits where both sides had opportunity to present their case.

**Appeal Deadline:** The appeal must be filed **within 30 days** from the entry of the judgment. Wyoming statutes don’t spell out a special small claims appeal period, so the general civil appeal deadline of 30 days from entry of judgment (per Wyoming Rules of Appellate Procedure 2.01) applies. **Count 30 calendar days** from when the judgment or order was filed (the date on the written judgment). If the 30th day falls on a weekend or holiday, it extends to the next business day. This deadline is jurisdictional – a late appeal will be dismissed. So, if you intend to appeal, mark that date and don’t miss it.

**Where/How to File:** You file a **Notice of Appeal** with the Circuit Court that decided the case, and serve a copy on the other party. The Notice of Appeal is a short document stating you appeal from the judgment, identifying the case and judgment date, and the court appealed to (here, the District Court for that county). The clerk may have a form or you can draft it. The Circuit Court will then transmit the case file to the District Court. You will likely have to pay an appeal **filing fee** (District Court filing fee for civil appeals, often around $70-$85 – check with the clerk) and possibly post a **cost bond** or undertaking. Wyoming may require an appellant to post a bond to cover costs or judgment – the clerk can tell you. Often for small claims, the appellant must post a bond equal to the judgment amount (to stay enforcement during appeal) or some fixed amount like $100 to cover appeal costs. For instance, if the judgment is against the defendant and they appeal, to stop the plaintiff from executing (collecting) while appeal is pending, the defendant might need to post a supersedeas bond in the amount of the judgment plus interest/costs. Ask the Circuit Court clerk about bond requirements under the Wyoming Rules of Appellate Procedure for civil appeals from circuit.

**Nature of Appeal – Not a New Trial:** This is extremely important: A Wyoming small claims appeal is **not a retrial of the facts** (“trial de novo”). The District Court does **not** hold a new hearing with witnesses. Instead, the appeal is **on the record and questions of law only** ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)). In other words, the District Court will review the proceedings for legal error, not simply re-hear the case. The small claims rules specify that the appeal shall be “pursuant to the Wyoming Rules of Appellate Procedure and only on questions of law and not for a review of the sufficiency of the evidence.” ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)) This means:
  - The District Court judge will look at whether the Circuit Court applied the correct law, and whether any legal rulings were incorrect. They will **not override** the Circuit Court’s factual findings just because they might have weighed evidence differently; they only check that there was at least some evidence supporting the judgment (they won’t re-weigh credibility).
  - There usually isn’t a formal transcript of a small claims hearing, so the appellant may need to provide a *statement of the evidence or proceedings* if appealing something that happened orally. This can be tricky since small claims aren’t recorded. Often, appeals from small claims focus on **legal issues** like jurisdiction, statute of limitations, interpretation of a statute, admission/exclusion of evidence improperly, etc., rather than disputes about who was more believable.
  - For example, if the judge in small claims miscalculated the damages or applied the wrong interest rate, that could be an appealable error of law. Or if the judge refused to allow a clearly admissible defense (like ignoring a statutory cap on damages), that’s appealable. But simply arguing “the judge believed them over me and that’s wrong” will not succeed – that’s sufficiency of evidence, which cannot be the basis of appeal ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)).
  
**No New Evidence on Appeal:** You cannot introduce new evidence or witnesses at the District Court. The appeal is confined to what was presented in Circuit Court. This is why it’s crucial to present everything in the small claims hearing – you won’t get another shot at telling the story to a different judge with new evidence. The District Court generally relies on the record (which might just be the papers filed and the judge’s notes or audio recording). If there was no recording and parties disagree about what was said, the appellant can prepare a proposed statement of the evidence, and the Circuit Court judge will approve or modify it (W.R.A.P. 3.03) – effectively reconstructing the record for appeal.

**Appeal Briefs:** The appellant will likely need to file an **appeal brief** to the District Court, explaining the legal errors and citing relevant statutes or case law. The appellee (other party) can file a response brief. The District Court may hold an oral argument (or may decide on briefs alone for a small case). Many small claims appeals might be handled on briefs given the amounts at stake, but you can request oral argument. Remember, you are now in a formal appellate setting – if you weren’t represented before, you might consider consulting an attorney for the appeal, because writing a persuasive legal brief is challenging for non-lawyers. However, if the issue is simple (like the judge applied the wrong interest rate or misinterpreted a statute), you can point that out and cite the correct law on your own.

**Appeal Bond and Staying Execution:** Filing a Notice of Appeal does not automatically stop enforcement of the judgment. To **stay** (pause) the plaintiff’s collection efforts during the appeal, the appellant usually must post a **supersedeas bond**. In Wyoming, when appealing a money judgment, an appellant often must post a bond equal to the amount of judgment plus 10% to cover interest and costs, or some amount set by the court, to secure the judgment if the appeal fails. If no bond is posted, the plaintiff can potentially continue with collection (though often they will wait if an appeal is pending and timely, especially if it’s within 30 days and thus automatically within the appeal period). Check with the Circuit Court about bond requirements at the time of filing the appeal.

**Outcome of Appeal:** The District Court will either affirm the small claims judgment, reverse it, or remand (send back) with instructions. 
  - If **affirmed**, the original judgment stands. The case is effectively over (except possibly an appeal to the Wyoming Supreme Court, which is theoretically possible but typically the Supreme Court may decline to hear a further appeal from a small claims case unless a significant legal principle is at stake).
  - If **reversed**, the District Court may enter the correct judgment (for example, if they find for the appellant) or remand to Circuit Court to enter judgment for the other side or to conduct further proceedings as directed.
  - If **remanded**, the case goes back to the Circuit Court to handle whatever the higher court directed (could be a new hearing on a specific issue or entry of a modified judgment). 
Because appeals consider only legal errors, a reversal is uncommon unless a clear mistake of law occurred.

**No *De Novo* Trial:** Emphasizing again – unlike some states (like appeals from justice court in Texas, or small claims in some jurisdictions) where you get a whole new trial, here you do **not** get a new trial in District Court ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)). The District Court acts as an appellate reviewer.

**Appealing a Dismissal:** If you as a plaintiff lost (case dismissed) and you believe the judge erred (for example, dismissed on an incorrect legal basis like wrong interpretation of a statute of limitations or venue), you can appeal on those grounds. You cannot appeal just because the judge didn’t believe your evidence – that’s a factual issue, not appealable. But if, say, the judge refused to hear a key witness or incorrectly concluded your claim was time-barred when it actually wasn’t, those are legal issues you could raise.

**Costs on Appeal:** Usually the losing party on appeal may be assessed the appellate court costs. District Court might award costs of the appeal (filing fees, perhaps reasonable attorney fees if statute allows, etc.) to the prevailing party. However, since small claims are low-dollar, attorney fees are not typically awarded on appeal unless a contract or statute provides for them. The cost bond you post (if any) might be used to pay the appellee’s costs if you lose the appeal.

**Further Appeal to Supreme Court:** Technically, one can seek review by the Wyoming Supreme Court of the District Court’s appellate decision. However, the Supreme Court’s review is discretionary in such cases (it’s not an “appeal of right” because the District Court was acting in an appellate capacity). The Supreme Court seldom takes small claims matters unless a novel legal question is involved, as the amounts are small and the District Court’s decision is usually final for practical purposes. If you believe a fundamental legal error remains, you would file a Petition for Writ of Review or Certiorari to the Wyoming Supreme Court soon after the District Court’s decision (usually within 30 days). But this is rare and likely not cost-effective for most small claims.

**Settling During Appeal:** If the parties settle the case while an appeal is pending, they can file a stipulation to dismiss the appeal. This will leave the Circuit Court judgment in place or modified per agreement, or sometimes part of the settlement is that the judgment is vacated. The District Court will typically dismiss the appeal and remand for entry of any agreed modification if needed.

**Stay mindful of Appeal Procedures:** Appeals can be procedurally complex. If you’re the appellant (especially if you were the defendant below and now appealing), consider at least a consultation with an attorney. There are formalities: you’ll need to serve the Notice of Appeal on the other party, possibly file a **designation of record** (telling circuit clerk what to send up – likely “the entire record” since it’s small), and follow the Wyoming Rules of Appellate Procedure for briefs (format, deadlines). The clerk of the Circuit Court can provide basic guidance and the District Court clerk will handle docketing the appeal. But they can’t give legal advice, so tread carefully.

**Summary:** Appeals in Wyoming small claims are available to correct errors of law, not to re-argue the facts. The process must be initiated within 30 days by filing a notice and paying fees/bond. The District Court will review for legal mistakes. If you feel the magistrate or judge simply got the facts wrong, you’re likely stuck with the outcome – appeals won’t re-weigh evidence. But if the judge applied the wrong law or denied you a legal right, an appeal is your remedy.

*(Authority: Rules and Forms Governing Small Claims Cases, Rule 7: *“Appeals of small claims actions to district court shall be processed pursuant to W.R.A.P. and only on questions of law and not for a review of the sufficiency of evidence.”* ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)); Wyo. Stat. § 5-9-128(a)(iv) (district courts have appellate jurisdiction over circuit court cases); W.R.A.P. 1.03, 2.01 (30-day notice of appeal); W.R.A.P. 3.02 (civil appeal bond); W.R.A.P. 3.03 (statement of evidence if no transcript); and general practice. The 30-day limit and “on the record” nature is explicitly confirmed by Rule 7 of small claims ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)). FindLaw’s summary also notes either party can appeal within 30 days, but trial is not de novo ([www.findlaw.com](https://www.findlaw.com/litigation/going-to-court/wyoming-small-claims-courts.html#:~:text=A%20party%20may%C2%A0appeal%C2%A0the%20small%20claims,A%20party%20that%20wants%20to)).)*

## 12. Post-judgment collection

Winning a judgment is one thing – **collecting** the money is another. Nationally, a high percentage of small claims judgments go unpaid unless the creditor takes action. In Wyoming, once you have a judgment (whether by default or after a hearing), it is a legally enforceable debt. The court doesn’t automatically collect for you, but it provides tools you can use to enforce the judgment. Here’s how you can turn that paper judgment into actual dollars:

**Docketing the Judgment:** After the judge signs the Judgment, it will be filed with the Circuit Court clerk. You should obtain a certified copy of the judgment from the clerk (small fee of a few dollars) for your records and for any lien filings. The judgment will include the principal amount, court costs, and any interest awarded. By Wyoming law, judgments from the date of entry accrue **post-judgment interest at 10% per year** (or at any rate the contract specified, if applicable) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)). This interest is automatically added as time passes until the judgment is paid. 

**Voluntary Payment:** First, give the debtor a chance to pay voluntarily. Wyoming’s small claims info notes that “most of the time, the losing party, the Debtor, will pay what they now owe” ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Sometimes%2C%20the%20hardest%20part%20is,Seizing%20personal%20property%20or%20assets)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=When%20you%20win%20a%20Judgment,pay%20you%20after%20you%20win)). Often, once a judgment exists, the debtor may simply pay to avoid further hassle or damage to their credit. You can write a polite letter to the defendant (now judgment debtor) after the hearing summarizing the judgment and how to pay (e.g., “Please remit payment of $X to me by [date] to satisfy the judgment, or I will proceed with formal collection methods.”). Sometimes that’s enough.

If the debtor doesn’t pay within a reasonable time (the Wyoming Judicial Branch suggests the loser has 30 days to pay voluntarily ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=When%20you%20win%20a%20Judgment,pay%20you%20after%20you%20win)), which is common courtesy though not a fixed rule), you can proceed with formal collection.

**Judgment Lifecycle and Liens:** A Wyoming circuit court judgment is valid for **5 years** before it becomes “dormant” if not acted upon ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2022/title-1/chapter-17/article-3/section-1-17-307/#:~:text=1)). You can renew (revive) a dormant judgment for an additional period (up to 10 years after dormancy, effectively making judgments enforceable for up to 15 years or more with revival) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-5/section-1-16-503/#:~:text=,to%20bring%20the%20action%20was)). But ideally, you act long before dormancy. 

One of the first things to do is create a **lien on any real estate** the debtor owns. In Wyoming, a circuit court judgment itself does not automatically create a lien on real property until you **file it with the District Court and record it with the County Clerk**. Specifically, you can obtain an **Abstract of Judgment** from the Circuit Court (for a $5 fee) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=,00%29%20fee)). Then:
  - **File** a transcript of the judgment with the Clerk of the District Court in the same county ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=,transcript%2C%20the%20filing%2C%20recording%20and)), and 
  - **Record** a certified copy or abstract with the County Clerk (the county land records office) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=,transcript%2C%20the%20filing%2C%20recording%20and)).
Once recorded, the judgment becomes a lien on any real estate the debtor has in that county, from that time onward ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=the%20entry%20on%20the%20docket,rendered%20in%20the%20district%20court)). If the debtor owns property in other Wyoming counties, you can similarly file and record a transcript in those counties to lien property there ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=,district%20court%20of%20any%20county)). The lien lasts 5 years from judgment (since that’s when judgment dormancy hits, unless renewed) and can be extended by renewing the judgment and re-recording. A lien means the debtor usually cannot sell or refinance the property without paying you off, as buyers and title companies will require clearing the lien. **Note:** Wyoming’s homestead exemption is $100,000 of a home’s value ($200k if jointly owned) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Clerk,force%20a%20sale%20of%20the)), which means if the debtor’s primary residence equity is under that, a forced sale might not be practical. But the lien still sits there accruing interest, and if the property is valuable above exemption or not a homestead, you could later force a sale (via execution) if needed.

**Finding Assets:** To collect, you need to identify where the debtor has income or assets:
  - **Employment (Wages):** If the debtor is employed and earning wages, you can do a **Wage Garnishment** (called **Continuing Garnishment** in Wyoming) to take a portion of their paycheck each pay period until the judgment is paid ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=To%20garnish%20wages%20or%20income%2C,court%20multiple%20responses%20and%2For%20payments)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Continuing%20Garnishment,court%20multiple%20responses%20and%2For%20payments)). Wyoming allows continuing wage garnishments for 90-day stretches ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=To%20garnish%20wages%20or%20income%2C,court%20multiple%20responses%20and%2For%20payments)). You must know (or find out) where they work. Often you learn this from the hearing, or you may have seen their workplace or know it from information in the case. You might also ask around or use public data.
  - **Bank Accounts:** If the debtor has a bank or credit union account with funds, you can do a one-time **bank account garnishment** (called **Non-Continuing Garnishment** in Wyoming) to freeze money in their account on the day the garnishment hits and have it turned over to you ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=To%20seize%20money%20from%20a,the%20service%20of%20the%20Garnishment)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Court,be%20released%20to%20the%20Creditor)). You’ll need to know where they bank.
  - **Personal Property:** If they own valuable non-exempt personal property (a vehicle, equipment, etc.), you could have the sheriff **seize property (levy execution)** and sell it to satisfy the judgment ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=How%20do%20I%20seize%20personal,or%20real%20property)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=If%20you%20chose%20to%20go,you%20must%20attend%20a%20hearing)). This is more involved and may require you to pay advance fees for the sheriff and auction, which you recover if the sale yields enough. It’s less common unless the debtor has something significant and isn’t paying.
  - **Real Estate Execution:** If they own non-exempt real estate (e.g. a rental property or a house with lots of equity beyond the homestead exemption), you can levy and force a sheriff’s sale. This is rare for small claims because of cost and the homestead laws, but it’s possible if the circumstances fit.
  - **Debtor’s Auto:** Wyoming exempts up to $2,400 in one vehicle’s value ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,two%20thousand%20four%20hundred%20dollars)). If the debtor’s vehicle is valuable beyond that (say a $10,000 truck owned free and clear), you could theoretically have the sheriff levy it and auction it. But if it’s their primary transport, they might use exemptions to protect some of it. Still, a vehicle can be a target if clearly above exemption values.
  - **Other Income or Debts Owed to Debtor:** Wyoming allows garnishment of other debts (e.g., if a third party owes the debtor money, you can garnish that obligation). Also, if the debtor is receiving rent from tenants, you might garnish that rent through a non-continuing garnishment on the rent payment.
  - **Discovery of Assets:** If you don’t know what assets/income the debtor has, Wyoming law provides for a **Judgment Debtor Examination** (a type of post-judgment discovery). You can apply to the Circuit Court for an order requiring the debtor to come to court and **answer under oath about their assets and income** (sometimes called an “Order in Aid of Execution” or a “judgment debtor exam”). The court can compel the debtor to produce documents (bank statements, pay stubs, etc.) and testify about financial affairs. If the debtor fails to appear to such an exam, the court can issue a bench warrant for contempt. This exam helps you identify where to direct garnishments or levies. In practice, some courts require you to attempt simpler collection first or send a questionnaire to the debtor before ordering an exam. But Wyoming statutes (W.S. 1-15-301 through 1-15-306) allow creditors to seek an oral examination of the debtor in aid of execution ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=rendered%20by%20a%20circuit%20court,00%29%20fee)). 
    - To initiate, you’d file a Motion or request for an Order for Examination of Judgment Debtor, and the court will set a date and issue an order for the debtor to appear. That order must be served on the debtor (personal service) like a subpoena. At the exam, you (or your attorney) can question the debtor about assets. It’s like a deposition in court, usually before a judge or commissioner. If the debtor doesn’t show, they could be held in contempt.
  
**Garnishment Process:** Wyoming has detailed statutes for garnishment (W.S. 1-15-401 to 1-15-511). In summary:
  - You (the **creditor** or “garnishor”) file with the court a **Request/Application for Writ of Garnishment** (there are separate sets for *continuing* vs. *non-continuing* garnishments – continuing is for wages over time ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=To%20garnish%20wages%20or%20income%2C,court%20multiple%20responses%20and%2For%20payments)), non-continuing is for a one-time grab like bank accounts ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=To%20seize%20money%20from%20a,the%20service%20of%20the%20Garnishment))). Use the Self-Help form SC-01 in the Garnishment packet (there’s “Garnishment Forms” available – e.g., *Request for Writ of Continuing Garnishment* ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=03%20Order%20for%20Service%20Continuing,20)) and *Request for Writ of Non-Continuing Garnishment* ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=Name%20%20,28))).
  - The court will issue a **Writ of Garnishment** (Form SC-02 in garnishment packet) directed to the **Garnishee** (the third party holding debtor’s assets – e.g., the employer or bank) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=)). This writ commands the garnishee to seize/withhold the debtor’s funds.
  - You must properly **serve the Writ** on the garnishee (sheriff or process server, or in some cases certified mail to a bank’s registered agent). For a **wage garnishment**, you also must deliver a **Notice of Garnishment and Exemptions** to the debtor ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=Download%20%2005%20Notice%20of,Download)) so they are informed and can object if something is exempt. The self-help forms include *Notice of Right to Hearing and Claim of Exemption* ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=05%20Notice%20of%20Right%20to,34)) that you must serve on the debtor and garnishee with the writ.
  - The garnishee (e.g. employer or bank) is required to file an **Answer to the Writ** (there are Answer forms: SC-06 for continuing, SC-06 for non-continuing) stating what money they have that belongs to the debtor ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=03%20Order%20for%20Service%20Continuing,20)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=01%20Request%20for%20Writ%20of,Download)). For a bank, they will list the account and amount frozen (up to the judgment amount). For an employer, they’ll confirm the debtor’s employment and pay cycle and start withholding per law.
  - **Limits:** A wage garnishment can take up to **25% of disposable earnings** or the amount by which disposable earnings exceed 30× federal minimum wage, whichever is less ([wyoleg.gov](https://wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/2023%20Wyoming%20Statutes%2F2023%20Titles%2F1%2F29%2F34#:~:text=%28i%29%C2%A0%C2%A0Twenty,earnings%20for%20that%20week%3B%20or)) ([wyoleg.gov](https://wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/2023%20Wyoming%20Statutes%2F2023%20Titles%2F1%2F29%2F34#:~:text=,505%28b%29%28iii)). That’s the standard federal limit (Wyoming follows federal law on this). So about 25% of after-tax paycheck is the max. Certain types of income are exempt from garnishment: Social Security, VA benefits, unemployment, etc. Wyoming law also exempts any earnings of the head of a household making below 30× federal minimum wage per week (which is essentially the same calculation) ([wyoleg.gov](https://wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/2023%20Wyoming%20Statutes%2F2023%20Titles%2F1%2F29%2F34#:~:text=%28i%29%C2%A0%C2%A0Twenty,earnings%20for%20that%20week%3B%20or)). The *Notice of Right to Claim Exemption* form (SC-05) informs the debtor of common exemptions ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=Download%20%2005%20Notice%20of,Download)).
  - If the debtor or garnishee believes the garnishment is improper or the funds are exempt, they can file an **Objection** and request a hearing. For instance, if a bank account contains Social Security funds (which are 100% federally exempt), the debtor can object and prove those funds are from Social Security, then the court will release them. If an employer incorrectly tries to garnish more than allowed, the debtor can object, etc. The forms include an *Objection to Garnishment* and the court will set a hearing if filed ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Court,be%20released%20to%20the%20Creditor)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=personal%20service%2C%20or%20by%20Certified,a%20decision%20regarding%20the%20objection)).
  - If no objections, the garnishee will either send the money to the court or creditor as directed by the writ. For a bank, they usually remit the non-exempt amount on deposit at the time of service (effectively a one-time freeze). For an employer, they will remit 25% of each paycheck for 90 days (the law says continuing garnishments run for not more than 90 days at a time ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Continuing%20Garnishment,court%20multiple%20responses%20and%2For%20payments)); if the debt isn’t fully paid in that span, you can file to continue with another writ after the first expires). Many creditors simply refile sequentially until paid.
  - You should credit any collected amounts against the judgment and accrued interest. 
  - Note: Wyoming law (W.S. 1-15-408) limits that only one garnishment can run at a time per debtor per pay period (so if the debtor already has a garnishment in place from another creditor, you either wait your turn or share pro rata depending on timing – but typically it’s first-come, first-served, except child support has priority).
  
**Execution (Levy) on Property:** If you know the debtor has non-exempt property, you can ask the court for a Writ of Execution. This authorizes the sheriff to seize certain property and sell it. The process:
  - File a **Request for Writ of Execution** with the Circuit Court (there isn’t a form in the small claims packet specifically labeled, but you can draft a motion or the clerks might have a generic execution form).
  - The court issues a **Writ of Execution** directing the sheriff to satisfy the judgment from the debtor’s property. 
  - You coordinate with the sheriff on what property to target. You may have to identify the property (e.g. “2020 John Deere Tractor, serial #… located at Debtor’s barn” or “black 2015 Ford F-150 truck, WY plate 123ABC”). The sheriff will typically go levy (take legal possession) of the item. The sheriff will then arrange a public sale (notice of sale published etc.). The proceeds are used to pay the judgment (and sheriff’s fees), any surplus goes back to debtor. If sale yields less than judgment, you can repeat on other assets or the remaining balance stays owed. 
  - **Exemptions:** Wyoming exempts quite a bit of personal property (wearing apparel, up to $2,000 in tools of trade, some furniture, etc.) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,not%20exceeding%20in%20value%20two)). The debtor can claim these exemptions to stop seizure of those items. For a vehicle, exemption is $2,400 equity ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,two%20thousand%20four%20hundred%20dollars)); for tools of trade $2,000 ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,not%20exceeding%20in%20value%20two)); for furniture, etc., it might fall under general property exemption (Wyoming’s personal property exemptions total $2,000 for miscellaneous items beyond specific categories) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,not%20exceeding%20in%20value%20two)). Homestead (house equity) exempt up to $100,000 ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Clerk,force%20a%20sale%20of%20the)). Also certain benefits (social security, etc.) are exempt by federal law even after deposited (Treasury rules protect two months of direct-deposited benefits automatically at bank). The debtor must affirmatively claim state exemptions if a levy happens (the sheriff or notice will list rights). At a sheriff’s sale, often bidders are few and things sell cheap, so weigh costs – sometimes the threat of execution can prompt payment without actually seizing.
  - **Till Tap / Keeper**: In other states, creditors can sometimes have a sheriff do a “till tap” (take cash from a business’s cash register) or place a keeper in a debtor’s business to collect proceeds. Wyoming doesn’t have a specific provision for keepers, but a writ of execution could be used to collect cash on hand at a business. Rare in small claims, but theoretically if the debtor runs a retail shop and won’t pay, a sheriff could show up and take the day’s cash receipts by execution. This is dramatic and usually a last resort.

**Renewing Judgments:** If time goes on and you haven’t collected in full, remember to **renew** the judgment before 5 years passes. Wyoming calls it “revivor of judgment.” You must bring an action (or motion) to revive a dormant judgment within 10 years of dormancy ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-5/section-1-16-503/#:~:text=,to%20bring%20the%20action%20was)). Practically, file a motion for revivor in Circuit Court a bit before the 5-year mark if unpaid (the clerk can often help with the simple procedure, which may involve an order to show cause to the debtor why it shouldn’t be revived). Once revived, it’s treated as new for another 5-year period, and you can re-record liens.

**Judgment Debtor’s Options:** The debtor might:
  - Pay in full (ideally).
  - Negotiate a **payment plan** with you. Courts encourage this sometimes. If you agree to a plan, put it in writing. Some courts can incorporate it into the judgment (e.g. judgment says no execution if they pay $100/month). If the debtor fails the plan, you can then enforce.
  - File for **bankruptcy**. A judgment can be discharged in Chapter 7 bankruptcy if it’s an unsecured debt (unless it’s based on certain wrongful conduct like willful injury or fraud, which might be non-dischargeable). If the debtor declares bankruptcy, an automatic stay halts your collection immediately. You’d then have to pursue it in bankruptcy court or file a claim there. Often small claims judgments get wiped out in consumer bankruptcies. Keep an eye out: if you get a bankruptcy notice, stop all collection and consult an attorney. Wyoming small claims judgment creditors do not get special priority (except if it’s for something like wages or support).
  - Claim **exemptions** on specific collection attempts (as discussed). For example, if you garnished their bank and it contained exempt funds, they can claim that and you won’t get those funds.

**Post-Judgment Interest Accounting:** Remember, your judgment accrues interest at 10% per year ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)), simple interest (not compounded). That means roughly 0.0274% per day. You are allowed to add this to what you collect. For a long-outstanding judgment, it can be significant. When the debtor pays partially, interest stops accruing on that portion but continues on the balance. You can use an online calculator to track judgment interest. Also, any **reasonable costs of collection** (like sheriff’s fees for service, execution fees, etc.) can typically be added to the judgment as recoverable costs – Wyoming statutes allow taxation of costs for execution/garnishment to the debtor ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=,transcript%2C%20the%20filing%2C%20recording%20and)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=the%20entry%20on%20the%20docket,rendered%20in%20the%20district%20court)). For instance, the $5 abstract fee, the sheriff’s service fee, etc., can be added. Often, the way to do that is to file a **cost bill** or include them in the garnishment application for the current debt total. Keep receipts and document any such out-of-pocket expenses.

**Priority of Liens/Garnishments:** If the debtor has multiple judgments or debts, Wyoming law sets priority often by first come, first served. A recorded judgment lien takes priority from its recording time as to real estate ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=the%20entry%20on%20the%20docket,rendered%20in%20the%20district%20court)). Multiple wage garnishments – only one runs at a time; subsequent ones queue up ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Sometimes%2C%20the%20hardest%20part%20is,Seizing%20personal%20property%20or%20assets)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=include%3A%20Filing%20a%20lien%20against,Seizing%20personal%20property%20or%20assets)). Child support always has top priority over other garnishments (federal law). Tax liens (IRS, state) can trump others depending on timing and nature. If the debtor owes many, you might have to jockey for position or coordinate. 

**Satisfaction of Judgment:** If the debtor pays in full, the creditor **must file a Satisfaction of Judgment** with the court acknowledging the judgment is paid. This is important to clear the debtor’s record and any liens. The court may provide a form (often a simple one-paragraph statement that judgment is satisfied, signed by creditor). Also provide a copy to the debtor. The Wyoming Judicial Branch strongly encourages creditors to inform the court when paid ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Sometimes%2C%20the%20hardest%20part%20is,Seizing%20personal%20property%20or%20assets)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=When%20you%20win%20a%20Judgment,pay%20you%20after%20you%20win)). If you had recorded an abstract on property, you should also file a release of lien (often the recorded satisfaction or a separate release form with the county clerk). Ethically and legally, once you’re paid, you must promptly file satisfaction so the debtor isn’t harmed by a lingering lien or credit report item.

**Credit Reporting:** Circuit Court judgments in Wyoming will likely appear on the debtor’s credit report as public records. Once satisfied, the record should show it’s satisfied. You can provide the debtor proof of satisfaction so they can update credit files. Wyoming does not have a statutory penalty for not filing satisfaction timely, but failing to do so could expose you to a claim by the debtor or at least a motion in court.

**If Debtor Skips Town or Has No Assets:** Sometimes you hit a wall – the debtor might have no job, no property in their name, or move out-of-state. An out-of-state move means your Wyoming judgment is not automatically enforceable there – you’d have to **domesticate** it in the new state (use that state’s process to recognize the Wyoming judgment, typically by filing an authenticated copy in that state’s court under the Uniform Enforcement of Foreign Judgments Act, which Wyoming and most states have). Once domesticated, you can use that state’s collection mechanisms. This can be worthwhile if the debtor moved to, say, Colorado and has a job there – you could domesticate in Colorado and garnish their wages there. If the debtor has truly nothing (is “judgment-proof”), you may have to wait. Judgments last a long time (renewable up to potentially 15+ years) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-5/section-1-16-503/#:~:text=,to%20bring%20the%20action%20was)), so the debtor’s situation might improve (they might inherit money, get a good job, etc.). It’s wise to periodically check on the debtor’s status (employment, property records, etc.). A **renewed driver’s license** or car registration can give a current address. Sometimes, simply being patient and renewing the judgment is a strategy – interest accrues, and one day they might need to clear the judgment to buy a house or car.

**Exempt Assets in Wyoming:** To manage expectations, know what you **cannot seize**:
  - Homestead (primary residence equity up to $100,000) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Clerk,force%20a%20sale%20of%20the)).
  - One vehicle equity up to $2,400 ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,two%20thousand%20four%20hundred%20dollars)).
  - Tools of trade up to $2,000 ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,not%20exceeding%20in%20value%20two)).
  - Necessary clothing and personal items (reasonably).
  - Retirement accounts (ERISA-qualified pensions, 401ks are generally exempt from creditors).
  - Social Security, SSI, VA benefits (exempt by federal law, even after deposit, though commingling funds can complicate).
  - Unemployment and worker’s comp benefits.
  - Child support payments.
  - **Important:** Wyoming does **not** have a broad “head of family” wage exemption beyond the federal 25% rule (except as covered by the disposable earnings formula) ([wyoleg.gov](https://wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/2023%20Wyoming%20Statutes%2F2023%20Titles%2F1%2F29%2F34#:~:text=%28i%29%C2%A0%C2%A0Twenty,earnings%20for%20that%20week%3B%20or)). So wages can be garnished unless this would drop the person below poverty line (the 30× minimum wage rule handles that).
  
If the debtor’s only income is Social Security or similar, and they have no non-exempt assets, you realistically won’t collect now. In that case, docket the judgment and perhaps recheck later; you might ultimately be unable to recover (sometimes judgments go unpaid indefinitely if the debtor remains insolvent).

**Assistance:** If you’re having trouble with post-judgment process, you might consult the Circuit Court clerk for procedural help (they often have local practices, e.g., some provide a “Judgment Debtor Worksheet” for the debtor to fill out voluntarily before ordering an exam). The clerk can’t give legal advice, but can give forms and explain steps. If it’s complex (e.g., a contested exemption hearing or a need to pursue assets in another state), consider hiring an attorney for that limited purpose, as it may expedite collection or ensure you don’t miss a legal step.

**Keep the Pressure On:** The Wyoming Judicial Branch candidly notes that *“sometimes, the hardest part is getting your money”* ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Sometimes%2C%20the%20hardest%20part%20is,Seizing%20personal%20property%20or%20assets)) and that the creditor will have to take steps like liens, garnishments, etc., to collect ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=winning%20party%2C%20the%20Creditor%2C%20will,Seizing%20personal%20property%20or%20assets)). Don’t get discouraged – many debtors pay once they see you are serious and know their paycheck or property is on the line. Use the legal tools methodically. Often a wage garnishment is the most effective because it draws money directly from their income continuously.

In sum, as a judgment creditor in Wyoming:
  - **Record an abstract** to lien real property ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=,transcript%2C%20the%20filing%2C%20recording%20and)).
  - **Garnish wages** if available (25% of take-home) ([wyoleg.gov](https://wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/2023%20Wyoming%20Statutes%2F2023%20Titles%2F1%2F29%2F34#:~:text=%28i%29%C2%A0%C2%A0Twenty,earnings%20for%20that%20week%3B%20or)).
  - **Garnish bank accounts** for any lump sums ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=To%20seize%20money%20from%20a,the%20service%20of%20the%20Garnishment)).
  - **Levy property** if needed (vehicles, etc.), mindful of exemptions.
  - **Use debtor’s exam** if you need information on assets ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=rendered%20by%20a%20circuit%20court,00%29%20fee)).
  - Add **10% interest** per year ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)) and **costs** to what’s owed as you incur them.
  - **Renew** the judgment before dormancy at 5 years ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2022/title-1/chapter-17/article-3/section-1-17-307/#:~:text=If%20execution%20on%20a%20judgment,estate%20of%20the%20judgment%20debtor)) if not fully collected.
  - **File satisfaction** when it’s paid ([www.wyoleg.gov](https://www.wyoleg.gov/NXT/gateway.dll/Statutes%2F2021%20Titles%2F2%2F54%2F66#:~:text=,to%20any%20other%20relief%20available)).

By following through with these steps, you greatly increase your chance of actually getting the money you won in small claims court.

*(Authority: Wyo. Stat. § 5-9-138 (abstract of judgment and lien) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=rendered%20by%20a%20circuit%20court,00%29%20fee)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=,transcript%2C%20the%20filing%2C%20recording%20and)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-5/chapter-9/article-1/section-5-9-138/#:~:text=the%20entry%20on%20the%20docket,rendered%20in%20the%20district%20court)); Wyo. Stat. § 1-21-128 (Circuit judgments can be filed in district and become liens – incorporated in § 5-9-138); Wyo. Stat. § 1-17-301 et seq.
