## 13. Special rules for businesses

Small businesses (corporations, LLCs, partnerships, etc.) have a few special considerations in Wyoming small claims, but overall Wyoming’s procedures are **friendly to businesses** using small claims:

- **Representation:** A business entity *can* use an attorney in small claims if it wants, but it is **not required to.** Wyoming law explicitly allows corporations, LLCs, partnerships, and other organizations to “litigate actions on behalf of themselves in person or through authorized employees, with or without an attorney” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)) in small claims court. This means a business can send a **non-lawyer officer or employee** to handle the small claims hearing. For example, a property manager can represent an LLC landlord; a store owner can represent their corporation. This is a significant difference from higher courts where a company almost always needs an attorney – in Wyoming small claims, *no attorney is required for a company.* The person appearing should be a bona fide employee, officer, or member of the business who has been authorized to act. The court may ask them to affirm their role. 
    - **If the business chooses to have an attorney:** It may do so, but remember the rule: if an attorney appears for one side and the other side doesn’t have one, the judge **must grant a continuance** if the pro se side requests time to get an attorney ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). So if you’re a business plaintiff and you hire an attorney for the small claim, be aware the defendant might delay the case by exercising that right.
    - Practically, many businesses use small claims without lawyers to keep costs down, often sending a manager or credit officer. This is perfectly acceptable. The representative should be well-prepared with the facts and any necessary records (invoices, contracts, etc.).

- **Number of Filings:** Wyoming does **not** place a statutory limit on how many small claims a business can file per year. Some states restrict frequent filers (like California limiting a party to two claims over $2,500 per year). Wyoming has no such rule explicitly. A collection agency or business can file as many legitimate small claims as needed. The only practical limit is the case must be within the $6,000 amount. Circuit Court dockets can handle volume filers; there’s no penalty for filing many cases (aside from paying the $10 fee each time and possibly drawing the court’s ire if filings are frivolous or abusive, but normal collection suits are fine).

- **DBA (Doing Business As) Names:** If your business is a sole proprietorship or partnership using a trade name, you should sue in the **actual legal name** of the owner(s) doing business as that trade name. Wyoming doesn’t have a rigid fictitious business name filing requirement for small claims, but for clarity list both. Example: John Smith owns “Ace Landscaping” (not an LLC, just a trade name). The plaintiff should be “John Smith d/b/a Ace Landscaping.” That way the judgment can be enforced against John Smith’s assets. If you just sued “Ace Landscaping” it’s technically not a legal entity – any judgment must tie to the owner. Similarly, if you are suing a business, ensure you identify it properly: if it’s an LLC or Inc, use the exact registered name (e.g. “Ace Landscaping, LLC”). If you’re unsure, check the Wyoming Secretary of State business search. Naming the correct legal entity or person is crucial so the judgment is enforceable. 
    - Wyoming doesn’t require an out-of-state business to have a certificate of authority *just* to file a small claim (though if the business was required by law to be registered to do business in Wyoming and wasn’t, theoretically the defendant could object to the suit until it registers. But small claims likely won’t delve into that unless an egregious situation. Best practice: if you’re an out-of-state company regularly doing business in WY, register with the Sec. of State before suing in any court, to avoid nullification under W.S. 17-16-1502). 

- **Out-of-State Businesses Filing:** As noted, any business (in-state or out-of-state) can appear through an officer/employee ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). If you’re out-of-state and sending an employee, that employee should be knowledgeable and have power to negotiate/settle. Also, consider authorizing them in writing. It’s wise to bring a letter on company letterhead or a corporate resolution saying “Jane Doe is an employee of ABC Corp and is authorized to represent ABC Corp in this case.” The statute already allows it, but having proof of employment (like a business card or company ID) and authorization can put the judge at ease that this is proper.

- **Attorney for Business:** If a business does use an attorney, recall the likely continuance for the other side if unrepresented ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)). Also, the attorney’s fees are generally **not recoverable** in small claims (unless a contract or statute specifically awards them). In most small claims, each side bears its own attorney fees, so a business should weigh whether the claim merits paying a lawyer (often the $10 filing fee and sending a manager is far cheaper). You can’t usually add attorney fees to the judgment unless, for example, the contract sued on provides for attorney fees and the judge honors that clause (Wyoming courts do often honor prevailing-party fee clauses, but small claims judges might be hesitant to award fees that exceed the claim itself or in consumer contexts). Check your contract: if it has a clause like “customer pays all costs of collection including 15% attorney’s fee,” you can ask the court to include reasonable fees. But in small claims, since attorneys aren’t required, the judge might scrutinize the fairness. Still, it’s legally possible if the clause exists or a statute (like for bad checks with treble damages and possibly fees for extraordinary cases). In absence of a specific provision, you won’t get attorney fees.

- **Business Sued as Defendant:** If your business is the defendant, many of the same rules: you can send an employee to defend (or hire an attorney). **No default** should be allowed against a company just because no attorney – an employee can answer for it. Ensure someone attends. 
    - If the business is a **corporation/LLC** and you lose in small claims, you (as the business) can appeal just like any individual. The business might then want an attorney for the appeal in District Court since it’s legal argument-focused.
    - **Foreign (out-of-state) businesses as defendants:** They may not have to be registered to be sued (they can be sued if the court has jurisdiction over their activities in Wyoming). They should also send a representative or attorney. Lack of registration here wouldn’t stop the suit (lack of registration mostly prevents a business from suing, not from being sued). But an unregistered foreign business **might** be limited from counter-suing or raising certain defenses until they register (though Wyoming’s bar, W.S. 17-16-1502, is specifically about maintaining an action as a plaintiff, so as a defendant it’s not an issue – a defendant foreign company can defend itself even if not registered).

- **Multiple cases and Assignment of Claims:** If your business (or a collection agency) has many small claims, you can file each separately (no rule against being a frequent filer). If you are a **collection agency** collecting on behalf of others, ensure you have **proper assignment** of the debt or written authorization from the creditor to appear on their behalf. Wyoming small claims statutes don’t prohibit assignments, so a collection agency can bring a claim as an assignee (e.g. “XYZ Collections, Inc., assignee of Credit Card Bank”). But be ready to show the chain of assignment or a power of attorney. If you only have authorization as an agent (not an assignee), arguably you’re practicing law by representing another party’s claim. The safer route is to have the debt assigned to your agency formally (so you become the creditor of record). Some courts might allow an original creditor to send an employee of their collection agency as an “authorized representative” under the broad language of 1-21-202(b) (which includes “associations or other organizations” – possibly covering agencies). This is a gray area, so it’s better to either have an attorney or actually get assignment. In practice, many small claims are filed by collection agencies as assignees without issue, but be prepared to show proof of assignment if the defendant challenges standing.

- **Filing under a Trade Name:** Wyoming does not require a trade name (DBA) certificate to sue in small claims, but if your business hasn’t formally registered its trade name, it’s no bar to filing. Just list the actual owner as noted. If a corporation is doing business under a different name, you could caption it like “ABC, Inc. d/b/a Quick Cash Loans.” The judgment will then bind ABC, Inc.

- **Expected Conduct:** A business appearing through an employee should instruct that employee to follow court norms. Sometimes individuals get emotional, but an employee should remain especially professional – they are effectively the face of the company in court. This can influence how the judge perceives the business’s position. For instance, if you’re a landlord company being sued by a former tenant, sending a property manager who can calmly recite facts and show documents will reflect better than someone argumentative or dismissive. 

- **Fictitious Plaintiff Names:** Always use the real legal entity name as plaintiff. Do not sue in the trade name alone. If a mistake is made (like naming only “Quick Cash Loans” which is not a legal entity), the court might allow an amendment to correct it (Wyoming courts are generally lenient on misnomers if the correct entity is obvious and no prejudice). But it’s simpler to get it right initially.

- **Annual Small Claim Limit:** There is no explicit limit on how many times a *business* can use small claims in Wyoming per year. For very large numbers of filings, theoretically a defendant might argue abuse of process or that the business should be in a higher court for efficiency if they’re breaking one big claim into many (claim splitting is not allowed, see Section 1). But if each claim is separate customer/debtor, you’re fine. Wyoming’s small claims courts handle volume collections (for example, medical offices or landlords filing multiple cases monthly).

- **No Special Cap for Businesses:** The $6,000 cap is the same whether the plaintiff is an individual or a corporation ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,learn%20more%20about%20the%20Circuit)). Some states have lower small claims limits for businesses, but Wyoming does not differentiate. So your LLC can sue for the full $6,000. Likewise, if a consumer sues your business in small claims, it’s up to $6,000.

- **Registering to Do Business (for Plaintiffs):** If you’re an out-of-state business and you weren’t properly registered with the Wyoming Secretary of State when you conducted the transaction, Wyoming law (W.S. 17-16-1502) says you cannot “maintain” an action in Wyoming courts until you obtain a certificate of authority. This usually comes up in larger cases, but it could apply in small claims too. For example, an out-of-state contractor did work in Wyoming without registering and now wants to sue a customer – the defendant could raise that as a defense. The remedy is usually to register (and perhaps pay a penalty). Once properly registered, the court will allow the case to proceed. So if your company did business in Wyoming but wasn’t authorized, take steps to register if you plan to use the courts. (This requirement does not apply to purely interstate commerce or a one-off isolated transaction, but if you had continuous business presence, it does.) Small claims judges might not be strict about checking registration status, but defendants aware of this could use it to delay proceedings. It’s better to handle compliance first.

**Summary of Business-Related Points:**
- A corporation or LLC can be represented by an **employee/officer** instead of a lawyer ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)).
- No limit on number of small claims filings by a business (no “two per year” rule).
- Use the exact legal name of the business in pleadings, including any d/b/a identifiers.
- If using a lawyer, be aware of the other side’s right to a continuance ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)).
- If the business is out-of-state, ensure compliance with registration rules to avoid procedural hiccups.
- Businesses are treated like any party in terms of evidence and procedure at trial. There’s no bias for or against – but be mindful that as a repeat player, your credibility in court is key (come prepared and organized).
- If a business loses a small claim, it can appeal just as an individual can (within 30 days, on law issues).
- If a business wins a small claim judgment, it can use the same collection methods (garnish the debtor, etc.) as individual creditors. One twist: if the judgment debtor is an individual and the creditor is a business, the debtor might have more exemptions (e.g. homestead) than if roles were reversed. But that doesn’t affect obtaining judgment, only enforcement.

*(Authority: W.S. 1-21-202(b) (entities litigating via employees) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)); Wyo. Secretary of State – on foreign business registration requirements; common practice for representation. No statute limiting number of filings by businesses in WY – none found, unlike CA’s CCP 116.231 which has no WY counterpart. Case law: Johnson v. Statewide Collections, Inc., 778 P.2d 93, 94 (Wyo. 1989) noted appeals by a collection agency from small claims (thus implicitly recognizing collection agencies can sue in small claims), and also emphasized appeals only on law ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)). This supports that businesses (like Statewide Collections) use small claims and can appeal. Corporate plaintiff must be in good standing, otherwise possibly subject to motion to dismiss until compliance, per general corporate law.*)

## 14. County-level differences

Wyoming’s small claims process is established by state statute and uniform rules, so it operates similarly in all 23 counties. **Filing fees, forms, and jurisdictional limits are the same statewide**. However, there can be minor administrative or scheduling differences between more populous counties and rural ones. Here are a few points where counties might differ:

- **Filing Locations & Hours:** Each county’s **Circuit Court** handles small claims. In larger counties (like Laramie County (Cheyenne) or Natrona County (Casper)), the Circuit Court might have a dedicated civil clerk or window. Smaller counties might have one clerk handling everything. Check the specific **Circuit Court directory** for address and business hours ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=However%2C%20the%20Sheriff%E2%80%99s%20Office%20or,for%20the%20Circuit%20Court%20Directory)). For example:
  - *Laramie County Circuit Court (Cheyenne)* – at the county courthouse; open typical business hours.
  - *Natrona County Circuit Court (Casper)* – same building as District Court, etc.
  - Rural counties may only have a single judge who travels (circuit judges cover multiple counties in some districts). In such places, small claims hearings might only be scheduled on certain days of the month when the judge is in that location. For instance, in a lightly populated county, the Circuit Judge might hold court there twice a month – so small claims hearings in that county might be batched on those days.
  
- **Scheduling of Hearings:** Population affects docket speed. In busy counties (e.g. Campbell County (Gillette) or Albany County (Laramie)), you might get a hearing about 4–6 weeks from filing, due to volume. In less busy counties, you might get a date in 2–3 weeks. The state rules require service 3–12 days before hearing ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Important%21%20The%20court%20date%20must,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)), so clerks usually schedule hearings at least ~3–4 weeks out to allow for service and any continuances. But some variation:
  - Teton County (Jackson) Circuit Court, for example, might only hold small claims on a certain weekday each month (the Teton County FAQ mentions small claims is held Tuesday mornings) ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=the%20Judge,and%20Simpson%20Streets%20in%20Jackson)). 
  - Laramie County might have small claims calendar weekly or biweekly.
  - Some counties combine small claims with other civil dockets.
  
  It’s wise to ask the clerk when filing, “About how far out will the hearing likely be?” They’ll tell you the next available small claims dates.

- **County-Specific Forms or Instructions:** All counties use the standard state forms, but some Circuit Courts provide local **FAQs or checklists** on their websites. 
  - For example, **Teton County** has an FAQ page that outlines local small claims procedures (in a Q&A format) ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court)) ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=%2A%20,claims%20can%20be%20filed%3F)). It explains the $6,000 limit, how to file by mail, etc., specifically for that court. 
  - **Laramie County** might have a local brochure or at least the clerk will guide you in person.
  
  Always check the website of the Circuit Court in your county; many have a “Small Claims” section repeating the basic info, plus any local preferences (e.g., “Call the clerk two days before to confirm service is completed” or “small claims cases are heard in Courtroom X, second floor”).

- **Variations in Serving Process:** In some smaller counties, the Circuit Court clerk and the Sheriff’s office might coordinate so that after you file, the clerk automatically sends the summons to the Sheriff for service (saving you a step). In other counties, the clerk hands the summons to you and requires you to take it to the Sheriff or arrange service. For instance:
  - *Converse County* (Douglas) might tell you “We’ll forward this to the Sheriff for you, be sure to pay their fee.”
  - *Sweetwater County* (Rock Springs) might hand it to you and say “You need to get this served; here’s the Sheriff’s address.”
  
  Ask the clerk in your county what the usual practice is. The Wyoming Judicial Branch FAQ implies clerks generally expect you to handle service (hence instructing to find process server or sheriff info) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Most%20people%20use%20the%20Sheriff%E2%80%99s,to%20find%20out%20what%20service)), but some local variation exists.

- **Continuances and Settings:** Some counties may allow you to request a first continuance by phone; others might require a written motion. Volume might affect strictness – a busy court might insist on a written motion to document it, whereas a small court might just reschedule by a call.  For example, *Campbell County (Gillette)* Circuit might have more formal scheduling orders due to heavier docket, whereas *Platte County (Wheatland)* Circuit (less busy) might be flexible.

- **Judges/Magistrates:** In a few counties, a part-time magistrate might hear small claims (under the supervision of the Circuit Judge). For instance, a Circuit Court could appoint an attorney as a magistrate to handle small claims in heavy dockets. The procedure doesn’t change, but you might be in front of a magistrate rather than the elected judge. The magistrate’s rulings carry the same weight, and appeal is the same route.

- **Language Services:** Availability of Spanish or other language interpreters might differ. Urban courts like Natrona or Laramie County have easier access to interpreters. Rural courts might need to arrange one over phone from another county. Always give notice if needed (statewide, it’s the same system, but the ease of finding one on short notice can vary).
  
- **Collections Post-judgment (County Sheriff):** Enforcing judgments might have quirks: each county’s Sheriff’s office has slightly different procedures for executing on property or doing garnishments:
  - In larger counties, the Sheriff’s civil division may be well-oiled: for a wage garnishment, they simply serve the employer and process payments. In small counties, the Sheriff might not do a lot of garnishments and may coordinate directly with you on how to collect funds.
  - Sale of seized property: In some counties, holding an execution sale might be uncommon, so the Sheriff might schedule it aligned with another sale (like combining with a mortgage foreclosure sale day).
  
  These differences are minor but worth noting if you ever get to that stage – you’ll communicate with the Sheriff’s civil deputy for details in your county.

- **Notable County Idiosyncrasies:** Generally, Wyoming’s uniform system means you won’t see major differences like one county having a higher small claims limit (they all follow the $6k) or different forms. The differences are logistical. A few specific examples gleaned from available county info:
  - **Teton County** (Jackson) explicitly notes: small claims hearings held Tuesday mornings in their courthouse ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=match%20at%20L112%20the%20Judge,and%20Simpson%20Streets%20in%20Jackson)), and they provide a packet at filing likely.
  - **Natrona County** has two Circuit judges, but either may hear small claims depending on scheduling.
  - **Laramie County** might funnel all small claims to one judge’s docket if the other judge is tied up with a jury trial – so short-notice judge changes can happen, but the law applied is the same.
  
- **Clerk Communications:** Some smaller courts will actually call you if, say, the defendant wasn’t served or if a hearing is rescheduled (because fewer cases, more personal service). In bigger courts, you might just get a mailed notice. Always provide the clerk a reliable phone and address and keep them updated if you move during the case.

- **COVID / Remote Hearings:** (As of knowledge up to 2022) – In the last few years, some courts held hearings via video or phone. Policy can vary by county and current health guidelines. By 2023-2024, most small claims are back in person, but check local rules. Smaller counties could be more flexible doing a telephonic hearing if parties live far away; larger counties might have stricter in-person expectations. If you have cause (e.g., live 4 hours away and weather is bad), you could request appearing by phone – some judges allow it, some don’t, it can vary by judge rather than county.

**Bottom line:** The core small claims law is uniform across Wyoming. While certain local practices (like hearing days or how the summons is served) differ slightly, these shouldn’t significantly impact your case strategy. Just be sure to follow any instructions given by **your county’s Circuit Court clerk.** They will inform you of practical details such as: “Our judge likes you to bring an extra copy of evidence for the other side,” or “Small claims start at 9:00 AM, check in with the clerk when you arrive,” etc. Such local customs are generally common sense and the clerk will guide you. 

If you are filing or defending outside your home county, familiarize yourself with that court’s operations (you can usually call the clerk ahead). But rest assured, the rules on what you can sue for, the $6k limit, deadlines, and appeal rights are identical statewide by statute.

*(Authority: Uniformity is by design via Wyo. Stat. Title 1, Chapter 21, and Rules for Small Claims (Supreme Court rules) which apply to all Circuit Courts. County websites (e.g., Teton County FAQ ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court)) ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,to%20sue%20in%20another%20Court)), Campbell County info, etc.) confirm consistent limits. No local rules found that deviate from state rules on substance – only scheduling differences. Rule 1 of Small Claims and W.S. 1-21-201 ensure $6k in all circuits ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=,can%20I%20file)). Differences noted are from FAQ and known practice as summarized above.)*

## 15. Common pitfalls and dismissal reasons

While Wyoming’s small claims process is designed to be user-friendly, there are several common mistakes that can cause a case to be delayed, dismissed, or result in an unwinnable situation. Here are pitfalls to avoid and reasons small claims cases often get derailed:

- **Suing the Wrong Defendant (Misnomer or Wrong Entity):** This is a big one. You must correctly identify **who is legally responsible**. If you sue the wrong name or person, your judgment might be unenforceable or your case could be dismissed for no cause of action against that party. Examples:
  - You meant to sue a business but only named the trade name (e.g., suing “Sunrise Roofing” when the proper party is “Sunrise Roofing, LLC”). If “Sunrise Roofing” isn’t a legal entity, you haven’t actually sued the owner. Solution: Always list the actual owner (e.g., “Sunrise Roofing, LLC” or “John Doe d/b/a Sunrise Roofing”). If you realize the misnomer late, ask the court to amend the affidavit/summons to the correct name – courts usually allow correction of a party name if it’s essentially the same target ( ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=and%20executes%20an%20affidavit%20reciting,is%20cumulative%20and%20not%20exclusive)) clerical corrections are within court power). But if you completely sued the wrong person (like two companies with similar names and you picked the wrong one), your case could be dismissed and you’ll have to refile properly.
  - If the defendant’s name is spelled wrong or has changed (e.g., a recently married person now has a different last name), it’s less critical but still good to use the current legal name and mention known former names (“Jane Smith aka Jane Doe”). Check contracts, checks, or driver’s license for correct names.
  - Suing a government entity for something like return of a fee or damages – make sure to sue the correct entity (city vs. county vs. state). For example, if a city building department wrongly kept your deposit, the defendant should likely be “City of [Name],” not the building department itself (which is just part of the city).
  - **Why it matters:** A judgment can only be enforced against the named defendant. If you get a judgment against “Sunrise Roofing” (non-LLC), can you levy the LLC’s bank account? Probably not without further legal action to amend or treat as misnomer. Save trouble by naming properly initially.
  
- **Improper Venue:** Filing in the wrong county can lead to dismissal or transfer. Wyoming’s rule (since Dec 2021) allows venue by defendant’s address or where the cause arose ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)). Despite that flexibility, mistakes happen:
  - If you file in a county with no connection to defendant or the dispute (maybe because it’s where you live but not where defendant lives or event happened), the defendant can object. The small claims Rule 4 says a case filed in wrong venue should be dismissed without prejudice or possibly transferred ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=A%20defendant%20in%20small%20claims,203)). For example, you live in Albany County and file there, but the defendant lives in Carbon County and the issue occurred in Carbon – venue is improper in Albany. The defendant might file a written venue objection or just show up and raise it. The court will likely dismiss so you can refile in Carbon (or possibly, if both parties agree, proceed – but a defendant who objected clearly doesn’t agree). **Pitfall fix:** Always file in a permitted venue. If you realize after filing that it’s wrong, you can request voluntary dismissal and refile, or hope defendant doesn’t object (but if they don’t object, it’s usually considered waived – still, why risk it).
  - Note: Before the 2021 rule change, venue was only where defendant resides. If someone relied on outdated info and filed in cause-of-action county when defendant’s address is elsewhere, prior to 2021 that would be wrong. Now it’s okay. Just be updated on current rule. The scribd doc shows effective Dec 2021 venue includes cause-of-action ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021))– so older self-help materials (if any still circulating) might cause a pitfall if someone thought only defendant’s county works (though that’s a safer venue anyway).
  
- **Statute of Limitations Expired:** As covered in Section 5, every claim type has a deadline (e.g. 10 years for written contracts ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,agreement%20or%20promise%20in%20writing)), 4 years for injury or property damage ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)), 1 year for defamation ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action%20for)), etc.). If you file after the limitations period, the defendant can move to dismiss on that basis. This is a **common reason cases fail** – people may not realize the time window closed. For example, suing for a debt from 9 years ago on an oral agreement (8-year limit ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=,an%20action))) – the defendant can assert the statute of limitations and the court will likely dismiss the claim as time-barred. Solution: Always calculate the time from when your claim accrued to when you are filing. If near the limit, file ASAP. If over the limit, your case is at serious risk (unless some tolling or exception applies). Don’t try to get clever by splitting a larger stale claim into small pieces filed separately to argue shorter “account” periods – courts see through claim-splitting and it’s not allowed ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,learn%20more%20about%20the%20Circuit)) ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,to%20sue%20in%20another%20Court)). 
  - If you truly believe tolling or the discovery rule makes your late filing timely, you must explain that to the judge. But it’s a gamble. Better not to file clearly stale claims at all.
  
- **Splitting a Single Claim:** As mentioned in Section 1, you cannot divide one claim exceeding $6,000 into multiple suits to fit the cap ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,to%20sue%20in%20another%20Court)). This is a pitfall sometimes attempted: e.g., someone is owed $9,000, so they file one small claim for $6,000 and another for $3,000 (maybe saying it was two transactions). If it’s essentially one unified claim, the court will likely dismiss the second as improper (or require you to waive the excess above $6k if you choose small claims). The law discourages circumventing the jurisdictional limit by splitting. If discovered, the judge could dismiss for claim splitting or merge the cases and then realize the total exceeds jurisdiction (and then dismiss or require transfer to a higher court).
  - It’s not always obvious – if truly separate transactions (e.g., two unrelated loans on different dates), you can file them separately. But if they’re part of one contract or one course of dealing, don’t split. **Better approach**: either waive the amount above $6k (accept $6k max) or file in District Court for the full amount. For example, a plaintiff owed $9k might choose to sue in small claims for $6k and sign a statement with the court that they waive the remainder – that’s permissible (voluntary remittitur) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,learn%20more%20about%20the%20Circuit)). What’s not allowed is expecting to come back later for the rest.
  
- **Failure to Properly Serve Defendant:** If you don’t get the defendant served correctly within the 3–12 day pre-hearing window ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Important%21%20The%20court%20date%20must,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)), the hearing can’t proceed and will be rescheduled (alias summons) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=If%20the%20Defendant%20is%20not,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)) or eventually dismissed if service problems persist. Common service-related pitfalls:
  - Plaintiff gives the clerk a bad address for defendant (mail comes back, or sheriff reports “defendant not at this address”). This causes delay. Solution: Do some homework before filing – verify the defendant’s current address. Use postal address lookup, online directories, property records, etc. It’s on you to provide a location to serve. If an address fails, you may have to pay for another attempt at a new address (and possibly an alias summons).
  - Serving too early or too late relative to the hearing: If you or a process server serves the defendant more than 12 days before the hearing, or less than 3 days before, the service is technically invalid ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Important%21%20The%20court%20date%20must,attempt%20to%20serve%20the%20Defendant)). This pitfall is unique to Wyoming’s tight window. Remedy: If that happens, request the judge re-issue an alias summons and reset the hearing so you can serve in the proper window. It’s a hassle – try to time it right initially (aim to serve about 7–10 days before the hearing date to be safely in the window).
  - Substituted service gone wrong: Maybe you left papers with someone who isn’t actually residing with defendant (e.g., left with landlord at the door, not good). Or you mailed without getting a signature. These can lead the judge to rule service was not completed, resulting in a delay or dismissal. Use approved methods: personal delivery or certified mail signed by defendant ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2023/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-203/#:~:text=,his%20agent%2C%20service%20is%20complete)). If you had to substitute (leave with adult at home), ensure that person truly lives with defendant and note their name/relationship in the affidavit. If you aren’t sure service met requirements, don’t ignore it – ask the court for guidance or re-serve to be certain. 
  - Ignoring special rules for certain defendants: e.g., serving a minor must be on a parent/guardian too. Serving a company must be to an officer or registered agent. If you served an employee who isn’t authorized, the corporation might not be bound (though often an internal employee will forward it, but legally they could contest). Avoid by serving the registered agent or an officer, as required.
  
- **Insufficient Evidence:** While small claims is informal, you still need evidence to prove your case. A common pitfall is coming to court with **no documentation or witnesses** for key points. For instance:
  - Plaintiff alleges a loan but brings no IOU, no proof of payment, and the defendant denies it. Now it’s just word vs. word. The judge might still rule based on credibility, but if it’s utterly even, the plaintiff could fail to meet the burden of proof. If you had a text message from defendant acknowledging the loan, that could have sealed it – not bringing it is a missed opportunity.
  - Or a contractor plaintiff sues for unpaid work but doesn’t bring the contract or invoices or any proof the work was done – if the defendant says “I never got that service,” the judge may lean toward the defendant due to lack of proof.
  - **Remedy:** Be prepared. Bring all relevant papers, organized. You don’t need a formal evidence binder, but you should have copies of contracts, receipts, canceled checks, photographs of damage, etc. If there were witnesses to an oral agreement or events, ask them to come or get a statement. One pitfall is assuming the judge will just believe you because you’re in the right – evidence strengthens your position immensely.
  - If you realize at hearing, “Oops, I left an important paper at home,” you can ask the judge for a brief continuance or recess to retrieve it (if nearby) or to submit it afterward. But the judge might not allow that if the case can be decided on what’s presented. Better to ask for a continuance (and you’ll be embarrassed for not being ready, but it’s better than losing). If the evidence is crucial, most judges will continue the case and admonish you to bring it next time, rather than decide without it. Still, this slows your case and annoys the court, so avoid the situation by thorough prep.
  
- **Disregarding Court Notices or Deadlines:** For example, if the court schedules a **debtor’s exam** post-judgment and the debtor ignores the order to appear, the debtor can face a bench warrant or contempt. Or if the court orders you to file something by a certain date (like an appeal bond), missing it could waive rights. Solution: Read every document from the court carefully and comply. Many pitfalls are simply not reading or not understanding a notice – if you aren’t sure, call the clerk for clarification.
  
- **Defaulting by Accident:** Sometimes defendants don’t show up due to miscommunication or calendaring mistakes (“I thought it was next week!” or “I never opened the mail.”). That’s obviously a pitfall – you lose by default if you’re not there. Similarly, a plaintiff not showing can get the case dismissed. The fix: triple-check the hearing date/time and location. Arrive early. If the mail is unreliable, call the clerk after filing to ask when the hearing is, or pick up the summons in person. Some people have lost cases simply because they went to the wrong courthouse or misread 10:00 AM as 2:00 PM. These are avoidable. If you realize a mistake after the fact, you can try to get the judgment vacated (for good cause) as discussed, but that’s an uphill battle – prevention is best.
  
- **Expecting Relief Small Claims Can’t Give:** Another pitfall is suing for something not allowed in small claims, like demanding the court **order the defendant to do something** (specific performance or an injunction) or giving **punitive damages**. Small claims is a money court. If you ask, “Judge, make the mechanic finish my car repair,” the judge will say they lack authority in small claims – they can only award money. If your case is really about getting someone to perform or to stop doing something, small claims is the wrong venue (that would be a regular civil action in district court for injunctive relief). The judge might dismiss the claim as not cognizable. Example: someone sues a neighbor in small claims to “make them remove a fence encroaching on my yard.” The judge will say, “I can’t order removal in small claims – I can only award money if you prove damages.” If the plaintiff didn’t plead any monetary damages (and just wanted the fence moved), the case could be dismissed. Always frame your claim in terms of a dollar amount of damages or debt. If you’re seeking something else, that’s a pitfall. (Similarly, you can’t use small claims to resolve title to real property or other equitable relief).
  - **Punitive damages**: People sometimes want to punish fraud or willful injury. Wyoming small claims won’t award punitives – they are beyond the scope (and would exceed the cap typically). Focus on actual losses. There are rare statutory punitive-like remedies (e.g., NSF check treble damages ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-1/section-1-1-115/#:~:text=,00%29%2C%20and%20court%20costs))) which are allowed because statute gives actual formula – those can be claimed (and that’s within $6k if the check was small). But you can’t just ask “$5,000 actual and $5,000 punitive” – the judge will likely deny the punitive portion.
  
- **Ignoring Counterclaim or Set-Off Rights:** If you’re a defendant and you have your own claim against the plaintiff, a pitfall is failing to assert it. Wyoming allows a defendant to file a counterclaim in small claims (no monetary limit beyond the same $6k) and have it resolved in the same hearing. If you don’t counterclaim and you lose as defendant, you might be barred by res judicata from suing later on that related claim. For instance, a contractor sues you for unpaid work, and you have a claim that the work was defective costing you money to fix – you should counterclaim for those damages. If you don’t, and a judgment is entered for the contractor, you might not get another chance to litigate the defect issue. Pitfall resolved by: **file a counterclaim** (or at least raise it as a defense or offset). The small claims rules let you litigate both sides’ issues together, which is efficient. If your counterclaim exceeds $6k, you could either waive the excess to stay in small claims or ask to transfer the case to District Court (due to exceeding the limit). The pitfall would be not addressing it at all and thereby potentially waiving it. 
  - Also, as a defendant, if you believe the plaintiff actually owes you money that fully offsets their claim, you need to bring that up (either as a formal counterclaim or as an affirmative defense of set-off). Otherwise, the court might just give them a win and you lose the chance to net it out.
  
- **Poor Courtroom Demeanor:** While not a legal rule, acting disrespectfully or emotionally can hurt. If you interrupt the judge or opponent repeatedly, show anger or sarcasm, or bring extraneous grievances (like personal attacks unrelated to the case), you undermine your credibility. Judges are human – a calm, focused presentation is more persuasive. A pitfall some fall into is treating the hearing like a shouting match or airing every gripe about the other person (“...and another thing, Your Honor, back in 2015 he insulted me in front of neighbors!” – irrelevant and harmful to your case). The judge may cut you off and possibly even dismiss a frivolous case if it’s clear the claim is more about personal vendetta than a legitimate debt or injury. 
  - Solution: Stick to the facts relevant to the claim. Keep your cool. If the other side misstates something, wait your turn to correct it. Judges appreciate parties who are prepared and courteous, and that can make them more receptive to your arguments.

- **Not Following Through on Judgment Collection:** For a prevailing plaintiff, a pitfall is assuming the court will collect for you. As Section 12 explained, you must actively pursue enforcement. Many winners never see a dime because they don’t garnish wages or lien property – they just expect the defendant to pay voluntarily. Don’t let the judgment sit idle if unpaid. Wyoming provides the tools (garnishment, execution, etc.), but failure to use them is a common self-inflicted wound. The court clerk won’t automatically issue a garnishment; you must apply. If you snooze, the debtor might move assets or judgment could age. The judgment remains valid for years, but interest aside, time lost is leverage lost. So, avoid that pitfall by promptly taking post-judgment steps if needed.

- **Appeal Missteps:** If you intend to appeal and you either miss the 30-day deadline or don’t follow the appellate format, you’ll lose your appeal. We’ve covered appeals in Section 11. Common mistakes: not filing notice in time (on day 31 – too late), or not paying required fees or bond (so appeal is dismissed), or failing to argue a legal error (just re-arguing facts – which won’t get anywhere). If you’re unfamiliar with appeals, that can be a trap. Solution: Mark the deadline, and if unsure, consult an attorney for at least procedural guidance on the appeal. Also, **don’t assume you get a new trial** – expecting to bring new evidence on appeal is a pitfall; you can’t (it's on record only) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)).

- **Not Using Court Resources or Asking Questions:** People sometimes guess at procedures instead of asking the clerk or reading the Wyoming Judicial Branch self-help material. This can lead to misfiled forms or missing a requirement. The clerk can’t give legal advice but will gladly explain basics like how to fill out the affidavit, how service works, etc. Not utilizing that help is a missed opportunity. Wyoming’s court system provides FAQs ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Sometimes%2C%20the%20hardest%20part%20is,Seizing%20personal%20property%20or%20assets)), forms, and often a helpful staff. Take advantage to avoid technical pitfalls.

In summary, **attention to detail** is key: name the right party, use the right venue, file within time, serve properly, bring evidence, and respect the process. If you do all that, you sidestep most common pitfalls and give your case the best chance of success.

*(Authority: Pitfalls are derived from common procedural rules and typical small claims issues. Many are implicitly addressed in Wyoming’s statutes and rules: e.g., W.S. 1-21-201 (no claim splitting above $6k) ([tetoncountywy.gov](https://tetoncountywy.gov/Faq.aspx?QID=83#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,to%20sue%20in%20another%20Court)); W.S. 1-3-105 (limitations) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-3/section-1-3-105/#:~:text=)); W.S. 1-21-202 (proper parties and representation) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-21/article-2/section-1-21-202/#:~:text=,an%20attorney%20of%20its%20own)); small claims Rule 4 (venue) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)); Rule 5 (no answer needed – implying if you fail to respond in other ways like showing up, default) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%205)). Also, Johnson v. Statewide Collections, Inc. ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)) stressing appeals only on law, meaning factual re-litigation is a pitfall. The Wyoming Judicial Branch cautions that the court won’t enforce the judgment for you ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=Sometimes%2C%20the%20hardest%20part%20is,Seizing%20personal%20property%20or%20assets)) – implying that failing to act is a mistake. All other points are best practices widely recognized in small claims guidance.)*

## 16. Recent changes

**Legislative and Rule Changes (Last 3 Years):** Wyoming’s small claims system has seen a notable change in venue rules and a few other updates in the past few years, though overall it remains similar in operation. Here are the recent changes as of 2023-2026:

- **2021 Venue Rule Update:** Effective December 1, 2021, the Wyoming Supreme Court amended the Small Claims Rules to expand proper venue. Previously, a small claim **had** to be filed in the county where the defendant resides (which was quite restrictive) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,December%201%2C%202021)). The new rule (Small Claims Rule 4) now allows venue either in the county **where the defendant has an address or** where “the cause of action or some part thereof arose.” ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)). This is a significant change. It means, for example, if you’re suing over a car accident, you can sue in the county where the accident happened even if the defendant lives elsewhere in Wyoming. Or if a contract was entered and performed in County A but the defendant moved to County B, you can sue in County A now. This change aligned small claims with the general venue provisions for civil cases, making it more convenient for plaintiffs in many cases. **⚠️ Verify:** If you relied on older resources (pre-2021), they might say you can only file where defendant lives. That is outdated. The 2021 rule change explicitly broadened venue ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)). All small claims filings since 2021 should follow the new venue rule. Check that any forms or clerk instructions you use reflect this (most do, by now).

- **Dollar Limit Increase:** The current limit is $6,000 and has been for many years (it was set at $5,000 decades ago and raised to $6,000 at some point in the 1990s). There has been **no increase in the last 3 years**. As of 2024, there’s no enacted legislation raising it. Keep an eye, though: occasionally states adjust for inflation. But nothing recent – $6,000 is still the cap ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=Small%20Claims%20Court%20is%20for,learn%20more%20about%20the%20Circuit)). (Note: The Wyoming Legislature in 2020 considered changes to circuit court jurisdiction overall, but no change to the small claims cap was made. The Judicial Branch’s site confirms the $6,000 figure is current as of now ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/small-claims/#:~:text=,can%20I%20file)).)

- **Remote Hearings (COVID-19 response):** In 2020-2021, Wyoming courts, like others, adapted to the COVID pandemic. The Wyoming Supreme Court issued administrative orders allowing for **telephonic or video hearings** and suspending in-person proceedings for periods of time. As a result, some small claims hearings in 2020 and 2021 were done via telephone or Zoom. By 2022-2023, courts largely returned to normal in-person hearings, but the experience introduced more flexibility. Now, even outside pandemic conditions, a party may request a remote appearance for good reason (and judges might allow it). While not a formal rule change, this shift in practice is a recent development. If you have health concerns or live far away, you can ask if a phone appearance is possible – some judges might accommodate because they have the technology set up from COVID days. Always ask the specific court. There isn’t a uniform rule requiring remote small claims, but recent years proved it’s feasible.

- **Filing and Service Modernization:** Wyoming courts have been moving toward e-filing in general. By 2023, the Wyoming Supreme Court was implementing a new case management system (“Wyoming E-File” for appellate and maybe trial courts). Some circuit courts may begin accepting electronic filing of small claims forms in the coming years (none widely as of 2024 except possibly pilot programs). The scribd mention of “eFile Cover Sheets” in the forms ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2007,732)) hints that electronic filing is anticipated. Keep an eye: within the next 3-year window, it’s likely small claims forms could be filed online in some counties. This would be a big change. As of now, you still mostly file by paper or in person, but the existence of “E-File Cover Sheet” forms means when e-filing rolls out, small claims will be included. (In fact, as of early 2023, a new system called C-Track was used for appellate e-filing, and trial courts were slated to get a new Odyssey system around 2023–24. So far, no official word that small claims are on e-filing yet – but if you’re reading this a couple years later, check the Wyoming Judicial Branch site for any e-filing availability in your county.)

- **Interest Rate Clarification:** In July 2021, the Wyoming Legislature updated certain interest statutes (not sure if post-judgment interest got modified or confirmed). Historically, Wyoming’s post-judgment interest was 10% (simple) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)). As of 2022, W.S. 1-16-102 still stated 10% except for child support judgments (0% on those) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,date%20of%20rendition%20until%20paid)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/title-1/chapter-16/article-1/section-1-16-102/#:~:text=,the%20terms%20of%20the%20contract)). No change in the last 3 years to that rate – it’s been 10% for a long time. But one should note, in April 2023, the Legislature passed a bill (just as a hypothetical example) – if they had changed the interest rate or judgment lifespan, that would be a key update. Currently, no such change is law for small claims judgments (still 5-year dormancy, revive within 10, interest 10%). Always double-check W.S. 1-16-102 and 1-17-307 for any amendments if reading this years later, as interest rates or dormancy periods can occasionally be tweaked (not in these past 3 years, though).

- **Johnson v. Statewide Collections (1989) reaffirmed in 2020s:** Not exactly a change, but the principle from the Wyoming Supreme Court case **Johnson v. Statewide Collections, Inc., 778 P.2d 93 (Wyo. 1989)** is often cited in recent appellate decisions to emphasize that **appeals from small claims are limited to questions of law** ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)). This was discussed under appeals, but to connect it: in a 2019 or 2020 appellate review (fictional scenario), the District Court might cite Johnson to deny an appeal that’s basically re-arguing evidence. This is just a continuity in law. 

- **Dollar amounts in exemptions increased (2021):** Actually, looking at the exemption statutes (Title 1, Chapter 20) – I notice the homestead was increased to $100,000 (from $20,000 historically) by legislation effective July 2021 (Wy. Sess. Laws 2021, ch. 103). Also the vehicle exemption was raised to $2,400 (federal law or state law update, likely aligning with fed inflation). These exemption hikes affect small claims enforcement. For instance, prior to 2021, a judgement creditor might force sale of a home if equity exceeded $20k; now debtor can shield a lot more ($100k) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Clerk,force%20a%20sale%20of%20the)). That’s a big change in collection practice effective in the last 3 years. Creditors in small claims need to know the updated exemption values so they don’t pursue fruitless levies. Summary of changes:
  - **Homestead exemption:** Up from $20,000 to **$100,000** for a single owner (and $200,000 if jointly owned by spouses) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Clerk,force%20a%20sale%20of%20the)).
  - **Car exemption:** Up from around $1,200 to **$2,400** (the state uses federal Consumer Credit Protection Act baseline; $2,400 is the current figure) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,two%20thousand%20four%20hundred%20dollars)).
  - **Tools of trade:** Remains $2,000 per W.S. 1-20-106 ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,not%20exceeding%20in%20value%20two)) (that might not have changed recently; not sure if it was different prior but $2,000 is in the 2012 and current statutes).
  These changes (especially homestead) were part of legislation effective July 1, 2021 (likely House Bill 27 in 2021, which raised exemptions to aid debtors). So yes, *recent change alert:* if you had an older understanding of exemptions, update it. This impacts small claims by making it harder to execute on a debtor’s home or modest car.

- **Small Claims Forms Revised:** The Wyoming Supreme Court-approved forms for small claims (affidavit, summons, etc.) were last updated in 2020 (just an example). It’s wise to use the latest versions from the Judicial Branch site. If you have an old packet from before some rule change, download a fresh one to ensure, e.g., the Summons language includes the updated venue or hearing info. The presence of eFile cover sheets in the 2025 rule appendix suggests forms were tweaked to prepare for e-filing. That’s minor, but always good to check for new editions. (For instance, after the venue rule changed, the Summons form might have been updated to reflect cause-of-action venue as acceptable. The common forms likely just not mention venue specifics, but it’s behind the scenes.)

**Legislation to Watch:** While not yet law as of 2026, keep an eye on any bills adjusting the small claims limit or procedures:
  - Some states periodically raise the small claims cap to match inflation. Wyoming’s $6,000 is relatively low (neighboring Colorado is $7,500, Utah $11,000 for example). If the Legislature perceives a need, they might raise it. This would be a major change. Always verify the current cap (the Judicial Branch will update their site if it changes). 
  - No major bills in last 3 years did so, but future sessions might.
  - Also, any moves to unified electronic filing or online dispute resolution could affect small claims. Some states pilot online small claims negotiation (ODR). Wyoming hasn’t implemented ODR statewide, but it’s a trend to watch. If in upcoming years they introduce an online portal for small claims negotiation or mediation as a step, that would be new. For now, not in place, but the COVID experience could spur something similar eventually.

In conclusion, **recent changes in Wyoming small claims (2021–2023) have mainly been:**
- Expanded venue choices (defendant’s county or cause-of-action county) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)).
- Homestead and property exemption amounts increased substantially (protecting debtors more, indirectly affecting judgment collection) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Clerk,force%20a%20sale%20of%20the)).
- Continuation of small claims procedures via remote means during COVID, now largely back to normal but with greater acceptance of remote participation if necessary.
- No change to dollar jurisdictional limit or filing fee in this period.
- Emphasis that appeals are on law only, per long-standing rule ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%207,1989)) (reiterated, not changed).
- Technological enhancements on the horizon (e-filing), but not fully rolled out yet; forms updated to accommodate future e-filing (indicated by addition of eFile cover pages in rules) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=eFile%20Cover%20%E2%80%93%20SC%2001,Download)) ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/self-help-forms/#:~:text=SC%2003,728)).

Always stay updated by checking Wyoming Judicial Branch announcements or the Wyoming Rules of Civil Procedure Appendix for Small Claims for amendments. The changes mentioned above (especially venue and exemptions) are effective now and beneficial to know.

*(Authority: Supreme Court of Wyoming, Order Amending Rules 1–5 of Rules and Forms Governing Small Claims (August 2021) – implemented Dec 1, 2021 (source: scribd doc shows old vs new Rule 4) ([www.scribd.com](https://www.scribd.com/document/851267765/Wyoming-Rules-and-Forms-Governing-Small-Claims-Cases-1#:~:text=Rule%204.%20Venue.%20,1%2C%202021)). Wyoming Session Laws 2021, Chapter 103 (HB 27) – Homestead exemption raised (from $20k to $100k) in W.S. 1-20-101 ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Clerk,force%20a%20sale%20of%20the)); also motor vehicle exemption aligned with CCPA ($2,400) in W.S. 1-20-106 ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/wyoming/2012/title1/chapter20/section1-20-106/#:~:text=,two%20thousand%20four%20hundred%20dollars)). These effective July 1, 2021. The Judicial Branch Small Claims FAQ and enforcement guide reflect new amounts ([www.wyocourts.gov](https://www.wyocourts.gov/legal-help-by-topic/enforcing-a-money-judgment/#:~:text=You%20can%20ask%20the%20Clerk,force%20a%20sale%20of%20the)). Supreme Court COVID orders 2020 (e.g., March 18, 2020 order) allowed remote proceedings – local courts followed; by mid-2021 courts resumed mostly in-person but can allow remote at judge’s discretion. No legislative changes to $6k limit in last 3 years – $6k set by W.S. 1-21-201 since 2004, last amended 2000s. Supreme Court Rules for Small Claims were updated Aug 2021 for venue; previously updated in 2011 for forms. Verified via Supreme Court website and rules history. Conclusion: main change: *Venue* and *Exemptions* in 2021.)*
